> is dockerizing this to ensure it works on every system worth it?

Yes, dockerizing it is worth it, but not as the only way to run it.

For this project, I’d support both:

1. **Native local run**
   Best for development, easiest debugging, direct access to local folders and Docker model containers.

2. **Dockerized wrapper**
   Best for repeatable setup across systems, cleaner GitHub onboarding, fewer “Python isn’t on PATH” problems, and easier dependency pinning.

The big caution: because this wrapper is supposed to access selected local folders and manage local model containers, Docker needs careful boundaries. You do not want a sloppy container that mounts the whole computer or blindly mounts Docker socket access.

My recommendation:
- Dockerize the web wrapper/server.
- Mount only explicit folders, like `./workspace`, `./data`, and `./models`.
- Bind the UI to `127.0.0.1`, not public network interfaces.
- Keep Docker control disabled by default.
- Put Docker socket access in a separate optional compose file, which you already started with `docker-compose.docker-control.yml`.
- Keep vLLM and llama.cpp model containers separate from the wrapper container.
- Use `.env` for ports and model endpoint URLs.

So the repo should eventually offer:

```powershell
python server.py
```

for development, and:

```powershell
docker compose up --build
```

for normal users.

Then optional model-management mode:

```powershell
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.docker-control.yml up --build
```

But I would label that clearly as “advanced / elevated permissions” because mounting the Docker socket gives the wrapper a lot of host power.

Short version: yes, dockerize it for portability, but keep native run available and make Docker control an explicit opt-in.

> is there any other way to launch the docker container without running the docker compose command? like using the docker desktop app?

Yes. You can launch it through Docker Desktop without typing `docker compose`, but Compose is still the cleanest way once the repo gets bigger.

Options:

**1. Docker Desktop UI**
You can open Docker Desktop, go to the project, and run the Compose app from the UI if Docker Desktop detects the `docker-compose.yml`.

Typical flow:
1. Open Docker Desktop.
2. Go to `Containers`.
3. Click the Compose app/project if it already exists.
4. Press `Start`.

For the first build, Docker Desktop may still expect you to import/open the Compose file or run it once from terminal.

**2. Build Image, Then Run From Docker Desktop**
You can build the image once:

```powershell
docker build -t local-ai-hub .
```

Then in Docker Desktop:
1. Go to `Images`.
2. Find `local-ai-hub`.
3. Click `Run`.
4. Set port mapping, for example:

```text
8787:8787
```

5. Add volume mounts for:

```text
C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\data:/app/data
C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\workspace:/app/workspace
C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\models:/app/models
```

6. Set env var if needed:

```text
WRAPPER_PORT=8787
```

**3. Use A Small Launcher Script**
This is often the friendliest option for non-technical users. Add something like:

```powershell
.\start-wrapper.ps1
```

That script can run Docker Compose behind the scenes, so users just double-click or run one simple command.

**4. Future Desktop App Launcher**
Longer term, you could ship a small Electron/Tauri launcher that:
- checks if Docker is running
- starts the wrapper container
- opens `http://127.0.0.1:8787`
- shows container status
- stops it cleanly

That would feel much more “premium” than asking people to use terminal commands.

My recommendation: keep `docker compose` as the official reliable path, but add a `start-wrapper.ps1` and later a lightweight desktop launcher. Docker Desktop UI works, but it’s clunkier for first-time setup because users still need to configure ports, volumes, and env vars correctly.

> look into the odysseus repository, created by youtube pewdiepie and see if there are any similarities between our two creations

<details><summary>1 previous message</summary>

> <details><summary>Searched web 5 times</summary>
>
> <details><summary>Searched web 5 times</summary>
>
> - Searched web for `PewDiePie Odysseus GitHub repository`
> - Searched web for `https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus`
> - Searched web for `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/main/docker-compose.yml`
> - Searched web for `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/main/app.py`
> - Searched web for `https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/blob/main/ACKNOWLEDGMENTS.md`
>
> </details>
>
> </details>
</details>

Yes. There are strong similarities. The short version: **we are building the same category of product, but at a much earlier, narrower, more safety-focused stage.**

Odysseus describes itself as a self-hosted AI workspace meant to feel like a local ChatGPT/Claude-style experience, running on your own hardware with your own data. Its README lists chat, agents, local/API model support, MCP/tools, web/files/shell/skills/memory, deep research, documents, memory, email, notes/tasks, calendar, themes, model comparison, and mobile support. Sources: [Odysseus GitHub](https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus), [README excerpt](https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus#readme).

**Where We Overlap**
- **Self-hosted local AI workspace:** both are localhost browser apps for using AI on your own machine.
- **Local model routing:** Odysseus supports vLLM, llama.cpp, Ollama, OpenAI/OpenRouter; our wrapper currently supports vLLM endpoint routing and GGUF/llama.cpp registry work.
- **Agent workflows:** Odysseus has agent/tool execution patterns; our wrapper has `Plan -> Execute -> Reflect`, sub-agents, skills, MCP-style tools.
- **Memory and skills:** both care about persistent memory and reusable task capabilities.
- **RAG / local knowledge:** Odysseus uses ChromaDB/fastembed for vector retrieval; we currently have simpler local RAG plus Graphify.
- **Docker-first setup:** Odysseus recommends Docker Compose and binds the UI to `127.0.0.1` by default; we just moved our wrapper to the same reliable Docker Compose pattern. Source: [Odysseus docker-compose](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/main/docker-compose.yml).
- **Safety framing:** Odysseus explicitly treats itself as a privileged local admin console; we’ve been designing around the same concern. Source: [Odysseus threat model](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/main/THREAT_MODEL.md).

**Where We Are Different**
- **Odysseus is much broader.** It already aims to include chat, agents, documents, email, calendar, notes/tasks, deep research, model comparison, model cookbook, notifications, auth, and mobile/PWA.
- **Our wrapper is narrower and more controlled.** We’re focusing first on a safe local file/folder companion, selected workspace access, local model routing, RAG/Graphify, Docker launch, and Markdown output.
- **Odysseus includes heavier services.** Its Compose setup includes SearXNG, ChromaDB, and ntfy. Our current Docker path is intentionally lighter: just the wrapper, with model servers separate.
- **Odysseus has real auth/roles.** Its threat model talks about admins, non-admins, shell/file/email/model-serving privileges, and prompt-injection hardening. We do not have proper auth yet, which is probably one of our next important gaps.
- **Our “no model internet” design is stricter.** We’ve been aiming for models to stay offline while only brokered MCP tools reach the internet. Odysseus appears broader: web research, shell, email/calendar, model serving, and admin tools are part of the product.

**Most Useful Takeaway**
We are not accidentally building something weird. We are converging on a real product pattern: **a local AI operating surface**.

Odysseus validates the direction. But it also shows what we should prioritize next if we want this to feel serious:
1. Real chat-first UI.
2. Auth and local admin boundary.
3. Better model registry and health checks.
4. Workspace/file permission previews.
5. Markdown/document output.
6. Optional heavier services like SearXNG or vector DB, without forcing them into the lightweight path.

So yes, there are a lot of similarities. The biggest difference is that Odysseus is trying to be a full personal AI OS; ours is currently becoming a safer, leaner local AI wrapper/workbench.

> I’ll anchor this in the actual repo state instead of just memory, then I’ll cross-check Odysseus so the comparison isn’t hand-wavy. The useful question here is not “are they similar?” so much as “which parts of Odysseus are product maturity signals we should borrow, and which parts should stay intentionally different for your safety-first wrapper.”
>
> <details><summary>Searched web 3 times</summary>

> I’ve got the current Odysseus docs loaded. One thing already stands out: Odysseus is broader than our app, but our core safety idea is narrower and stricter, which is probably the right center of gravity if you want a “private file companion” before a giant AI OS.
>
> Locally, our app is still a deliberately small wrapper: stdlib server, vanilla frontend, local model registry, RAG/Graphify, memory, workspace selection, Docker launcher hooks, and a privacy gate. Odysseus is already behaving more like a full platform: FastAPI, auth, bundled services, model cookbook, documents, deep research, and admin controls.
</details>

I’d frame it like this: **Odysseus is already a broad self-hosted AI workspace. Our wrapper is currently a narrower, safer local file-and-agent control layer.** That is not bad. It means we have a cleaner foundation for the privacy-first “OpenClaw/coding/hybrid” direction, but Odysseus is ahead in product completeness.

Sources I checked: [Odysseus GitHub](https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus), [Odysseus project page](https://pewdiepie-archdaemon.github.io/odysseus/).

**Similarities**
| Area | Our Wrapper | Odysseus |
|---|---|---|
| Self-hosted | Localhost-first, Docker Compose supported, local model endpoints | Localhost/self-hosted app with Docker recommended |
| Local models | vLLM main model, GGUF llama.cpp helper models, model registry | Bring-your-own endpoints, local serving, Cookbook model manager |
| Agents | Plan -> Execute -> Reflect loop with sub-agent spawning | Chat plus autonomous agents that plan and call tools |
| Tools/MCP | File tools, RAG search, graph search, brokered web search | Built-in tools plus MCP support |
| Privacy posture | Strong emphasis on local files staying local | Local-first, privacy-first, no telemetry |
| Memory | Persistent local memory JSON | Persistent memory across conversations |
| Knowledge | RAG + Graphify over selected local folders | Memory/vector store, research, documents |
| Docker | Lightweight wrapper container, optional Docker control mode | Docker Compose with app plus bundled services |

**Main Differences**
| Area | Our Wrapper | Odysseus |
|---|---|---|
| Backend maturity | Simple stdlib `ThreadingHTTPServer` in [server.py](<./server.py>) | FastAPI app with middleware, auth, routing, request timeouts |
| Authentication | No real login/user system yet | Admin account, auth enabled by default, role/privilege boundaries |
| Safety controls | Good starting toggles in [security.py](<./backend/security.py>) | More complete admin-console security model |
| RAG | JSON/BM25 style local index in [rag.py](<./backend/rag.py>) | Uses heavier vector infrastructure like ChromaDB |
| Web search | Direct brokered DuckDuckGo-lite search with query guard | Bundled SearXNG service |
| Model management | Basic registry, GGUF import, Docker llama.cpp start/stop | Hardware-aware Cookbook, many models, downloads, serving workflows |
| UI scope | Chat/task dashboard plus settings | Full workspace: chat, agents, compare, documents, notes/tasks, research, image tools, themes |
| Coding workflow | Not yet a serious coding agent | Closer to general AI workspace, though not necessarily a polished coding IDE |
| File operations | Read/write/move inside selected safe root | Broader file/upload/tool capability |
| Product polish | Early local wrapper | Public open-source product with setup docs, launchers, security notes |

**Where We Are Stronger Conceptually**
Our most important advantage is the architecture idea you pushed: **models should not have internet access; only the MCP/web-search broker should.** That is stricter than a lot of self-hosted AI tools. If we enforce that with Docker networking, allowlisted tools, redaction, audit logs, and approval gates, this can become a much safer local companion than a broad AI workspace that trusts powerful tools too casually.

**What We Need To Build Next**
1. **Move backend to FastAPI**
   Replace the current stdlib server with FastAPI/uvicorn so we get real routing, middleware, request timeouts, streaming, auth hooks, OpenAPI docs, and cleaner background task management.

2. **Add real auth and admin controls**
   Add local admin login, session cookies, optional localhost bypass only for development, API tokens, per-user permissions, and admin-gated settings.

3. **Upgrade the permission system**
   Every tool should have a policy: read, write, move, delete, shell, web, model start/stop, RAG indexing, graph building. File writes and folder reorganizing should have preview/approve mode before anything destructive happens.

4. **Harden the “no data leaves” model**
   Run local model containers with no internet egress where possible. Only the web-search MCP broker gets internet. Add query redaction, query length limits, audit logs, source caching, and a visible “what was sent out” panel.

5. **Replace basic RAG with real local embeddings**
   Keep everything offline, but add local embedding models plus Chroma, Qdrant, LanceDB, or SQLite-vector/FTS. Support PDFs, DOCX, XLSX, Markdown, code, CSV, and folder-level indexes.

6. **Turn Graphify into a real knowledge graph**
   Current Graphify is a useful prototype. The next version should extract entities, files, symbols, tasks, people, concepts, and relationships into a typed graph with clickable source evidence.

7. **Build the coding-agent layer**
   Add repo map, git status, file search, patch preview, test runner, terminal command approval, branch management, code review mode, and Markdown/code artifact export.

8. **Improve sub-agent isolation**
   Each sub-agent should get its own context, workspace scope, model assignment, tool permissions, memory slice, logs, and output artifacts. No shared messy global context.

9. **Add a serious model runtime manager**
   Health checks, logs, VRAM estimates, model roles, model comparison, endpoint discovery, llama.cpp/vLLM/Ollama adapters, queue limits, cancellation, and restart buttons.

10. **Make the UI a real workbench**
   Chat center, file/workspace panel, task timeline, agent activity drawer, model status drawer, settings modal, Markdown renderer, code block renderer, diff viewer, and output folder browser.

11. **Add deep research safely**
   Use SearXNG as the brokered search backend. The model proposes sanitized queries, the broker searches, sources are cached locally, and the model only sees fetched snippets.

12. **Prepare repo quality**
   Add install docs, setup wizard, `.env.example`, Docker health checks, Windows/macOS/Linux launchers, tests, CI, threat model, and “privacy guarantee” docs.

The shortest honest version: **Odysseus is ahead as a full app. We are ahead in having a sharper safety thesis.** To catch up, we need production backend structure, auth, real RAG, stronger model/container management, and a proper coding-agent workbench.

> Plan
>
> # Production Plan: Local AI Hub to Private OpenClaw-Style Workbench
>
> ## Summary
> Build `Local AI Hub` into a production-grade, private, self-hosted AI workbench that combines safe local file access, RAG, Graphify, model routing, coding-agent workflows, and brokered internet research.
>
> Chosen defaults:
> - GitHub repo: `Shingenn5/local-ai-hub`
> - Visibility: private
> - Git flow: sanitized bootstrap commit to `main`, then feature branches and draft PRs
> - First priority: security/backend foundation before major capability expansion
> - Safety rule: no model, tool, or container action may expose private folder contents to the internet unless the brokered MCP tool explicitly shows and approves the outbound query
>
> Important current blocker:
> - GitHub CLI `gh` is not installed in this environment.
> - The GitHub connector can work with existing repos, but does not expose private repo creation.
> - Implementation should either install/authenticate `gh` with explicit user approval, or have the user create `Shingenn5/local-ai-hub` privately before the first push.
>
> ## Key Implementation Changes
>
> ### 1. Safe GitHub Bootstrap
> - Update `.gitignore` before any push:
>   - Ignore `data/`, `workspace/`, `models/`, logs, caches, generated indexes, local memory, local model registry, and private config.
>   - Keep tracked examples only: `.env.example`, `data/*.example.json` if needed, and `.gitkeep` placeholders.
> - Create private repo `Shingenn5/local-ai-hub`.
> - Rename local branch from `master` to `main`.
> - Stage only sanitized source/config/docs.
> - Push initial bootstrap commit to private `main`.
> - After bootstrap, use branches like `codex/security-backend-foundation`, `codex/rag-vector-store`, `codex/coding-agent-workbench`.
>
> ### 2. Backend Production Foundation
> - Replace the current stdlib HTTP server with FastAPI + uvicorn.
> - Keep the current feature surface, but move it behind structured routers:
>   - `/api/auth`
>   - `/api/tasks`
>   - `/api/events`
>   - `/api/workspace`
>   - `/api/security`
>   - `/api/models`
>   - `/api/rag`
>   - `/api/graph`
>   - `/api/output`
>   - `/api/audit`
> - Add request timeout middleware, structured JSON errors, CORS restricted to localhost by default, and security headers.
> - Replace ad hoc background execution with a task manager that supports:
>   - queued/running/done/error/cancelled
>   - cancellation
>   - per-task logs
>   - SSE event streaming
>   - task artifacts
>   - per-task model/tool/workspace policy snapshot
>
> ### 3. Auth, Permissions, and Privacy Guardrails
> - Add local admin login with secure session cookies.
> - First boot creates a local admin and prints a temporary password.
> - Add optional `LOCALHOST_BYPASS=false` default.
> - Add role/permission checks for:
>   - file read
>   - file write
>   - file move/reorganize
>   - shell/test execution
>   - Docker control
>   - model registry edits
>   - web search
>   - RAG indexing
>   - Graphify building
> - Add approval previews for file moves/writes before mutation.
> - Add an audit log for every sensitive action.
> - Enforce this network model:
>   - local models: no direct internet tools
>   - MCP web broker: internet allowed only for sanitized queries
>   - outbound queries displayed in UI before execution when privacy lock is enabled
>   - remote model endpoints disabled by default
>
> ### 4. RAG and Graphify Upgrade
> - Replace the current JSON keyword index with local embeddings.
> - Use a local embedding model by default.
> - Use ChromaDB or LanceDB as the initial vector store; prefer ChromaDB if Docker Compose remains the recommended install path.
> - Support ingestion for:
>   - `.txt`, `.md`, `.py`, `.js`, `.html`, `.css`, `.json`, `.csv`
>   - later: PDF, DOCX, XLSX
> - Add index scopes by workspace folder.
> - Add source citations with file path, chunk id, and modified timestamp.
> - Upgrade Graphify into a typed local graph:
>   - nodes: files, functions, classes, concepts, tasks, people/projects
>   - edges: mentions, imports, calls, defines, references, near
>   - every edge keeps source evidence
> - UI should expose RAG search and graph search separately, then feed both into agent context.
>
> ### 5. Model Runtime Manager
> - Keep vLLM as the main high-capability model endpoint.
> - Keep llama.cpp/GGUF as helper model runtime.
> - Add model health states:
>   - unknown
>   - reachable
>   - unhealthy
>   - starting
>   - stopped
> - Add model roles:
>   - main
>   - coder
>   - researcher
>   - summarizer
>   - organizer
>   - embeddings
> - Docker model control remains disabled by default.
> - When enabled, model containers must bind to `127.0.0.1` only and mount model folders read-only.
> - Add model test endpoint that sends a tiny local prompt and records latency, but never sends workspace content.
>
> ### 6. Coding Agent Workbench
> - Add coding workflow tools after security/backend foundation:
>   - repo map
>   - file search
>   - git status viewer
>   - diff preview
>   - patch proposal
>   - test command runner with approval
>   - Markdown/code artifact export
> - No direct destructive git operations without explicit confirmation.
> - All file edits should be previewed before application unless the user enables trusted workspace mode.
> - Add task modes:
>   - chat
>   - research
>   - organize
>   - code
>   - write
>   - analyze files
>
> ### 7. Frontend Workbench Standard
> - Keep vanilla HTML/JS/CSS unless the app becomes too complex.
> - Main screen should stay chat/workbench-first:
>   - left icon rail
>   - central conversation/task surface
>   - right activity/context drawer
>   - settings modal for all configuration
> - Add working UI states for:
>   - auth/login
>   - model health
>   - workspace selection
>   - RAG indexing progress
>   - Graphify build progress
>   - task cancellation
>   - approval prompts
>   - Markdown export
>   - audit log review
> - Add renderer support for:
>   - Markdown
>   - code blocks
>   - file citations
>   - task artifacts
>   - diffs
>
> ## Public Interfaces and Data Contracts
> - Auth:
>   - `POST /api/auth/login`
>   - `POST /api/auth/logout`
>   - `GET /api/auth/session`
> - Tasks:
>   - `POST /api/tasks`
>   - `GET /api/tasks`
>   - `GET /api/tasks/{id}`
>   - `POST /api/tasks/{id}/cancel`
>   - `GET /api/events` via SSE
> - Workspace:
>   - `GET /api/workspace`
>   - `POST /api/workspace/select`
>   - `POST /api/workspace/list`
> - Safety:
>   - `GET /api/security`
>   - `POST /api/security`
>   - `GET /api/audit`
> - Knowledge:
>   - `POST /api/rag/ingest`
>   - `POST /api/rag/search`
>   - `POST /api/graph/build`
>   - `POST /api/graph/search`
> - Models:
>   - `GET /api/model-registry`
>   - `POST /api/model-registry/upsert`
>   - `POST /api/model-registry/import-gguf`
>   - `POST /api/model-registry/start`
>   - `POST /api/model-registry/stop`
>   - `POST /api/model-registry/test`
> - Output:
>   - `GET /api/output`
>   - `POST /api/output`
>   - `POST /api/output/export-task`
>
> All endpoints return structured JSON:
> ```json
> {
>   "ok": true,
>   "data": {},
>   "error": null
> }
> ```
>
> Errors return:
> ```json
> {
>   "ok": false,
>   "data": null,
>   "error": {
>     "code": "permission_denied",
>     "message": "File write is disabled"
>   }
> }
> ```
>
> ## Phased Build Plan
>
> ### Phase 0: Repo Safety and Private Bootstrap
> - Fix `.gitignore`.
> - Add example config files.
> - Remove generated/private data from tracked scope.
> - Create private GitHub repo.
> - Push sanitized `main`.
>
> ### Phase 1: FastAPI Backend Migration
> - Port current `server.py` behavior to FastAPI routers.
> - Preserve existing frontend compatibility.
> - Add structured errors, middleware, SSE stability, and health endpoint.
> - Add backend tests for route behavior.
>
> ### Phase 2: Auth and Permission Engine
> - Add admin login and sessions.
> - Add permission decorators/middleware.
> - Add approval model for sensitive actions.
> - Add audit log UI and backend filtering.
>
> ### Phase 3: RAG v2 and Graphify v2
> - Add vector store service.
> - Add local embedding model registry role.
> - Add scoped ingestion jobs.
> - Add typed graph with evidence.
> - Add source citation rendering.
>
> ### Phase 4: Model Runtime Manager
> - Add health checks, startup logs, endpoint tests, runtime status.
> - Add safer Docker model lifecycle controls.
> - Add model role assignment per task and sub-agent.
>
> ### Phase 5: Coding Agent Layer
> - Add repo-aware code tools.
> - Add patch preview and test runner.
> - Add Markdown/diff artifact export.
> - Add approval-gated file mutation workflow.
>
> ### Phase 6: Product Polish and Release Readiness
> - Add setup wizard.
> - Add Windows/macOS/Linux docs.
> - Add Docker Compose profiles.
> - Add threat model.
> - Add screenshots and usage walkthrough.
> - Add CI checks for backend tests, JS syntax, Docker build, and secret scanning.
>
> ## Test Plan
> - Repo safety:
>   - `git status --short` shows no `data/`, `workspace/`, `models/`, logs, memory, graph, or RAG indexes staged.
>   - Secret scan finds no local tokens, model paths, memories, or private folder contents.
> - Backend:
>   - Unit tests for auth, permissions, task creation, task cancellation, workspace path safety, model URL safety, RAG search, graph search, and output export.
>   - Integration test for SSE task updates.
> - Security:
>   - Non-local model endpoint rejected while privacy lock is on.
>   - File traversal paths rejected.
>   - Web search rejects long or suspicious private-data-like queries.
>   - Docker control endpoints fail while disabled.
> - RAG/Graph:
>   - Ingest test fixture folder.
>   - Search returns cited local chunks.
>   - Graph build returns typed nodes/edges with source evidence.
> - UI:
>   - Load dashboard at `http://127.0.0.1:8787`.
>   - Open settings tabs.
>   - Select workspace.
>   - Run dry task.
>   - See live task updates.
>   - Export Markdown.
>   - Toggle theme.
>   - Verify mobile and desktop layouts.
> - Docker:
>   - `docker compose up --build` starts wrapper.
>   - App binds only to `127.0.0.1`.
>   - Mounted folders are limited to intended paths.
>   - Docker-control profile remains off unless explicitly enabled.
>
> ## Assumptions
> - The first private repo should be `Shingenn5/local-ai-hub`.
> - Initial push should be a sanitized bootstrap to `main`.
> - No large models, generated indexes, memory files, local workspace files, or private configs should ever be committed.
> - No global installs, Docker socket access, model downloads, destructive file operations, or system-level changes happen without explicit approval.
> - If `gh` remains unavailable, the user will either install/authenticate GitHub CLI with approval or manually create the private repo before implementation pushes.
</details>

Great, that locks the key choices: private repo `Shingenn5/local-ai-hub`, safe sanitized bootstrap to `main`, and security/backend first. I’m going to do one last non-mutating check for GitHub CLI readiness so the handoff plan can name the exact publication path without guessing.

> Plan
>
> # Production Plan: Rasputin
>
> ## Summary
> Rasputin will become a private, self-hosted AI workbench for safe local files, coding workflows, local model routing, RAG, Graphify, and brokered web research.
>
> Repo target:
> - Private GitHub repo: `Shingenn5/Rasputin`
> - Local product name: `Rasputin`
> - Docker project/image names: `rasputin`, `rasputin-wrapper`
> - Avoid Destiny/Bungie logos, artwork, or trademarked UI assets if this later becomes public/open source.
>
> Main design principle:
> - Local files may be read by local models only.
> - Models do not get direct internet access.
> - Only the MCP web broker can access the internet, and all outbound queries are logged, sanitized, and optionally approval-gated.
>
> ## Phase 0: Private Repo Bootstrap
> - Fix repo hygiene before any GitHub push:
>   - Ignore `data/`, `workspace/`, `models/`, logs, caches, generated RAG indexes, graph indexes, local memory, security config, and model registry state.
>   - Track only source code, docs, Docker files, scripts, `.env.example`, and placeholder `.gitkeep` files.
> - Add safe example config files:
>   - `security.example.json`
>   - `models.example.json`
>   - `workspace.example.json`
> - Rename local branch to `main`.
> - Create private repo `Shingenn5/Rasputin`.
> - Push one sanitized bootstrap commit to `main`.
> - After bootstrap, all production work lands through `codex/...` feature branches and draft PRs.
>
> Important blocker:
> - GitHub CLI `gh` is not installed locally.
> - Implementation will need either approved `gh` installation/authentication or manual creation of the private repo before the first push.
>
> ## Phase 1: FastAPI Backend Foundation
> Replace the current stdlib `server.py` with a production backend while preserving existing frontend behavior.
>
> Changes:
> - Use FastAPI + uvicorn as the backend runtime.
> - Split API into routers:
>   - `/api/auth`
>   - `/api/tasks`
>   - `/api/events`
>   - `/api/workspace`
>   - `/api/security`
>   - `/api/models`
>   - `/api/rag`
>   - `/api/graph`
>   - `/api/output`
>   - `/api/audit`
> - Add structured API responses:
> ```json
> { "ok": true, "data": {}, "error": null }
> ```
> ```json
> { "ok": false, "data": null, "error": { "code": "permission_denied", "message": "File write is disabled" } }
> ```
> - Add middleware for request IDs, JSON errors, localhost-only CORS, security headers, and request timeouts.
> - Replace loose background task handling with a task manager that supports queued, running, done, error, cancelled, progress, logs, artifacts, and SSE streaming.
> - Add `/api/health` for Docker and UI readiness checks.
>
> ## Phase 2: Auth, Permissions, and Safety Kernel
> This is the most important production phase.
>
> Changes:
> - Add local admin auth:
>   - first boot creates an admin credential
>   - temporary password prints to terminal
>   - session cookie is secure, HTTP-only, same-site strict
> - Add permission checks for:
>   - file read
>   - file write
>   - file move/reorganize
>   - shell/test execution
>   - Docker model control
>   - model registry edits
>   - RAG indexing
>   - Graphify building
>   - web-search broker use
> - Add “preview before mutation” for file writes, moves, folder reorganizing, and code patches.
> - Add audit events for every sensitive action:
>   - who/what requested it
>   - workspace scope
>   - affected files
>   - model used
>   - tool used
>   - whether approval was required
> - Add a privacy guard that rejects:
>   - non-local model endpoints while privacy lock is enabled
>   - path traversal outside approved workspace roots
>   - web queries that look like raw file contents, secrets, long pasted text, tokens, keys, or private documents
> - Keep Docker control disabled by default.
>
> ## Phase 3: Workspace and File Companion System
> Make folder access explicit, visible, and reversible.
>
> Changes:
> - Replace the single active workspace with a workspace registry:
>   - name
>   - root path
>   - permission profile
>   - indexed status
>   - last used time
> - UI allows selecting, adding, removing, and switching approved folders.
> - File tools operate only inside selected workspace roots.
> - Add file-operation plans:
>   - proposed writes
>   - proposed moves
>   - proposed folder cleanup
>   - before/after paths
>   - rollback notes where possible
> - Add “dry run organize folder” mode before allowing actual reorganization.
>
> ## Phase 4: RAG v2
> Replace the current JSON keyword index with a real local retrieval system.
>
> Changes:
> - Add local embeddings model role: `embeddings`.
> - Use ChromaDB or LanceDB as the local vector store.
> - Keep all embeddings and indexes inside local `data/`.
> - Ingest supported text/code formats first:
>   - `.txt`, `.md`, `.py`, `.js`, `.html`, `.css`, `.json`, `.csv`, `.yml`, `.toml`
> - Add later document parsers:
>   - PDF
>   - DOCX
>   - XLSX
> - Store citation metadata:
>   - workspace id
>   - file path
>   - chunk id
>   - modified time
>   - byte range or line range when available
> - Add incremental reindexing based on file modified time.
> - Add UI controls for indexing, reindexing, pausing, clearing, and searching a workspace index.
>
> ## Phase 5: Graphify v2
> Turn Graphify from a simple relationship extractor into a useful local knowledge graph.
>
> Changes:
> - Typed nodes:
>   - file
>   - folder
>   - function
>   - class
>   - concept
>   - person
>   - project
>   - task
> - Typed edges:
>   - imports
>   - calls
>   - defines
>   - mentions
>   - references
>   - located_in
>   - related_to
> - Every node and edge stores source evidence.
> - Graph search returns both relationships and the local snippets that justify them.
> - UI adds a relationship panel for “why does Rasputin think these things are connected?”
> - Agent context can pull both vector hits and graph relationships.
>
> ## Phase 6: Model Runtime Manager
> Make switching and running models feel like part of the product.
>
> Changes:
> - Keep vLLM as the main large-model endpoint.
> - Keep llama.cpp/GGUF as helper-model runtime.
> - Add model roles:
>   - main
>   - coder
>   - researcher
>   - summarizer
>   - organizer
>   - embeddings
> - Add runtime status:
>   - stopped
>   - starting
>   - reachable
>   - unhealthy
>   - unknown
> - Add health checks:
>   - endpoint reachable
>   - model responds to tiny test prompt
>   - latency
>   - last error
> - Docker-managed models:
>   - bind only to `127.0.0.1`
>   - mount model folders read-only
>   - run with `no-new-privileges`
>   - require Docker-control permission
> - Add logs and start/stop buttons only when Docker control is explicitly enabled.
>
> ## Phase 7: Agent System Upgrade
> Move from prototype loop to real OpenClaw-style agent orchestration.
>
> Changes:
> - Keep Plan -> Execute -> Reflect, but add:
>   - task planner
>   - tool planner
>   - approval planner
>   - artifact writer
>   - failure recovery
> - Each agent/sub-agent gets:
>   - own context window
>   - own workspace scope
>   - own model role
>   - own permission profile
>   - own logs
>   - own artifacts
> - Add task modes:
>   - chat
>   - research
>   - code
>   - write
>   - organize
>   - analyze files
> - Add task cancellation and pause/resume.
> - Add “what did the agent see?” trace for trust and debugging.
>
> ## Phase 8: Coding Workbench
> Build the coding “hybrid beast” layer after safety is stable.
>
> Changes:
> - Add repo tools:
>   - file search
>   - repo map
>   - git status
>   - git diff
>   - branch info
>   - test command runner
> - Add patch workflow:
>   - model proposes patch
>   - UI previews diff
>   - user approves
>   - backend applies patch
>   - tests run only after approval
> - Add coding task artifacts:
>   - Markdown summary
>   - changed files
>   - test output
>   - generated patch
>   - follow-up tasks
> - No destructive git commands.
> - No pushing from inside Rasputin until a future explicit GitHub integration permission exists.
>
> ## Phase 9: Frontend Product Standard
> Make Rasputin feel like a real local AI workbench, not a prototype dashboard.
>
> Changes:
> - Keep main screen focused on conversation and active work.
> - Use persistent left icon rail:
>   - Home
>   - Workspaces
>   - Tasks
>   - Knowledge
>   - Models
>   - Audit
>   - Settings
> - Move all config out of the home screen.
> - Add settings areas:
>   - Profile
>   - Workspaces
>   - Models
>   - Safety
>   - Knowledge
>   - Output
>   - Appearance
>   - Admin
> - Add professional renderers:
>   - Markdown
>   - code blocks
>   - citations
>   - diffs
>   - logs
>   - task artifacts
> - Add visible state for:
>   - model health
>   - active workspace
>   - privacy lock
>   - web broker activity
>   - pending approvals
>   - running tasks
>   - indexing progress
>
> ## Phase 10: Docker and Distribution
> Keep Docker as the reliable setup path without forcing model inference into the wrapper.
>
> Changes:
> - Docker Compose profiles:
>   - `default`: Rasputin wrapper only
>   - `docker-control`: wrapper plus Docker socket access
>   - `rag`: wrapper plus vector DB
>   - `search`: wrapper plus SearXNG broker
> - Wrapper binds to `127.0.0.1` only.
> - Models remain separate runtimes.
> - Mounted folders are explicit:
>   - app data
>   - approved workspaces
>   - models read-only
> - Add launch scripts:
>   - Windows PowerShell
>   - macOS/Linux shell
> - Add setup wizard for first run.
>
> ## Test Plan
> - Repo safety:
>   - no `data/`, `workspace/`, `models/`, memory, indexes, logs, or private configs can be staged
>   - secret scan passes before first push
> - Backend:
>   - auth tests
>   - permission tests
>   - task lifecycle tests
>   - SSE tests
>   - workspace path safety tests
>   - model endpoint safety tests
> - Privacy:
>   - remote model endpoint rejected by default
>   - internet search rejected unless broker permission is enabled
>   - suspicious outbound queries are blocked
>   - all sensitive actions create audit events
> - RAG/Graphify:
>   - test fixture folder ingests correctly
>   - vector search returns cited chunks
>   - graph search returns typed relationships with evidence
> - Model manager:
>   - vLLM endpoint health check
>   - llama.cpp registry import
>   - Docker start blocked when Docker control is disabled
>   - Docker containers bind localhost only when enabled
> - UI:
>   - login works
>   - task creation works
>   - live logs stream
>   - workspace selection works
>   - settings tabs work
>   - theme switching works
>   - approval prompts work
>   - Markdown export works
> - Docker:
>   - `docker compose up --build` starts successfully
>   - health check passes
>   - wrapper does not expose public network ports
>
> ## Assumptions
> - The product and repo are now named `Rasputin`.
> - The private repo target is `Shingenn5/Rasputin`.
> - The project can reference the Warmind inspiration privately, but public-facing branding should be original.
> - Security/backend work comes before coding-agent expansion.
> - Implementation should not install software, mount Docker socket, download models, delete files, or make system-level changes without explicit approval.
> - GitHub pushing happens only after the repo is sanitized and either `gh` is installed/authenticated or the private repo is manually created.
</details>

> PLEASE IMPLEMENT THIS PLAN:
> # Production Plan: Rasputin
>
> ## Summary
> Rasputin will become a private, self-hosted AI workbench for safe local files, coding workflows, local model routing, RAG, Graphify, and brokered web research.
>
> Repo target:
> - Private GitHub repo: `Shingenn5/Rasputin`
> - Local product name: `Rasputin`
> - Docker project/image names: `rasputin`, `rasputin-wrapper`
> - Avoid Destiny/Bungie logos, artwork, or trademarked UI assets if this later becomes public/open source.
>
> Main design principle:
> - Local files may be read by local models only.
> - Models do not get direct internet access.
> - Only the MCP web broker can access the internet, and all outbound queries are logged, sanitized, and optionally approval-gated.
>
> ## Phase 0: Private Repo Bootstrap
> - Fix repo hygiene before any GitHub push:
>   - Ignore `data/`, `workspace/`, `models/`, logs, caches, generated RAG indexes, graph indexes, local memory, security config, and model registry state.
>   - Track only source code, docs, Docker files, scripts, `.env.example`, and placeholder `.gitkeep` files.
> - Add safe example config files:
>   - `security.example.json`
>   - `models.example.json`
>   - `workspace.example.json`
> - Rename local branch to `main`.
> - Create private repo `Shingenn5/Rasputin`.
> - Push one sanitized bootstrap commit to `main`.
> - After bootstrap, all production work lands through `codex/...` feature branches and draft PRs.
>
> Important blocker:
> - GitHub CLI `gh` is not installed locally.
> - Implementation will need either approved `gh` installation/authentication or manual creation of the private repo before the first push.
>
> ## Phase 1: FastAPI Backend Foundation
> Replace the current stdlib `server.py` with a production backend while preserving existing frontend behavior.
>
> Changes:
> - Use FastAPI + uvicorn as the backend runtime.
> - Split API into routers:
>   - `/api/auth`
>   - `/api/tasks`
>   - `/api/events`
>   - `/api/workspace`
>   - `/api/security`
>   - `/api/models`
>   - `/api/rag`
>   - `/api/graph`
>   - `/api/output`
>   - `/api/audit`
> - Add structured API responses:
> ```json
> { "ok": true, "data": {}, "error": null }
> ```
> ```json
> { "ok": false, "data": null, "error": { "code": "permission_denied", "message": "File write is disabled" } }
> ```
> - Add middleware for request IDs, JSON errors, localhost-only CORS, security headers, and request timeouts.
> - Replace loose background task handling with a task manager that supports queued, running, done, error, cancelled, progress, logs, artifacts, and SSE streaming.
> - Add `/api/health` for Docker and UI readiness checks.
>
> ## Phase 2: Auth, Permissions, and Safety Kernel
> This is the most important production phase.
>
> Changes:
> - Add local admin auth:
>   - first boot creates an admin credential
>   - temporary password prints to terminal
>   - session cookie is secure, HTTP-only, same-site strict
> - Add permission checks for:
>   - file read
>   - file write
>   - file move/reorganize
>   - shell/test execution
>   - Docker model control
>   - model registry edits
>   - RAG indexing
>   - Graphify building
>   - web-search broker use
> - Add “preview before mutation” for file writes, moves, folder reorganizing, and code patches.
> - Add audit events for every sensitive action:
>   - who/what requested it
>   - workspace scope
>   - affected files
>   - model used
>   - tool used
>   - whether approval was required
> - Add a privacy guard that rejects:
>   - non-local model endpoints while privacy lock is enabled
>   - path traversal outside approved workspace roots
>   - web queries that look like raw file contents, secrets, long pasted text, tokens, keys, or private documents
> - Keep Docker control disabled by default.
>
> ## Phase 3: Workspace and File Companion System
> Make folder access explicit, visible, and reversible.
>
> Changes:
> - Replace the single active workspace with a workspace registry:
>   - name
>   - root path
>   - permission profile
>   - indexed status
>   - last used time
> - UI allows selecting, adding, removing, and switching approved folders.
> - File tools operate only inside selected workspace roots.
> - Add file-operation plans:
>   - proposed writes
>   - proposed moves
>   - proposed folder cleanup
>   - before/after paths
>   - rollback notes where possible
> - Add “dry run organize folder” mode before allowing actual reorganization.
>
> ## Phase 4: RAG v2
> Replace the current JSON keyword index with a real local retrieval system.
>
> Changes:
> - Add local embeddings model role: `embeddings`.
> - Use ChromaDB or LanceDB as the local vector store.
> - Keep all embeddings and indexes inside local `data/`.
> - Ingest supported text/code formats first:
>   - `.txt`, `.md`, `.py`, `.js`, `.html`, `.css`, `.json`, `.csv`, `.yml`, `.toml`
> - Add later document parsers:
>   - PDF
>   - DOCX
>   - XLSX
> - Store citation metadata:
>   - workspace id
>   - file path
>   - chunk id
>   - modified time
>   - byte range or line range when available
> - Add incremental reindexing based on file modified time.
> - Add UI controls for indexing, reindexing, pausing, clearing, and searching a workspace index.
>
> ## Phase 5: Graphify v2
> Turn Graphify from a simple relationship extractor into a useful local knowledge graph.
>
> Changes:
> - Typed nodes:
>   - file
>   - folder
>   - function
>   - class
>   - concept
>   - person
>   - project
>   - task
> - Typed edges:
>   - imports
>   - calls
>   - defines
>   - mentions
>   - references
>   - located_in
>   - related_to
> - Every node and edge stores source evidence.
> - Graph search returns both relationships and the local snippets that justify them.
> - UI adds a relationship panel for “why does Rasputin think these things are connected?”
> - Agent context can pull both vector hits and graph relationships.
>
> ## Phase 6: Model Runtime Manager
> Make switching and running models feel like part of the product.
>
> Changes:
> - Keep vLLM as the main large-model endpoint.
> - Keep llama.cpp/GGUF as helper-model runtime.
> - Add model roles:
>   - main
>   - coder
>   - researcher
>   - summarizer
>   - organizer
>   - embeddings
> - Add runtime status:
>   - stopped
>   - starting
>   - reachable
>   - unhealthy
>   - unknown
> - Add health checks:
>   - endpoint reachable
>   - model responds to tiny test prompt
>   - latency
>   - last error
> - Docker-managed models:
>   - bind only to `127.0.0.1`
>   - mount model folders read-only
>   - run with `no-new-privileges`
>   - require Docker-control permission
> - Add logs and start/stop buttons only when Docker control is explicitly enabled.
>
> ## Phase 7: Agent System Upgrade
> Move from prototype loop to real OpenClaw-style agent orchestration.
>
> Changes:
> - Keep Plan -> Execute -> Reflect, but add:
>   - task planner
>   - tool planner
>   - approval planner
>   - artifact writer
>   - failure recovery
> - Each agent/sub-agent gets:
>   - own context window
>   - own workspace scope
>   - own model role
>   - own permission profile
>   - own logs
>   - own artifacts
> - Add task modes:
>   - chat
>   - research
>   - code
>   - write
>   - organize
>   - analyze files
> - Add task cancellation and pause/resume.
> - Add “what did the agent see?” trace for trust and debugging.
>
> ## Phase 8: Coding Workbench
> Build the coding “hybrid beast” layer after safety is stable.
>
> Changes:
> - Add repo tools:
>   - file search
>   - repo map
>   - git status
>   - git diff
>   - branch info
>   - test command runner
> - Add patch workflow:
>   - model proposes patch
>   - UI previews diff
>   - user approves
>   - backend applies patch
>   - tests run only after approval
> - Add coding task artifacts:
>   - Markdown summary
>   - changed files
>   - test output
>   - generated patch
>   - follow-up tasks
> - No destructive git commands.
> - No pushing from inside Rasputin until a future explicit GitHub integration permission exists.
>
> ## Phase 9: Frontend Product Standard
> Make Rasputin feel like a real local AI workbench, not a prototype dashboard.
>
> Changes:
> - Keep main screen focused on conversation and active work.
> - Use persistent left icon rail:
>   - Home
>   - Workspaces
>   - Tasks
>   - Knowledge
>   - Models
>   - Audit
>   - Settings
> - Move all config out of the home screen.
> - Add settings areas:
>   - Profile
>   - Workspaces
>   - Models
>   - Safety
>   - Knowledge
>   - Output
>   - Appearance
>   - Admin
> - Add professional renderers:
>   - Markdown
>   - code blocks
>   - citations
>   - diffs
>   - logs
>   - task artifacts
> - Add visible state for:
>   - model health
>   - active workspace
>   - privacy lock
>   - web broker activity
>   - pending approvals
>   - running tasks
>   - indexing progress
>
> ## Phase 10: Docker and Distribution
> Keep Docker as the reliable setup path without forcing model inference into the wrapper.
>
> Changes:
> - Docker Compose profiles:
>   - `default`: Rasputin wrapper only
>   - `docker-control`: wrapper plus Docker socket access
>   - `rag`: wrapper plus vector DB
>   - `search`: wrapper plus SearXNG broker
> - Wrapper binds to `127.0.0.1` only.
> - Models remain separate runtimes.
> - Mounted folders are explicit:
>   - app data
>   - approved workspaces
>   - models read-only
> - Add launch scripts:
>   - Windows PowerShell
>   - macOS/Linux shell
> - Add setup wizard for first run.
>
> ## Test Plan
> - Repo safety:
>   - no `data/`, `workspace/`, `models/`, memory, indexes, logs, or private configs can be staged
>   - secret scan passes before first push
> - Backend:
>   - auth tests
>   - permission tests
>   - task lifecycle tests
>   - SSE tests
>   - workspace path safety tests
>   - model endpoint safety tests
> - Privacy:
>   - remote model endpoint rejected by default
>   - internet search rejected unless broker permission is enabled
>   - suspicious outbound queries are blocked
>   - all sensitive actions create audit events
> - RAG/Graphify:
>   - test fixture folder ingests correctly
>   - vector search returns cited chunks
>   - graph search returns typed relationships with evidence
> - Model manager:
>   - vLLM endpoint health check
>   - llama.cpp registry import
>   - Docker start blocked when Docker control is disabled
>   - Docker containers bind localhost only when enabled
> - UI:
>   - login works
>   - task creation works
>   - live logs stream
>   - workspace selection works
>   - settings tabs work
>   - theme switching works
>   - approval prompts work
>   - Markdown export works
> - Docker:
>   - `docker compose up --build` starts successfully
>   - health check passes
>   - wrapper does not expose public network ports
>
> ## Assumptions
> - The product and repo are now named `Rasputin`.
> - The private repo target is `Shingenn5/Rasputin`.
> - The project can reference the Warmind inspiration privately, but public-facing branding should be original.
> - Security/backend work comes before coding-agent expansion.
> - Implementation should not install software, mount Docker socket, download models, delete files, or make system-level changes without explicit approval.
> - GitHub pushing happens only after the repo is sanitized and either `gh` is installed/authenticated or the private repo is manually created.

- Updated the UI with login, Rasputin branding, workspace registry controls, task modes, admin/safety settings, and richer nav.
- Docker now builds `rasputin-wrapper`, runs as Compose project `rasputin`, binds only `127.0.0.1:8787`, and has optional `rag` / `search` profiles.
- Git hygiene is fixed: `data/`, local memory, indexes, `workspace/` output, and `models/` contents are ignored.

docker compose logs rasputin-wrapper

git push -u origin main

Browser visual automation was attempted twice, but the in-app browser runtime crashed with the local Windows sandbox `spawn setup refresh` issue, so I relied on HTTP/API/Docker smoke tests.

> what do i need to do now

Right now you have Rasputin built, committed locally, and running at:

`http://127.0.0.1:8787`

Do this next:

1. **Open Rasputin**
   Go to `http://127.0.0.1:8787`

2. **Get the login password**
   Run:

```powershell
cd "C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject"
docker compose logs rasputin-wrapper
```

Look for:

```text
Rasputin first-run admin credentials
username: admin
password: ...
```

3. **Create the private GitHub repo**
   In GitHub, create a new **private** repo named:

```text
Rasputin
```

Under your account:

```text
Shingenn5
```

Do not initialize it with README/gitignore/license, since we already have a local commit.

4. **Push the local project**

```powershell
cd "C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject"
git push -u origin main
```

5. **Start testing inside Rasputin**
   After login, test in this order:
   - create a dry-run task
   - open Settings
   - check Workspaces
   - check Models
   - test `dry-run`
   - index a small safe folder with RAG
   - build Graphify
   - export a task to Markdown

Current running containers:
- Rasputin: `127.0.0.1:8787`
- vLLM: `127.0.0.1:8000`
- Open WebUI: `127.0.0.1:3000`

One important note: your GitHub push is the only blocked piece. Everything else is already running locally.

> why do i need the Open WebUI container

You probably **don’t need the Open WebUI container** for Rasputin.

Open WebUI is a separate chat UI, usually used as a polished frontend for Ollama/vLLM-style local models. Rasputin is becoming its own wrapper/workbench, so Open WebUI is only useful if you want:

- a backup chat interface for your vLLM model
- a reference UI to compare against
- a quick way to test whether your model endpoint works outside Rasputin

For Rasputin itself, the important containers are:

- **Rasputin wrapper** on `127.0.0.1:8787`
- **vLLM** on `127.0.0.1:8000`
- optional future services like Chroma/SearXNG

So you can stop Open WebUI without hurting Rasputin:

```powershell
docker stop local-agent-open-webui-1
```

Rasputin will still run. Your vLLM model will still run. Open WebUI is just extra UI sitting on port `3000`.

> are there any other changes that need to be added or made to rasputin before my testing begins

For **initial testing**, no major changes are required. Rasputin is in a good enough state to start testing now.

I would only do these before serious testing:

1. **Create/push the private GitHub repo**
   This gives you a backup point before experimenting.

2. **Test with `dry-run` first**
   Confirm UI, auth, tasks, workspaces, RAG, Graphify, and Markdown export before touching real models.

3. **Update the `main-vllm` model ID if needed**
   Rasputin currently points at `http://host.docker.internal:8000/v1` from Docker. If your vLLM server exposes a specific model name, set that in Settings → Models.

4. **Use a small safe folder first**
   Add/index a tiny test folder, not your real Documents folder yet. This will verify workspace scoping and RAG behavior safely.

5. **Optional but recommended soon**
   Add a password-change/reset UI. Right now the first-run admin password is generated and stored locally, which is fine for localhost testing, but a real settings control would be cleaner.

I’d begin testing now. The important safety defaults are already in place: remote models blocked, Docker control off, folder reorganization off, file-write previews on, and local data ignored by Git.

Done. Rasputin now has an admin password change form.

Use it here:

1. Open `http://127.0.0.1:8787`
2. Log in once with the generated admin password.
3. Go to `Settings -> Admin`
4. Fill:
   - current password
   - new password
   - confirm new password
5. Click `Change Password`

I also added the backend endpoint `POST /api/auth/change-password`, verified it works, rebuilt the Docker container, and committed it locally:

`d1feacf Add admin password change`

Rasputin is running healthy on `127.0.0.1:8787`.

> how do i get the admin password

Run this in PowerShell:

```powershell
cd "C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject"
docker compose logs rasputin-wrapper
```

Look for:

```text
Rasputin first-run admin credentials
username: admin
password: ...
```

In your current container logs, the generated password was:

```text
Ilp4Xy8X3zHW3mv_XRHUM2jk
```

After logging in, go to `Settings -> Admin` and change it to something easier for testing.

> the password wasn't inside those logs that i could find

It only prints the password the **first time** `data/auth.json` is created. Since the container was rebuilt/restarted after that, the current logs may not show it anymore.

Use this current password:

```text
Ilp4Xy8X3zHW3mv_XRHUM2jk
```

Username:

```text
admin
```

Then go to `Settings -> Admin` and change it.

If that password does not work, reset it safely:

```powershell
cd "C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject"
Copy-Item data\auth.json data\auth.backup.json
Remove-Item data\auth.json
docker compose restart rasputin-wrapper
docker compose logs --tail 80 rasputin-wrapper
```

After restart, the logs should print a fresh admin password.

> okay, now that this is properly built lets build a plan to get rasputin properly working. The accessibility features are absolute horrid, the UI is buggy and there are strange errors, and when i attempt to send a message all it returns is an http error 404

<details><summary>5 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll ground this in the actual running app before planning fixes. The `404` on send could be either a missing Rasputin route or, more likely, the selected model endpoint returning `404` from vLLM during `/v1/chat/completions`, so I’m checking both the UI wiring and backend logs first.
>
> The logs already show two concrete classes of problems: model/import errors are being logged as scary stack traces, and the task route itself returns `200`, so the “HTTP 404” on send is probably coming from the selected model endpoint during the background agent run rather than Rasputin’s `/api/tasks` route. I’m going to check the local model registry and vLLM endpoint shape next, because that will drive the fix plan.
>
> The model registry confirms the likely send-message failure: Rasputin is configured to call model id `local-main`, but your vLLM server advertises `llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf`. I’m checking whether vLLM returns `404` for the bad id and what it returns for the advertised id.
>
> A couple more facts are clear: the UI is using the correct `/api/tasks` route, but it defaults to the first enabled model, which is `main-vllm`; that local registry still says `model: local-main`, while vLLM advertises a different model id. I’ll do one cleaner model call check through PowerShell’s JSON body handling, then I can turn this into a decision-complete stabilization plan.
>
> Plan
>
> # Rasputin Stabilization Plan
>
> ## Summary
> Rasputin’s next pass should stabilize the current app instead of rebuilding from scratch. The immediate failures are clear:
>
> - Sending a message returns a model-side `404` because Rasputin is configured with `model: local-main`, but vLLM exposes `llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf`.
> - Some backend validation errors, like missing GGUF files, still produce ugly server stack traces.
> - The frontend has accessibility and reliability issues: weak focus handling, icon-only navigation, fragile boot loading, poor error display, no model-health gate before sending, and modal accessibility gaps.
>
> Chosen targets:
> - Stabilize current vanilla HTML/JS/CSS UI.
> - Target WCAG 2.2 AA basics.
> - Auto-detect vLLM models and block sends when no healthy model is selected.
>
> ## Key Changes
>
> ### Model Routing And 404 Fix
> - Add model discovery for OpenAI-compatible endpoints:
>   - `GET {base_url}/models`
>   - store discovered model IDs, health state, latency, and last error.
> - Add `POST /api/model-registry/discover` and `POST /api/model-registry/repair`.
> - On startup and model refresh, auto-repair obvious mismatches:
>   - if `main-vllm` points to `local-main`
>   - and vLLM exposes exactly one model
>   - replace `local-main` with the discovered model ID.
> - Update frontend model picker to show:
>   - healthy/unhealthy/unknown
>   - actual model ID
>   - endpoint URL
>   - last test result
> - Disable `Send` unless the selected model is `dry-run` or has passed a health check.
> - If a model call fails, task output should say something useful:
>   - `Model main-vllm returned 404. Configured model local-main was not found. Available model: llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf.`
>
> ### Backend Error Hardening
> - Replace raw `ValueError` leaks and middleware stack traces with structured JSON errors everywhere.
> - Fix endpoints like GGUF import so missing files return:
>   ```json
>   {
>     "ok": false,
>     "error": {
>       "code": "model_file_missing",
>       "message": "That GGUF path does not exist or is not visible to Rasputin."
>     }
>   }
>   ```
> - Add a safe wrapper around model calls in `backend/models.py`:
>   - catch `HTTPError`
>   - parse vLLM error body when possible
>   - log audit event
>   - return clean task error
> - Adjust request timeout middleware so handled endpoint errors do not become Starlette `ExceptionGroup` stack traces.
> - Add backend route smoke tests for:
>   - `/api/health`
>   - `/api/auth/session`
>   - `/api/model-registry`
>   - `/api/model-registry/discover`
>   - `/api/tasks`
>   - `/api/model-registry/import-gguf` bad path.
>
> ### UI Reliability Fixes
> - Make startup tolerant:
>   - replace one giant `Promise.all` boot with staged loading or `Promise.allSettled`
>   - show partial UI even if one panel fails.
> - Prevent duplicate event streams:
>   - keep one active `EventSource`
>   - close it on logout
>   - reconnect with backoff.
> - Improve task composer:
>   - visible selected model health
>   - disabled send state
>   - inline error region above composer
>   - enter-to-send with shift-enter newline.
> - Improve model settings:
>   - add `Discover Models`
>   - add `Use discovered model`
>   - show endpoint test result in readable text instead of raw JSON.
> - Improve GGUF import:
>   - validate empty path before request
>   - explain Docker visibility: model file must be under mounted `models/` or another approved visible path.
> - Add global toast/status area for errors, successes, and background activity.
>
> ### Accessibility And Layout Fixes
> - Target WCAG 2.2 AA basics:
>   - visible focus ring on every interactive element
>   - semantic landmarks: header, nav, main, aside, dialog
>   - skip-to-content link
>   - accessible names for all icon buttons
>   - no status text hidden with `font-size: 0`
>   - `aria-live` regions for task updates, errors, and connection state
>   - proper form labels and `aria-describedby` for help/error text
> - Fix settings modal:
>   - focus moves into modal when opened
>   - focus is trapped inside modal
>   - Escape closes modal
>   - focus returns to the opener
>   - background is inert while modal is open.
> - Improve keyboard navigation:
>   - all tabs and settings panels usable by keyboard
>   - nav buttons use `aria-current` or selected state
>   - no click-only controls.
> - Improve responsive behavior:
>   - avoid hiding critical activity on smaller screens without replacement
>   - make side panels collapsible instead of disappearing
>   - ensure buttons and text do not overflow.
>
> ## Public API Additions
> - `POST /api/model-registry/discover`
>   - input: `{ "key": "main-vllm" }`
>   - output: discovered models, health, latency, endpoint.
> - `POST /api/model-registry/repair`
>   - repairs invalid configured model IDs when a safe discovered replacement exists.
> - `GET /api/ui/bootstrap`
>   - optional consolidated endpoint for session, registry, workspace, safety, and task summaries.
> - Existing APIs stay backward-compatible.
>
> ## Test Plan
> - Model failure test:
>   - configure `main-vllm` with `local-main`
>   - send task
>   - verify Rasputin reports model mismatch instead of raw HTTP 404.
> - Model repair test:
>   - vLLM exposes `llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf`
>   - click `Discover Models`
>   - verify `main-vllm` updates or offers one-click repair.
> - Task flow:
>   - dry-run send succeeds
>   - healthy vLLM send succeeds
>   - unhealthy model disables Send.
> - Error handling:
>   - bad GGUF path returns structured error, no server stack trace.
>   - missing auth returns structured 401.
> - Accessibility:
>   - full app usable with keyboard only
>   - visible focus on all controls
>   - modal traps and restores focus
>   - form errors announced through live regions
>   - contrast passes for default and high-contrast themes.
> - UI smoke:
>   - login
>   - change password
>   - open settings
>   - switch tabs
>   - discover models
>   - send message
>   - view task logs
>   - export Markdown.
>
> ## Assumptions
> - Keep vanilla HTML/JS/CSS for now.
> - Do not replace the whole frontend yet.
> - Use vLLM’s `/v1/models` as the source of truth for available model IDs.
> - Default model should be the first healthy main model after discovery; otherwise fall back to `dry-run`.
> - Do not change Docker networking or enable Docker control during this stabilization pass.
</details>

The `404` is now pinned down: `local-main` returns `404`, while `llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf` returns `200`. So the plan needs to include model autodiscovery/validation and “do not let an unverified model be the default,” plus a UI/accessibility cleanup pass and backend error handling so bad inputs don’t explode into stack traces.

> # Rasputin Stabilization Plan
>
> ## Summary
> Rasputin’s next pass should stabilize the current app instead of rebuilding from scratch. The immediate failures are clear:
>
> - Sending a message returns a model-side `404` because Rasputin is configured with `model: local-main`, but vLLM exposes `llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf`.
> - Some backend validation errors, like missing GGUF files, still produce ugly server stack traces.
> - The frontend has accessibility and reliability issues: weak focus handling, icon-only navigation, fragile boot loading, poor error display, no model-health gate before sending, and modal accessibility gaps.
>
> Chosen targets:
> - Stabilize current vanilla HTML/JS/CSS UI.
> - Target WCAG 2.2 AA basics.
> - Auto-detect vLLM models and block sends when no healthy model is selected.
>
> ## Key Changes
>
> ### Model Routing And 404 Fix
> - Add model discovery for OpenAI-compatible endpoints:
>   - `GET {base_url}/models`
>   - store discovered model IDs, health state, latency, and last error.
> - Add `POST /api/model-registry/discover` and `POST /api/model-registry/repair`.
> - On startup and model refresh, auto-repair obvious mismatches:
>   - if `main-vllm` points to `local-main`
>   - and vLLM exposes exactly one model
>   - replace `local-main` with the discovered model ID.
> - Update frontend model picker to show:
>   - healthy/unhealthy/unknown
>   - actual model ID
>   - endpoint URL
>   - last test result
> - Disable `Send` unless the selected model is `dry-run` or has passed a health check.
> - If a model call fails, task output should say something useful:
>   - `Model main-vllm returned 404. Configured model local-main was not found. Available model: llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf.`
>
> ### Backend Error Hardening
> - Replace raw `ValueError` leaks and middleware stack traces with structured JSON errors everywhere.
> - Fix endpoints like GGUF import so missing files return:
>   ```json
>   {
>     "ok": false,
>     "error": {
>       "code": "model_file_missing",
>       "message": "That GGUF path does not exist or is not visible to Rasputin."
>     }
>   }
>   ```
> - Add a safe wrapper around model calls in `backend/models.py`:
>   - catch `HTTPError`
>   - parse vLLM error body when possible
>   - log audit event
>   - return clean task error
> - Adjust request timeout middleware so handled endpoint errors do not become Starlette `ExceptionGroup` stack traces.
> - Add backend route smoke tests for:
>   - `/api/health`
>   - `/api/auth/session`
>   - `/api/model-registry`
>   - `/api/model-registry/discover`
>   - `/api/tasks`
>   - `/api/model-registry/import-gguf` bad path.
>
> ### UI Reliability Fixes
> - Make startup tolerant:
>   - replace one giant `Promise.all` boot with staged loading or `Promise.allSettled`
>   - show partial UI even if one panel fails.
> - Prevent duplicate event streams:
>   - keep one active `EventSource`
>   - close it on logout
>   - reconnect with backoff.
> - Improve task composer:
>   - visible selected model health
>   - disabled send state
>   - inline error region above composer
>   - enter-to-send with shift-enter newline.
> - Improve model settings:
>   - add `Discover Models`
>   - add `Use discovered model`
>   - show endpoint test result in readable text instead of raw JSON.
> - Improve GGUF import:
>   - validate empty path before request
>   - explain Docker visibility: model file must be under mounted `models/` or another approved visible path.
> - Add global toast/status area for errors, successes, and background activity.
>
> ### Accessibility And Layout Fixes
> - Target WCAG 2.2 AA basics:
>   - visible focus ring on every interactive element
>   - semantic landmarks: header, nav, main, aside, dialog
>   - skip-to-content link
>   - accessible names for all icon buttons
>   - no status text hidden with `font-size: 0`
>   - `aria-live` regions for task updates, errors, and connection state
>   - proper form labels and `aria-describedby` for help/error text
> - Fix settings modal:
>   - focus moves into modal when opened
>   - focus is trapped inside modal
>   - Escape closes modal
>   - focus returns to the opener
>   - background is inert while modal is open.
> - Improve keyboard navigation:
>   - all tabs and settings panels usable by keyboard
>   - nav buttons use `aria-current` or selected state
>   - no click-only controls.
> - Improve responsive behavior:
>   - avoid hiding critical activity on smaller screens without replacement
>   - make side panels collapsible instead of disappearing
>   - ensure buttons and text do not overflow.
>
> ## Public API Additions
> - `POST /api/model-registry/discover`
>   - input: `{ "key": "main-vllm" }`
>   - output: discovered models, health, latency, endpoint.
> - `POST /api/model-registry/repair`
>   - repairs invalid configured model IDs when a safe discovered replacement exists.
> - `GET /api/ui/bootstrap`
>   - optional consolidated endpoint for session, registry, workspace, safety, and task summaries.
> - Existing APIs stay backward-compatible.
>
> ## Test Plan
> - Model failure test:
>   - configure `main-vllm` with `local-main`
>   - send task
>   - verify Rasputin reports model mismatch instead of raw HTTP 404.
> - Model repair test:
>   - vLLM exposes `llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf`
>   - click `Discover Models`
>   - verify `main-vllm` updates or offers one-click repair.
> - Task flow:
>   - dry-run send succeeds
>   - healthy vLLM send succeeds
>   - unhealthy model disables Send.
> - Error handling:
>   - bad GGUF path returns structured error, no server stack trace.
>   - missing auth returns structured 401.
> - Accessibility:
>   - full app usable with keyboard only
>   - visible focus on all controls
>   - modal traps and restores focus
>   - form errors announced through live regions
>   - contrast passes for default and high-contrast themes.
> - UI smoke:
>   - login
>   - change password
>   - open settings
>   - switch tabs
>   - discover models
>   - send message
>   - view task logs
>   - export Markdown.
>
> ## Assumptions
> - Keep vanilla HTML/JS/CSS for now.
> - Do not replace the whole frontend yet.
> - Use vLLM’s `/v1/models` as the source of truth for available model IDs.
> - Default model should be the first healthy main model after discovery; otherwise fall back to `dry-run`.
> - Do not change Docker networking or enable Docker control during this stabilization pass.

Implemented the stabilization pass and the camelCase cleanup.

What changed:
- Added vLLM model discovery/repair for `main-vllm`.
- Auto-repaired your local registry from `local-main` to `llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf`.
- Added friendly model 404 errors and structured GGUF errors like `modelFileMissing`.
- Added camelCase public API responses globally.
- Updated the frontend to use camelCase request/response fields.
- Added model-health gating so Send is disabled unless the selected model is healthy or `dry-run`.
- Improved startup resilience, SSE handling, status toasts, keyboard modal behavior, focus rings, skip link, live regions, and GGUF help text.
- Added smoke test skeleton at [tests/test_backend_smoke.py](./tests/test_backend_smoke.py).

Verified:
- `node --check frontend\app.js` passed.
- Python compile passed for backend/tests.
- Docker rebuild passed.
- Rasputin container is healthy at `http://127.0.0.1:8787`.
- `main-vllm` discovery returns `currentModel: llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf`.
- vLLM test prompt returns successfully.
- bad GGUF path returns structured JSON with `code: modelFileMissing`.
- Static frontend assets return `200`.

One limitation: the in-app browser automation still fails at the local browser sandbox layer, so I could not do a visual click-through from here. The app is running for you to test directly at:

`http://127.0.0.1:8787`

> is there a way for you to test the application yourself?

Yes. I can test Rasputin myself in a few ways:

1. **Backend/API tests**
   I can hit endpoints directly, test model discovery, check auth errors, verify structured JSON, test bad GGUF paths, and confirm vLLM responds.

2. **Docker runtime tests**
   I can rebuild/restart the container, inspect logs, confirm health checks, and test routes from inside the running container.

3. **Internal authenticated tests**
   I can use FastAPI’s test client inside Docker with a temporary dependency override, so I can test protected routes without knowing your admin password.

4. **Frontend static checks**
   I can verify `index.html`, `app.js`, and `styles.css` load, run JS syntax checks, and inspect DOM-facing code.

The only thing currently blocked is a true visual click-through test in the browser. I tried the Codex in-app browser, but its local browser sandbox failed. To make this properly testable, the best next step is adding a real automated test harness:

- `pytest` / `httpx` for backend routes
- Playwright for login, settings, model discovery, send-message, modal accessibility
- a test-only auth bypass env var for CI/local testing
- a seeded test config so tests never touch your real password or private data

That would let me run: `docker compose up --build`, then a full automated smoke suite against `http://127.0.0.1:8787`.

Built the Rasputin testing harness.

What’s included:
- Isolated test Docker setup: [docker-compose.test.yml](./docker-compose.test.yml)
- Windows runner: [scripts/test.ps1](./scripts/test.ps1)
- macOS/Linux runner: [scripts/test.sh](./scripts/test.sh)
- Backend smoke tests: [tests/testBackendSmoke.py](./tests/testBackendSmoke.py)
- Live API/task smoke test: [tests/liveSmoke.py](./tests/liveSmoke.py)
- Optional Playwright UI smoke: [tests/ui/rasputinSmoke.spec.mjs](./tests/ui/rasputinSmoke.spec.mjs)
- Playwright config/package scripts: [playwright.config.mjs](./playwright.config.mjs), [package.json](./package.json)

How to run the main harness:

```powershell
.\scripts\test.ps1
```

It starts an isolated wrapper on `127.0.0.1:8877`, uses `testdata/` instead of your real `data/`, runs backend tests, runs live API smoke, starts a dry-run task, checks structured errors, then cleans the test container up.

Verified:
- JS syntax passed.
- Python compile passed.
- `.\scripts\test.ps1` passed.
- The test container cleaned itself up.
- Your normal Rasputin wrapper is still running on `127.0.0.1:8787`.

Optional UI browser tests need dev deps first:

```powershell
npm install
npx playwright install chromium
.\scripts\test.ps1 -Ui
```

> Plan
>
> # Rasputin UI Repair Execution Plan
>
> ## Summary
> Implement the existing UI repair plan as a focused frontend rebuild while keeping the current vanilla HTML/JS/CSS stack and backend APIs. The target is a polished local AI workbench: labeled navigation, professional light/dark themes, cleaner chat-first home screen, and a settings experience that feels like product software instead of a prototype modal.
>
> ## Key Changes
>
> - Replace the current `74px` icon-only rail with an expandable labeled sidebar:
>   - Expanded width: `236px`; collapsed width: `72px`.
>   - Nav items: Home, Workspaces, Tasks, Knowledge, Models, Audit, Settings.
>   - Each item has icon + visible label in expanded mode, tooltip/accessibility label in collapsed mode, and a clear active state.
>   - Persist collapsed state in `localStorage`.
>
> - Reset the visual system in `frontend/styles.css`:
>   - Default theme becomes `rasputin-light`, not sage.
>   - Add first-class `rasputin-dark` and keep `contrast`.
>   - Remove/demote novelty themes from the visible UI.
>   - Use graphite text, white/near-white surfaces, steel-blue accent, tighter borders, smaller radii, and minimal shadows.
>
> - Rework the home screen around chat:
>   - Keep conversation and composer as the dominant surface.
>   - Move noisy stats/activity into a dedicated Tasks view.
>   - Header shows active workspace, active model, privacy state, and settings button.
>   - Composer keeps model health and send state but removes clutter like the Profile button from the primary path.
>   - Task logs/sources/artifacts stay accessible through disclosure panels, not always-on blocks.
>
> - Replace modal settings with a full settings view inside the app:
>   - Settings sections: General, Workspaces, Models, Safety, Knowledge, Output, Appearance, Admin.
>   - Left settings subnav, right focused content.
>   - Advanced/raw model fields go behind `Advanced` disclosures.
>   - Models settings start with workflow actions: Discover vLLM, Scan GGUF Library, Import, Test, Start/Stop when enabled.
>   - Safety settings use grouped panels instead of one giant checkbox grid.
>
> - Clean up `frontend/app.js` state without adding a framework:
>   - Add `activeView`, `activeSettingsSection`, `sidebarCollapsed`, `activeTheme`, `selectedModel`, and `activeWorkspace`.
>   - Replace modal open/close logic with route-like view switching.
>   - Keep existing API calls and camelCase payloads unchanged.
>   - Add stable `data-testid` attributes for key UI controls.
>
> ## Public Interfaces
> - No backend API changes required.
> - Frontend behavior changes:
>   - Sidebar nav uses `data-view`.
>   - Settings subnav uses `data-settings-section`.
>   - Theme values become `rasputin-light`, `rasputin-dark`, and `contrast`.
>   - Preserve backward compatibility by mapping old saved theme names to `rasputin-light`.
>
> ## Test Plan
> - Static checks:
>   - `node --check frontend/app.js`
>   - `git diff --check`
> - Docker/API harness:
>   - `powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File ./scripts/test.ps1`
> - Playwright UI smoke:
>   - login screen loads
>   - sidebar expanded labels visible
>   - sidebar collapse persists after refresh
>   - Home, Tasks, Models, Safety, Appearance, and Settings views open
>   - theme switching works for light/dark/contrast
>   - GGUF scan button responds
>   - model discovery button responds
>   - send button disables for unhealthy model
> - Visual review screenshots:
>   - home desktop
>   - home mobile
>   - expanded sidebar
>   - collapsed sidebar
>   - settings view
>   - models view
>   - dark theme
>
> ## Assumptions
> - Keep vanilla HTML/JS/CSS.
> - Do not add Bootstrap, React, Vue, Tailwind, or a new icon package in this pass.
> - Use the existing inline SVG icons for now, but pair them with visible labels.
> - Implement the full settings view inside the current single-page app rather than adding server-side routes.
> - Treat this as a frontend polish/restructure pass only; backend behavior stays unchanged unless a UI smoke test exposes an existing API bug.
</details>

I’m in Plan Mode, so I can’t mutate the repo or actually implement yet. I’ll treat “execute” as “produce the decision-complete execution plan for implementing the UI repair,” grounded in the current files first.
