> # Rasputin UI Repair Execution Plan
>
> ## Summary
> Implement the existing UI repair plan as a focused frontend rebuild while keeping the current vanilla HTML/JS/CSS stack and backend APIs. The target is a polished local AI workbench: labeled navigation, professional light/dark themes, cleaner chat-first home screen, and a settings experience that feels like product software instead of a prototype modal.
>
> ## Key Changes
>
> - Replace the current `74px` icon-only rail with an expandable labeled sidebar:
>   - Expanded width: `236px`; collapsed width: `72px`.
>   - Nav items: Home, Workspaces, Tasks, Knowledge, Models, Audit, Settings.
>   - Each item has icon + visible label in expanded mode, tooltip/accessibility label in collapsed mode, and a clear active state.
>   - Persist collapsed state in `localStorage`.
>
> - Reset the visual system in `frontend/styles.css`:
>   - Default theme becomes `rasputin-light`, not sage.
>   - Add first-class `rasputin-dark` and keep `contrast`.
>   - Remove/demote novelty themes from the visible UI.
>   - Use graphite text, white/near-white surfaces, steel-blue accent, tighter borders, smaller radii, and minimal shadows.
>
> - Rework the home screen around chat:
>   - Keep conversation and composer as the dominant surface.
>   - Move noisy stats/activity into a dedicated Tasks view.
>   - Header shows active workspace, active model, privacy state, and settings button.
>   - Composer keeps model health and send state but removes clutter like the Profile button from the primary path.
>   - Task logs/sources/artifacts stay accessible through disclosure panels, not always-on blocks.
>
> - Replace modal settings with a full settings view inside the app:
>   - Settings sections: General, Workspaces, Models, Safety, Knowledge, Output, Appearance, Admin.
>   - Left settings subnav, right focused content.
>   - Advanced/raw model fields go behind `Advanced` disclosures.
>   - Models settings start with workflow actions: Discover vLLM, Scan GGUF Library, Import, Test, Start/Stop when enabled.
>   - Safety settings use grouped panels instead of one giant checkbox grid.
>
> - Clean up `frontend/app.js` state without adding a framework:
>   - Add `activeView`, `activeSettingsSection`, `sidebarCollapsed`, `activeTheme`, `selectedModel`, and `activeWorkspace`.
>   - Replace modal open/close logic with route-like view switching.
>   - Keep existing API calls and camelCase payloads unchanged.
>   - Add stable `data-testid` attributes for key UI controls.
>
> ## Public Interfaces
> - No backend API changes required.
> - Frontend behavior changes:
>   - Sidebar nav uses `data-view`.
>   - Settings subnav uses `data-settings-section`.
>   - Theme values become `rasputin-light`, `rasputin-dark`, and `contrast`.
>   - Preserve backward compatibility by mapping old saved theme names to `rasputin-light`.
>
> ## Test Plan
> - Static checks:
>   - `node --check frontend/app.js`
>   - `git diff --check`
> - Docker/API harness:

>   - sidebar collapse persists after refresh

>   - GGUF scan button responds

>   - send button disables for unhealthy model
> - Visual review screenshots:
>   - home desktop

>   - collapsed sidebar
>   - settings view
>   - models view
>   - dark theme

> ## Assumptions
> - Keep vanilla HTML/JS/CSS.
> - Do not add Bootstrap, React, Vue, Tailwind, or a new icon package in this pass.
> - Use the existing inline SVG icons for now, but pair them with visible labels.
> - Implement the full settings view inside the current single-page app rather than adding server-side routes.
> - Treat this as a frontend polish/restructure pass only; backend behavior stays unchanged unless a UI smoke test exposes an existing API bug.

> where is anything being saved? is it being saved in browser storage or what?

<details><summary>3 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll trace the storage paths instead of guessing from memory, because Rasputin now has browser state, backend JSON state, and Docker-mounted app data all in play.
>
> I found the split: small UI preferences are in browser `localStorage`, while the real app state lives as JSON files under the project’s `data` folder. Docker mounts that same local folder into the container, so it persists across restarts.
>
> One subtle piece: the login session itself is not stored in `localStorage`; it’s an HTTP-only cookie, while the server keeps active session tokens in memory and the admin credential hash in `data/auth.json`.
</details>

Right now Rasputin saves state in **three places**:

1. **Browser `localStorage`**
Only tiny UI preferences:
- `rasputin-theme`
- `rasputin-sidebar-collapsed`
- old `local-ai-hub-theme` is only read for backward compatibility

That logic is in [frontend/app.js](</C:/Users/elliott/OneDrive/Documents/WrapperProject/frontend/app.js:86>).

2. **Browser cookie**
Login uses a cookie named `rasputin_session`, not `localStorage`. The cookie identifies the session, while the active session token map lives in backend memory and disappears when the server restarts. The saved admin credential hash is on disk in `data/auth.json`, not plaintext. See [backend/auth.py](</C:/Users/elliott/OneDrive/Documents/WrapperProject/backend/auth.py:14>) and [backend/main.py](</C:/Users/elliott/OneDrive/Documents/WrapperProject/backend/main.py:256>).

3. **Local project `data/` folder**
This is the real persistent app state. Because Docker Compose mounts `./data:/app/data`, it survives container restarts. See [docker-compose.yml](</C:/Users/elliott/OneDrive/Documents/WrapperProject/docker-compose.yml:23>).

Current saved files include:

```text
C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\data\auth.json
C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\data\security.json
C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\data\models.json
C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\data\workspace.json
C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\data\rag_index.json
C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\data\graph.json
C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\data\memory.json
C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\data\output.json
C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\data\audit.jsonl
```

Also:
- Default markdown exports go under `workspace/markdown-output`.
- Local model files live under `models/`, mounted read-only into Docker as `/app/models`.
- Approved workspace folders are stored in `data/workspace.json`, but the actual files remain wherever those folders are on your machine.
- Running/completed task objects are currently mostly in memory; after a server restart, the task list is not fully persistent unless exported or audited.

So no, the important Rasputin data is **not mainly browser storage**. Browser storage is just UI preference fluff. The durable app brain is mostly local JSON under `C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\data`.

Implemented. Rasputin is running here:

[http://127.0.0.1:8787](http://127.0.0.1:8787)

What changed:
- Added durable preferences at `data/preferences.json` via new `GET/POST /api/preferences`.
- Theme, sidebar collapsed state, selected model, skill, task mode, helper count, active view, and settings section now restore after login.
- Kept browser `localStorage` as a fast same-browser fallback, then syncs it back to backend preferences.
- Reworked the UI toward patterns from [Open WebUI](https://github.com/open-webui/open-webui), [AnythingLLM](https://anythingllm.com/), and [LibreChat](https://github.com/danny-avila/LibreChat): stronger left sidebar, real New Task action, cleaner chat canvas, quick-start prompt chips, better settings/model surfaces.
- Cleaned dry-run output so it no longer dumps internal prompts into the chat.
- Removed loud completed-task progress bars.

Verified:
- `node --check frontend\app.js` passed.
- `node --check tests\ui\rasputinSmoke.spec.mjs` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed, only CRLF warnings.
- Full Docker/API/UI harness passed: 9 backend tests, 4 Playwright UI tests.
- Main container is healthy and bound to `127.0.0.1:8787`.

I did not copy code from those projects; I used them as UI/product references. The app is better now, though I’d still call this “first acceptable product shell,” not final polish.

I’m going to inspect the app shell, model registry rendering, workspace controls, and the latest screenshots so the plan targets the real problems instead of vague “make it nicer” energy.
>
> I confirmed the current app still exposes three things that would confuse a normal user: raw model metadata displayed like “tags,” path-entry workspace forms, and placeholder-ish home behavior after previous smoke-test tasks. I’m checking the screenshots directly now so the plan addresses what a user actually sees.
>
> The mobile screenshot confirms the home page is especially rough: sidebar consumes too much of the first viewport, the chat starts halfway down, and old task cards crowd the composer. I’ll plan a stronger app-shell redesign, not just CSS polish.
>
> Plan
>
> # Rasputin Production UI Readiness Plan
>
> ## Summary
> Rasputin should move from prototype dashboard to a production-grade local AI workbench inspired by proven patterns from [Open WebUI](https://github.com/open-webui/open-webui), [AnythingLLM](https://anythingllm.com/), and [LibreChat](https://github.com/danny-avila/LibreChat): clean chat-first home, simple model selection, GUI workspace selection, and advanced configuration hidden until needed.
>
> Default product direction:
> - Home is a clean ChatGPT/Gemini-style conversation surface.
> - Tasks/logs/debugging move out of Home.
> - Normal model UI shows one active model, not registry tags.
> - Folder access uses approved roots, plus an optional Docker mount workflow for adding new host folders.
>
> ## Key Changes
>
> ### Home And App Shell
> - Rebuild Home as a clean chat-first surface:
>   - Empty state with short “What are we working on?” prompt and 3-4 useful quick actions.
>   - Composer pinned cleanly at bottom.
>   - No task cards, logs, badges, progress bars, or debug metadata by default.
>   - Recent/completed tasks move to Tasks view only.
> - Replace strange UI text and random periods:
>   - Display `No workspace selected` instead of `workspace: .`.
>   - Display `Project Root` or folder name instead of raw `.`.
>   - Remove lone punctuation pills such as `.` from task metadata.
> - Add message rendering polish:
>   - User/assistant bubbles with consistent spacing.
>   - Markdown renderer for assistant output.
>   - Collapsed “Details” panel for model, sources, logs, and export actions.
> - Improve mobile:
>   - Sidebar becomes a top compact nav/drawer, not a full first-screen takeover.
>   - Home chat and composer appear in the first viewport.
>   - Status chips collapse into one compact runtime bar.
>
> ### Model UI Simplification
> - Normal user path shows only:
>   - Active Model
>   - Status
>   - Refresh/Test button
>   - “Use this model” if discovery finds one
> - Hide registry implementation details under Advanced:
>   - `key`, `role`, `provider`, `modelId`, `baseUrl`, `runtime`, `latency`, `discoveredModels`.
> - Do not show `dry-run` or `local-embeddings` in the main model picker by default.
>   - `dry-run` becomes a Testing Mode toggle under Advanced/Developer.
>   - `local-embeddings` appears only in Knowledge settings.
> - Rename confusing labels:
>   - `tags`/badges become readable fields like `Purpose`, `Runtime`, `Health`.
>   - `main-vllm` becomes `Main Local Model`.
>   - `local-main` mismatch warnings become plain English.
>
> ### Workspace GUI And Docker Mounts
> - Add a dedicated Workspaces page with two tabs:
>   - `Approved Folders`: folders Rasputin can currently access.
>   - `Add Folder`: guided flow for adding a new folder.
> - Approved Roots browser:
>   - Backend exposes safe roots visible inside Docker.
>   - UI shows folder cards, breadcrumbs, search, and child-folder browsing.
>   - User selects folders with buttons, not manual path entry.
> - Docker mounted-folder workflow:
>   - Add “Mount New Host Folder” action behind Safety/Admin permission.
>   - Flow: choose host path text field or guided mount form, preview mount, choose read-only/read-write, approve, restart wrapper.
>   - Default mount mode is read-only.
>   - Write/reorganize permissions remain separate and off unless explicitly enabled.
> - Keep manual path entry only under Advanced.
>
> ### Settings Restructure
> - Settings sections become product-focused:
>   - General
>   - Workspaces
>   - Models
>   - Safety
>   - Knowledge
>   - Output
>   - Appearance
>   - Admin
> - Each section gets a focused page, not mixed dashboard panels.
> - Use plain language and inline help only where it prevents confusion.
> - Add persistent preferences for:
>   - theme
>   - sidebar state
>   - active model
>   - active workspace
>   - task mode
>   - selected settings section
>
> ## Public Interfaces
>
> ### Backend
> - Add/extend workspace APIs:
>   - `GET /api/workspace/roots`
>   - `POST /api/workspace/browse`
>   - `POST /api/workspace/approve`
>   - `POST /api/workspace/mount-plan`
>   - `POST /api/workspace/mount-apply`
> - Add response fields for display names:
>   - `displayName`
>   - `isMounted`
>   - `permissionProfile`
>   - `readOnly`
>   - `requiresRestart`
> - Keep existing path-based endpoints for backward compatibility.
>
> ### Frontend
> - Add stable UI test ids:
>   - `home-empty-state`
>   - `workspace-browser`
>   - `workspace-mount-plan`
>   - `active-model-card`
>   - `advanced-model-registry`
>   - `runtime-details-toggle`
> - Main model picker only lists user-facing chat models.
> - Advanced model registry remains available but hidden by default.
>
> ## Test Plan
> - UI smoke:
>   - Home loads cleanly with no task/debug clutter.
>   - No random `.` labels appear in Home, sidebar, model picker, or task metadata.
>   - Active model card shows one readable model.
>   - Advanced model registry can be expanded.
>   - Mobile first viewport shows chat/composer, not only sidebar.
> - Workspace tests:
>   - Approved roots load.
>   - Folder browser navigates via GUI.
>   - Selecting a folder updates active workspace.
>   - Manual path entry remains available under Advanced.
>   - Mount plan preview does not mutate anything.
>   - Mount apply is blocked unless Docker control permission is enabled.
> - Safety tests:
>   - New mounted folders default read-only.
>   - Write/reorganize permissions stay separate.
>   - Docker mount actions create audit events.
>   - Path traversal outside approved roots is rejected.
> - Regression tests:
>   - Existing `/api/workspace/select` and `/api/workspace/list` still work.
>   - Preferences persist across logout/login and container restart.
>   - Existing Playwright screenshots update for home desktop, home mobile, models, workspaces, and dark theme.
>
> ## Assumptions
> - Keep vanilla HTML/JS/CSS for this pass.
> - Keep Docker as the main runtime.
> - Use Approved Roots as the primary folder GUI.
> - Add Docker folder mounting as an explicit advanced/admin workflow, not automatic background behavior.
> - Default new mounts to read-only.
> - Hide `dry-run`, embeddings, raw keys, providers, and model roles from normal users.
> - Keep advanced registry/debug controls available for you as the builder.
</details>

One important implementation constraint popped out: because Rasputin is currently Dockerized, the backend cannot magically browse every folder on your Windows machine. It can only see folders mounted into the container. So a production folder picker needs an “approved host roots” design, or it needs a local desktop/helper layer with narrower OS permissions.

<details><summary>144 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll implement this as a focused production-readiness pass: backend workspace browse/mount-preview APIs, simpler model surfaces, chat-first Home with old tasks moved out, and UI tests to lock the behavior. I’m going to read the current workspace/model/task rendering first so the edits land cleanly instead of fighting the existing app state.
>
> I’ve got the current shape now. The backend already knows the approved workspace registry but has no “browse roots” abstraction, and the frontend currently uses raw path forms plus model badges everywhere. I’m adding the backend primitives first so the UI has real data to render.
>
> I’m going to keep the Docker mount workflow safe in this pass: the UI will create a mount plan and `mount-apply` will be permission-gated. Without Docker-control enabled, it returns a clean denial. With it enabled, it records the requested mount and tells you a restart/update is required, rather than silently mutating Docker Compose behind your back.
>

Rasputin is live here: [http://127.0.0.1:8787](http://127.0.0.1:8787)

What changed:
- Rebuilt Home into a cleaner chat-first surface with no task/debug clutter by default.
- Moved task history/log-heavy views into Tasks.
- Simplified the normal model UI to “Main Local Model” instead of raw tags/keys.
- Hid `dry-run`, embeddings, provider keys, model IDs, etc. behind Advanced/Developer controls.
- Added GUI workspace selection with approved folder cards and a folder browser.
- Added safe mount preview flow for new Docker folders. Actual mount apply is still blocked unless Docker control is enabled.
- Replaced awkward `.` labels with `Project Root` / readable folder names.
- Fixed mobile nav so it becomes icon-first instead of chopped text.
- Added persistent preferences for theme/sidebar/model/workspace/task defaults.
- Added backend workspace APIs for roots, browse, approve, mount-plan, and gated mount-apply.

Verification passed:
- `node --check frontend\app.js`
- `git diff --check`

Implemented the next production-readiness pass and restarted Rasputin.

Rasputin is live at: [http://127.0.0.1:8787](http://127.0.0.1:8787)

What I found and fixed:
- Hardened auth in [backend/auth.py](./backend/auth.py): session expiry, login failure throttling, safer env parsing.
- Tightened safety gates in [backend/main.py](./backend/main.py): RAG search, Graphify search, workspace browsing/listing, Markdown output, and exports now respect file read/write permissions.
- Enforced workspace permissions in [backend/mcp_layer.py](./backend/mcp_layer.py): read-only approved folders now actually block MCP writes/moves.
- Hardened model runtime behavior in [backend/model_registry.py](./backend/model_registry.py): no Docker status/log calls while Docker control is disabled, and GGUF imports must come from mounted `models/` or approved workspaces.
- Stabilized Project Root in [backend/workspace.py](./backend/workspace.py): the built-in root can’t be accidentally renamed/downgraded by folder approval flows.
- Stopped leaking raw internal errors in [backend/response.py](./backend/response.py).
- Fixed the PowerShell harness so failed UI tests actually fail the script.
- Expanded backend/live/UI tests to cover these production safety boundaries.
- Updated [README.md](./README.md) with the new hardening behavior.

Verification passed:
- `node --check frontend\app.js`
- `node --check tests\ui\rasputinSmoke.spec.mjs`
- `git diff --check`
- `.\scripts\test.ps1 -Ui`
- Full harness result: 15 backend smoke tests, live smoke, and 4 Playwright UI tests passed.
- Main Docker container is healthy on `127.0.0.1:8787`.
- Confirmed the running container includes the new hardening code.

Next production pass should probably focus on packaging/release hygiene: first-run setup wizard, env/config validation, release checklist, repo push/PR workflow, and a proper “production mode” config profile.

> what libraries are we currently using

<details><summary>2 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll pull this from the actual dependency files rather than memory, then I’ll separate “runtime libraries” from test/dev tooling and Docker images.
>
> The declared dependency list is very small. I’m doing one quick import scan too, because sometimes a package is listed but not actually used yet.
</details>

Currently Rasputin is using a pretty small dependency stack.

**Backend Python**
Declared in [requirements.txt](./requirements.txt):

- `fastapi==0.115.6` - API server framework
- `uvicorn[standard]==0.34.0` - ASGI server that exposes Rasputin on localhost
- `pydantic==2.10.4` - request/response models and camelCase API parsing
- `httpx==0.28.1` - installed, but not really used yet in the backend code

The backend mostly uses Python standard library modules for everything else: `json`, `pathlib`, `urllib`, `subprocess`, `hashlib`, `secrets`, `asyncio`, etc.

**Frontend**
No frontend framework right now.

- Vanilla `HTML`
- Vanilla `CSS`
- Vanilla `JavaScript`

Important note: we are not currently using Bootstrap in the actual UI, even though it was in the original desired stack. The current UI is custom CSS/JS.

**Testing**
Declared in [package.json](./package.json):

- `@playwright/test` - UI/browser smoke tests

The lockfile currently resolves Playwright to `1.60.0`.

**Docker / Runtime Images**
Used in [docker-compose.yml](./docker-compose.yml) and [Dockerfile](./Dockerfile):

- `python:3.12-slim` - main Rasputin wrapper image base
- `docker:27-cli` - copied into the wrapper image for optional Docker-control mode
- `chromadb/chroma:latest` - optional `rag` profile, not actively wired into code yet
- `searxng/searxng:latest` - optional `search` profile, not actively wired into code yet
- `ghcr.io/ggml-org/llama.cpp:server` - default image for managed GGUF helper models

**Local AI / Retrieval**
Currently custom-built, not library-backed:

- RAG is custom JSON + hash-vector retrieval in `backend/rag.py`
- Graphify is custom relationship extraction in `backend/graphify.py`
- Web search currently uses Python `urllib` against DuckDuckGo HTML, not SearXNG yet
- Model calls use OpenAI-compatible HTTP endpoints through Python `urllib`, not the OpenAI SDK

So the short version: FastAPI + Uvicorn + Pydantic on the backend, vanilla frontend, Playwright for UI tests, Docker for distribution/runtime, and most AI/RAG/Graph logic is still custom code.

> what would node.js replace if we were to use that?

Node.js would mainly replace the **Python backend runtime**, not the frontend.

Right now Rasputin is roughly:

```text
Browser UI
  -> vanilla HTML/CSS/JS
  -> Python FastAPI server
  -> local files / RAG / Graphify / model registry / vLLM / Docker control
```

If we switched backend to Node.js, it would become:

```text
Browser UI
  -> vanilla HTML/CSS/JS
  -> Node.js server
  -> local files / RAG / Graphify / model registry / vLLM / Docker control
```

Node.js would replace:

- `server.py`
- `backend/main.py`
- FastAPI routes
- Pydantic request models
- Uvicorn
- most backend Python modules over time:
  - `auth.py`
  - `workspace.py`
  - `model_registry.py`
  - `agent.py`
  - `mcp_layer.py`
  - `rag.py`
  - `graphify.py`
  - `security.py`
  - `audit.py`
  - `output.py`

The likely Node equivalents would be:

- FastAPI -> `Express`, `Fastify`, or `Hono`
- Uvicorn -> Node’s built-in HTTP server, usually through Express/Fastify
- Pydantic -> `zod` or `valibot`
- Python file APIs -> Node `fs/promises`
- Python `urllib` -> `fetch` / `undici`
- Python subprocess -> Node `child_process`
- Python SSE -> Express/Fastify streaming response
- Python tests -> `vitest`, `node:test`, or `jest`

It would **not** replace:

- the browser UI unless we choose to
- Docker
- vLLM
- llama.cpp
- GGUF models
- the localhost architecture
- the privacy/safety model

My honest take: Node would be especially good if Rasputin becomes more of a desktop/web product with lots of real-time UI, WebSockets/SSE, plugin management, and Electron/Tauri-style packaging. Python is better if the backend is going to lean heavily into ML/data tooling, document parsing, embeddings, vector databases, and agent experiments.

For Rasputin specifically, I’d keep Python for now. The app already works, and Python gives us easier access to future RAG/document/ML tooling. Node would be a reasonable later move only if we decide Rasputin’s “product shell” matters more than the AI/data backend.

> lets add a frontend stack to get away from the AI looking feel of our current UI

<details><summary>46 previous messages</summary>

> I agree with the direction. The backend is now the stronger half of Rasputin; the frontend is still carrying too much hand-rolled prototype energy. Correct. Rasputin should only start when you start the Docker container.

I added browser-opening as a launch helper only. It does **not** start Rasputin by itself. The flow is now:

1. You run `.\start-wrapper.ps1`
2. Docker starts the Rasputin container
3. A tiny helper waits for `http://127.0.0.1:8787/api/health`
4. Once healthy, it opens `http://127.0.0.1:8787` in your browser

If you do not want the browser to auto-open:

```powershell
.\start-wrapper.ps1 -NoBrowser
```

One important limitation: if you start the container directly from Docker Desktop, the container should **not** open your host browser. That is a good security boundary. In that case, manually open:

```text
http://127.0.0.1:8787
```

I also syntax-checked the PowerShell launch scripts, and they parse cleanly.

You’re thinking about it correctly. The wrapper **does** run inside the Docker container already.

Technically, this is the chain:

```text
Docker container starts
-> Docker runs CMD ["python", "-u", "server.py"]
-> server.py starts the FastAPI web server inside the container
-> FastAPI listens on container port 8787
-> Docker Compose maps host 127.0.0.1:8787 to container 8787
-> You manually open http://127.0.0.1:8787
```

Docker does not replace `server.py`; Docker only runs and isolates the process. Something inside the container still has to listen on a port and answer HTTP requests. In Rasputin’s case, that process is Python/FastAPI.

The helper I added was only a host-side PowerShell watcher that polled `/api/health` and opened the browser. Since you don’t want that, I removed it.

I also changed Docker behavior so Rasputin only starts when you start the container:

- Removed browser auto-open behavior
- Removed the helper script
- Changed `docker-compose.yml` restart policy from `unless-stopped` to `restart: "no"`
- Updated the currently running container restart policy to `no`

Verified current container policy:

```text
no
```

So now the intended behavior is: you start the Docker container, Rasputin starts inside it, Docker exposes the localhost port, and you open the browser manually.

The white screen was almost certainly browser cache.

What happened: the app was previously built to stable asset names like:

```text
/static/app.js
/static/styles.css
```

Your browser likely cached an older `app.js`. After the React/Vite migration, the HTML changed, but the browser could still reuse the stale JS file, which can produce a blank white page.

I fixed it properly by switching the frontend build to hashed assets:

```text
/static/assets/index-BdZ2Qwtb.js
/static/assets/index-aZ_epEzQ.css
```

That means every rebuild gets a new asset URL, so the browser is forced to load the correct code.

I also rebuilt/recreated the Docker container. Current status:

```text
rasputin-rasputin-wrapper-1   healthy   127.0.0.1:8787->8787/tcp
```

Verified:
- `http://127.0.0.1:8787/api/health` returns ready
- Headless Chromium renders the Rasputin login screen
- Full test harness passed: 15 backend tests, live smoke, 4 Playwright UI tests

Try opening this exact URL:

```text
http://127.0.0.1:8787
```

If your browser still shows white, do a hard refresh once: `Ctrl + Shift + R`. The new hashed assets should prevent this from recurring.

[FRONTEND_REDESIGN_PLAN.md](</C:/Users/elliott/OneDrive/Documents/WrapperProject/docs/FRONTEND_REDESIGN_PLAN.md>)

The recommendation is to **stay on React + Vite** since Rasputin already has that foundation, then redesign the UI around **Bootstrap 5.3 + React-Bootstrap** to keep custom CSS low. The plan calls out the current problem directly: `main.jsx` is still a monolith and `styles.css` is doing too much hand-rolled UI work.

The plan includes:
- stack decision and why not Svelte/Vue right now
- Bootstrap-first styling strategy
- proposed source folder structure
- dependency plan
- phased migration path
- acceptance criteria
- test commands
- rollback strategy
- first safe implementation slice

<details><summary>3 previous messages</summary>

> I’m going to execute the redesign in slices, starting with the plan’s foundation: install Bootstrap/React-Bootstrap, split the React monolith into a real app structure, and replace the custom visual shell with Bootstrap-based components while preserving the existing backend contracts and UI test IDs.
>

Bootstrap has to add CSS because Bootstrap **is mostly a CSS design system**.

When we use Bootstrap classes like:

```html
<button class="btn btn-primary">
<div class="card">
<select class="form-select">
<div class="row">
```

those classes do nothing unless Bootstrap’s CSS exists somewhere. The browser does not know what `btn`, `card`, `row`, `col`, `form-control`, `badge`, or `accordion` mean by itself.

So this import:

```js
import "bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css";
```

adds the stylesheet that defines all of those classes.

There are two different meanings of “less CSS”:

1. **Less custom Rasputin CSS**
   This is what Bootstrap helps with. We write fewer homemade styles because Bootstrap provides buttons, cards, forms, grids, spacing, badges, navs, etc.

2. **Less total CSS shipped to the browser**
   Full Bootstrap does not help with that by default. It adds a fairly large stylesheet because it includes many components we may not use.

The current build used full Bootstrap, which is why the CSS bundle got bigger. That is expected.

The better middle path is:

```text
Use Bootstrap as the styling system,
but import only the Bootstrap parts Rasputin actually uses.
```

That means instead of importing all Bootstrap CSS, we compile a smaller Bootstrap SCSS file with only things like:

```scss
@import "bootstrap/scss/functions";
@import "bootstrap/scss/variables";
@import "bootstrap/scss/mixins";
@import "bootstrap/scss/reboot";
@import "bootstrap/scss/grid";
@import "bootstrap/scss/buttons";
@import "bootstrap/scss/forms";
@import "bootstrap/scss/card";
@import "bootstrap/scss/nav";
@import "bootstrap/scss/badge";
@import "bootstrap/scss/accordion";
@import "bootstrap/scss/utilities";
```

So Bootstrap does need CSS, but we do **not** necessarily need the entire Bootstrap CSS payload.

> teach me how to build this myself

Absolutely. The way to learn this is to **not** start with “build Rasputin.” Start with the smallest local AI wrapper, then add one system at a time.

Think of Rasputin as five layers:

```text
Browser UI
  -> FastAPI backend
  -> task manager / agent loop
  -> model router
  -> local tools: files, RAG, graph, audit, Docker
```

The current guide is here too: [RASPUTIN_ARCHITECTURE_GUIDE.md](./docs/RASPUTIN_ARCHITECTURE_GUIDE.md)

**1. Learn The Runtime**
The app runs because Docker starts a container from [Dockerfile](./Dockerfile), then [docker-compose.yml](./docker-compose.yml) exposes the container’s internal FastAPI port to your machine:

```text
container: 0.0.0.0:8787
host:      127.0.0.1:8787
```

That means your browser talks to:

```text
http://127.0.0.1:8787
```

Docker is the thing exposing the port. Python is only the server running *inside* the container.

**2. Build The Tiny Backend First**
The backend should start as just:

```text
GET /api/health
POST /api/tasks
GET /api/tasks
```

In Rasputin, the entry is [server.py](./server.py), which boots the FastAPI app from the backend package.

The mental model:

```text
User submits message
  -> backend creates task
  -> task manager runs work in background
  -> logs/status update
  -> frontend polls or streams updates
```

Before models, RAG, Docker control, or Graphify, you want task creation to work perfectly.

**3. Build The Frontend As A Control Surface**
The frontend source is here:

[frontend-src/src](./frontend-src/src)

The important pieces are:

[main.jsx](./frontend-src/src/main.jsx)
Boots React.

[App.jsx](./frontend-src/src/app/App.jsx)
Owns app-level state and API loading.

[AppShell.jsx](./frontend-src/src/components/AppShell.jsx)
Controls the layout.

[HomeView.jsx](./frontend-src/src/features/chat/HomeView.jsx)
The main chat surface.

[SettingsView.jsx](./frontend-src/src/features/settings/SettingsView.jsx)
Models, workspaces, safety, appearance, etc.

You build the frontend with:

```powershell
npm install
npm run build
```

That produces static files in:

[frontend](./frontend)

FastAPI serves those files to the browser.

**4. Add Model Routing**
Do not hard-code “the model.” Instead, store model entries like:

```text
Main Local Model
  provider: openai-compatible
  baseUrl: http://host.docker.internal:8000/v1
  modelId: actual-model-name-from-vllm
```

The backend discovers available models by calling:

```text
GET /v1/models
```

against vLLM. Then Rasputin picks a healthy model and sends chat requests to it.

That logic lives around:

[backend/model_registry.py](./backend/model_registry.py)
[backend/models.py](./backend/models.py)

**5. Add Safety Before Power**
This is the most important lesson.

Do not let the model directly touch your computer.

Instead:

```text
model asks for action
  -> backend checks permission
  -> backend previews action
  -> user approves
  -> backend executes
  -> audit log records it
```

That pattern applies to file writes, folder moves, shell commands, Docker control, web search, RAG indexing, and Graphify.

**6. Add Workspaces**
A workspace is just an approved folder Rasputin is allowed to see.

The safe version is:

```text
approved roots only
no path traversal
read-only by default
write permission separate
organize/move requires preview
```

That way the model cannot randomly inspect your whole machine.

**7. Add RAG**
RAG means:

```text
files -> chunks -> embeddings -> vector index -> search results -> model context
```

The model does not magically know your files. Rasputin reads approved files, chunks them, embeds them locally, stores the index locally, then retrieves relevant chunks when you ask questions.

**8. Add Graphify**
Graphify is a second knowledge layer.

RAG finds “similar text.”
Graphify finds “relationships.”

Example:

```text
file A imports file B
function X calls function Y
document mentions project Z
folder contains related notes
```

So the agent can answer not only “what text is relevant?” but “how are these things connected?”

**9. Add Docker Last**
Docker makes the wrapper portable.

The normal command is:

```powershell
docker compose up -d --build
```

Then open:

```text
http://127.0.0.1:8787
```

The wrapper should start only when you start the container. No browser auto-open is needed.

**10. Test Constantly**
For this project, your main test command is:

```powershell
powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File .\scripts\test.ps1 -Ui
```

That checks backend routes, live task creation, and UI behavior.

The core learning order I’d recommend:

1. FastAPI health endpoint
2. Static frontend serving
3. React chat composer
4. Background task manager
5. vLLM model call
6. Model discovery
7. Workspace approval
8. File read tool
9. Audit log
10. RAG indexing
11. Graphify
12. Docker distribution

If you understand those twelve pieces, you understand the bones of Rasputin.

> would it be a reasonable line of thought to add the complete functionality of openclaw/hermes to our wrapper? i assume those run using a model that can be turned into an open source model, i would like to build one big wrapper for all of my needs if possible.

<details><summary>1 previous message</summary>

> <details><summary>Searched web 3 times</summary>
>
> <details><summary>Searched web 3 times</summary>
>
> - Searched web for `OpenClaw agent architecture GitHub`
> - Searched web for `https://openclawdoc.com/docs/agents/overview/`
> - Searched web for `'License' in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw`
>
> </details>
>
> </details>
</details>

Yes, that is a reasonable direction, but I would phrase it like this:

**Do not add OpenClaw/Hermes as “one giant feature.” Build Rasputin into an agent control plane that can reproduce the parts of OpenClaw/Hermes you actually want.**

The important correction: **OpenClaw and Hermes are not models.** They are agent runtimes. They sit *around* a model.

```text
Open-source model
  -> vLLM / llama.cpp / Ollama / LM Studio
  -> Rasputin model router
  -> agent runtime
  -> tools, files, memory, skills, browser/search, UI
```

So you do not “turn OpenClaw into an open-source model.” Instead, you run an open-source model, then give it OpenClaw/Hermes-style infrastructure.

OpenClaw’s own docs describe agents as having model, memory, tool, and channel layers, with planning, tool use, persistent memory, and workflows layered around the model. Its runtime docs also mention session persistence, skills, model selection, context compaction, and tool policy as runtime concerns, not model weights. Sources: [OpenClaw agents overview](https://openclawdoc.com/docs/agents/overview/), [OpenClaw runtime architecture](https://docs.openclaw.ai/agent-runtime-architecture).

Hermes is similar, but more memory/learning focused. A recent writeup describes Hermes as using persistent memory files, reusable `SKILL.md` workflows, periodic self-review, context compression, and SQLite full-text session recall. Source: [Better Stack Hermes Agent guide](https://betterstack.com/community/guides/ai/hermes-agent/).

**What Rasputin Should Borrow**
The best pieces to bring into Rasputin are:

1. **OpenClaw-style runtime**
   - task sessions
   - tool calling
   - sub-agents
   - scheduler
   - model routing
   - long-running tasks
   - workspace-scoped tools

2. **Hermes-style memory**
   - `user.md` for your stable preferences
   - `memory.md` for project facts
   - SQLite session archive
   - RAG/vector recall
   - “should I save this?” memory review
   - reusable skills generated from successful workflows

3. **Rasputin-specific safety**
   - models never get direct internet
   - models never get raw computer access
   - all tools go through Rasputin’s permission layer
   - file operations are previewed before mutation
   - web search is brokered and sanitized
   - everything sensitive is audited

That last part is where Rasputin should be different. OpenClaw/Hermes-style power is useful, but Rasputin’s identity should be: **local-first, privacy-locked, GUI-controlled, explicit workspaces, safe tool broker.**

**Would One Big Wrapper Make Sense?**
Yes, as long as “one big wrapper” means:

```text
one UI
one local backend
one model registry
one permission system
many modular capabilities
```

It should not mean one enormous tangled program where chat, tools, memory, files, Docker, browser control, RAG, and Graphify all blur together. That becomes impossible to secure.

The right architecture is:

```text
Rasputin UI
  -> Agent Runtime
  -> Model Router
  -> Permission Kernel
  -> Tool Broker
      -> Files
      -> RAG
      -> Graphify
      -> Web Search
      -> Code Runner
      -> Docker Model Manager
  -> Memory System
  -> Audit Log
```

**My recommendation:** yes, make Rasputin your all-purpose local AI workbench. But build it as an OpenClaw/Hermes-inspired system, not a clone. First add the agent runtime, then memory, then skills, then scheduler/sub-agents, then heavier automation. The bones we already built can support that.

> i agree, please build out a plan to do this

<details><summary>8 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll ground this in the actual Rasputin code first, then I’ll turn the OpenClaw/Hermes idea into a concrete build plan instead of a vague “add agents” blob. I’m going to inspect the current backend/frontend/docs shape and then lock the remaining product choices.
>
> Rasputin already has the early versions of the pieces we need: task manager, model registry, memory, RAG, Graphify, audit, skills, approvals, and a React UI. I’m checking the backend APIs and current agent/memory/tools now so the plan extends what exists instead of inventing a parallel system.
>
> The current agent loop is already Plan → Execute → Reflect, but it is still thin: memory is a small JSON file, sub-agents are just spawned task variants, tool use is manual/backend-driven, and there isn’t yet a durable session/skill runtime like Hermes or OpenClaw. That gives us a clean upgrade path rather than a rewrite.
>
> A few plan-critical facts from the repo: file mutation previews exist but are not a full approval queue yet; skills are Python modules rather than persisted `SKILL.md` packages; web search is brokered through the MCP layer; and safety flags already enforce local model endpoints and disabled Docker control.
>
> Workspace, model routing, RAG, and Graphify are all file-backed JSON implementations today. That is good for v0 stability, but the plan should include a durable SQLite-backed agent/session/memory layer before we pile on true Hermes-style behavior.
>
> Before I finalize the plan, there are three product-level choices that materially change the build. The repo answers the technical shape; these decide the first production milestone and safety posture.
>
> Got it: Rasputin-native runtime, Hermes-style memory first, and a mostly autonomous agent that escalates risky approvals to you through Telegram. I’m checking whether the repo already has any messaging/approval transport so the plan can specify whether Telegram is new infrastructure or a thin extension.
>
> Plan
>
> # Rasputin Agent Runtime Plan
>
> ## Summary
>
> Build Rasputin into a Rasputin-native OpenClaw/Hermes-style local agent workbench, not a clone or dependency. The first production target is a safe autonomous runtime with durable memory, skill creation, sub-agents, tool planning, and Telegram approval for risky actions.
>
> Chosen defaults:
> - Runtime strategy: Rasputin-native agent runtime.
> - First priority: Hermes-style memory and session recall.
> - Autonomy: mostly autonomous, with mutations and web search approval-gated.
> - Telegram: outbound Bot API polling only, no public webhook.
> - Telegram content: redacted approval summaries only.
>
> ## Key Changes
>
> ### Agent Runtime
>
> - Replace the thin in-memory `AgentHub` runtime with a durable SQLite-backed runtime in `data/rasputin.db`.
> - Add tables for sessions, messages, tasks, task events, tool calls, approvals, memory items, skills, schedules, artifacts, and agent traces.
> - Keep existing `/api/tasks` behavior compatible, but internally route tasks through a new runtime pipeline:
>   - intake
>   - context assembly
>   - planning
>   - tool planning
>   - approval check
>   - execution
>   - reflection
>   - memory review
>   - artifact write
> - Each task gets a persistent session id, message history, selected model role, active workspace, permission profile, retrieved context, tool trace, and final artifacts.
> - Add task pause/resume in addition to existing cancel.
> - Add sub-agent support with isolated context, own model role, own workspace scope, own logs, and parent/child task linking.
> - Add a context compactor that summarizes older messages and tool outputs once a session grows past a configurable context threshold.
>
> ### Hermes-Style Memory
>
> - Replace `data/memory.json` as the main memory store with SQLite-backed memory plus exported Markdown files:
>   - `data/memory/user.md`
>   - `data/memory/memory.md`
>   - `data/memory/projects/<workspaceId>.md`
> - On first boot, import existing `memory.json` into SQLite and keep a backup.
> - Add memory item types:
>   - preference
>   - fact
>   - project note
>   - workflow lesson
>   - tool lesson
>   - blocked pattern
> - Add memory review after completed tasks:
>   - explicit user preferences can be saved automatically
>   - inferred or sensitive facts become pending memory suggestions
>   - all memory writes are local only and audited
> - Add full-text recall using SQLite FTS5 over sessions, messages, artifacts, and memory items.
> - Agent context assembly should combine:
>   - active session summary
>   - relevant memory
>   - workspace RAG hits
>   - Graphify relationships
>   - recent task history
>   - active skill instructions
>
> ### Skill System
>
> - Add a real skill package format under `data/skills/<skillName>/SKILL.md`.
> - Each skill has metadata:
>   - name
>   - description
>   - allowed task modes
>   - allowed tools
>   - default model role
>   - required permissions
>   - workspace constraints
> - Existing Python skills become built-in skills with matching `SKILL.md` descriptors.
> - Add APIs:
>   - `GET /api/skills`
>   - `GET /api/skills/{name}`
>   - `POST /api/skills/create-from-session`
>   - `POST /api/skills/import`
>   - `POST /api/skills/{name}/enable`
>   - `POST /api/skills/{name}/disable`
> - Add a “Save as Skill” flow from successful sessions.
> - Generated skills must be previewed before being saved.
>
> ### Tool Broker And Approvals
>
> - Convert the current MCP layer into a typed tool broker with schemas, risk levels, permission flags, approval requirements, and audit hooks.
> - Tool risk levels:
>   - safe: local context assembly, memory read, RAG search, Graphify search
>   - guarded: RAG ingest, Graphify build, workspace browse, model health test
>   - approval required: file write, file move, folder reorganize, shell, Docker, model registry edits, web search
> - Add persistent approval queue:
>   - `pending`
>   - `approved`
>   - `denied`
>   - `expired`
>   - `executed`
> - Add APIs:
>   - `GET /api/approvals`
>   - `POST /api/approvals/{id}/approve`
>   - `POST /api/approvals/{id}/deny`
>   - `POST /api/approvals/{id}/expire`
> - Approval records include action type, risk level, workspace, shortened paths, model, requesting task, expiration, and redacted summary.
> - Approval records never include file contents, diffs, prompts, raw model output, secrets, or full document text.
> - Approved tool calls must use one-time approval ids with TTL and replay protection.
>
> ### Telegram Approval Integration
>
> - Add Telegram as an optional local integration using outbound Bot API polling.
> - No webhook and no public port exposure.
> - Store Telegram config in local ignored data:
>   - bot token
>   - allowed chat id
>   - enabled flag
>   - redaction mode
> - Add APIs:
>   - `GET /api/integrations/telegram`
>   - `POST /api/integrations/telegram/configure`
>   - `POST /api/integrations/telegram/test`
>   - `POST /api/integrations/telegram/disable`
> - Telegram messages contain only:
>   - approval code
>   - task title
>   - action type
>   - risk level
>   - workspace display name
>   - shortened affected paths
>   - approve/deny instructions
> - Telegram commands:
>   - `/approve <code>`
>   - `/deny <code>`
>   - `/status`
> - Telegram approvals must verify chat id, approval code, expiration, and task state before executing anything.
> - If Telegram is unavailable, approvals remain pending in the Rasputin UI.
>
> ### Model Routing And Orchestration
>
> - Extend model roles:
>   - main
>   - planner
>   - executor
>   - coder
>   - researcher
>   - summarizer
>   - memory
>   - embeddings
> - Default role fallback:
>   - missing specialized role falls back to main
>   - unhealthy model blocks real execution unless dry-run/testing mode is enabled
> - Agent runtime chooses model role by phase:
>   - planner for plan/tool plan
>   - executor/coder/researcher for work
>   - summarizer for reflection and compaction
>   - memory for memory review
> - Keep local-only endpoint enforcement.
> - Keep direct internet blocked from models.
>
> ### UI
>
> - Add first-class views:
>   - Agents
>   - Sessions
>   - Memory
>   - Skills
>   - Approvals
>   - Telegram
>   - Schedules
> - Home remains chat-first.
> - Task details show:
>   - plan
>   - tool calls
>   - pending approvals
>   - what the agent saw
>   - memory used
>   - artifacts
>   - sub-agent tree
> - Add approval cards in UI with approve/deny buttons.
> - Add Telegram setup screen with bot token entry, chat id verification, test message, and privacy warning.
> - Add memory review screen for pending memory suggestions.
> - Add skill editor/viewer for `SKILL.md`.
>
> ## Public Interfaces
>
> - Keep existing structured response shape:
>   - `{ "ok": true, "data": {}, "error": null }`
>   - `{ "ok": false, "data": null, "error": { "code": "...", "message": "..." } }`
> - Keep `/api/tasks`, `/api/events`, `/api/models`, `/api/workspace`, `/api/rag`, `/api/graph`, `/api/audit`, and `/api/security` backward-compatible.
> - Add:
>   - `/api/sessions`
>   - `/api/sessions/{id}`
>   - `/api/approvals`
>   - `/api/memory/review`
>   - `/api/memory/search`
>   - `/api/skills/create-from-session`
>   - `/api/integrations/telegram/*`
>   - `/api/schedules`
> - SSE events should include task updates, approval updates, memory suggestions, Telegram status, and sub-agent updates.
>
> ## Test Plan
>
> - Backend:
>   - SQLite initializes on first boot.
>   - Existing `memory.json` imports once and backs up safely.
>   - Existing `/api/tasks` dry-run flow still works.
>   - task pause/resume/cancel persist across process restart.
>   - sub-agents get isolated context and parent links.
>   - context compaction preserves recent messages and summaries.
> - Memory:
>   - explicit user preference is saved.
>   - inferred sensitive memory becomes pending review.
>   - FTS recall finds prior sessions.
>   - memory used in a task appears in “what did the agent see?”
> - Approvals:
>   - file write, move, shell, Docker, model registry edit, and web search create pending approvals.
>   - safe local reads/RAG/Graphify search run without approval.
>   - approval ids expire and cannot be replayed.
>   - denied approvals do not execute.
> - Telegram:
>   - bot polling runs only when enabled.
>   - wrong chat id is rejected.
>   - `/approve <code>` approves only the matching pending action.
>   - `/deny <code>` blocks execution.
>   - Telegram message contains no file contents, diffs, prompts, or raw output.
>   - offline Telegram failure leaves UI approval usable.
> - Safety:
>   - models cannot call internet directly.
>   - web search goes only through broker and approval.
>   - path traversal outside approved workspaces fails.
>   - redaction blocks secrets and long pasted text from Telegram and web queries.
> - UI:
>   - approval queue updates live.
>   - Telegram setup/test works.
>   - memory review works.
>   - skill save-from-session works.
>   - sub-agent tree renders.
>   - task trace shows retrieved memory, RAG, graph, and tool calls.
> - Harness:
>   - update `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui` to cover sessions, approvals, memory, skills, and Telegram with mocked Bot API.
>
> ## Assumptions
>
> - Rasputin will not vendor or depend on OpenClaw/Hermes internals.
> - Rasputin will reproduce the useful architecture patterns: durable sessions, memory, skills, tool planning, sub-agents, scheduling, context compaction, and model routing.
> - Telegram approval messages intentionally leave the machine, but only redacted metadata is sent.
> - No file contents, prompts, model outputs, diffs, secrets, or private document excerpts are sent to Telegram.
> - Browser automation and external channel integrations beyond Telegram approval are future phases.
> - Docker control remains disabled by default.
> - Shell execution remains disabled by default.
> - Web search requires approval in the mostly autonomous mode.
> - Local read/RAG/Graphify over approved workspaces can run autonomously.
</details>

Telegram approval changes the security model a little because approval messages themselves leave the machine. To keep Rasputin’s privacy promise, I need to lock the Telegram design before writing the final plan.

<details><summary>97 previous messages</summary>

> I’m going to implement this as a production slice rather than a rewrite grenade: durable SQLite runtime first, then memory/skills/approvals/Telegram APIs, then UI surfaces and tests. I’ll keep the existing API behavior alive while layering the new runtime under it.
>
> I’m starting with a narrow interface pass so I don’t break the working chat and settings flows while adding the bigger agent machinery. The key is preserving the old `/api/tasks` contract while adding richer session/runtime state underneath.
>
> I’m adding the SQLite core as a small runtime store module first. That gives every higher-level feature a shared durable place to land without making each feature invent its own JSON file yet again.
>
> Next I’m replacing the “memory is just one JSON blob” foundation with a compatibility layer: old calls like `load_memory()` still work, but the source of truth becomes SQLite plus local Markdown exports.

Implemented the Rasputin-native agent runtime plan and restarted the localhost wrapper.

Rasputin is running here:

[http://127.0.0.1:8787](http://127.0.0.1:8787)

What changed:
- Added SQLite runtime storage at `data/rasputin.db`.
- Added durable sessions, messages, tasks, task events, tool calls, approvals, memory items, skills, schedules, artifacts, and traces.
- Added pause/resume task support.
- Added Hermes-style memory with review queue, SQLite search, and Markdown exports.
- Added `SKILL.md` skill registry and session-to-skill previews.
- Added persistent approval queue for risky actions.
- Added optional Telegram approval integration using outbound polling only.
- Added approval wait/resume behavior for agent-owned risky tool calls.
- Added new UI views: Agents, Sessions, Approvals, Memory, Skills, Telegram, Schedules.
- Updated docs here: [RASPUTIN_ARCHITECTURE_GUIDE.md](./docs/RASPUTIN_ARCHITECTURE_GUIDE.md).

Verification passed:
- Python compile check
- `npm run build`
- `git diff --check`
- `.\scripts\test.ps1`
- `.\scripts\test.ps1 -Ui`
- 17 backend smoke tests passed
- 5 Playwright UI tests passed
- Docker container is healthy on `127.0.0.1:8787`

One note: the build still prints Bootstrap Sass deprecation warnings. They are upstream warnings from Bootstrap SCSS, not app failures.
