Implemented the pass.

What changed:
- Added a real top-level `Workspaces` screen with approved folders, active workspace, GUI folder browsing, folder selection, and advanced mount preview.
- Added concurrent/agentic controls on Home: task mode, helper-agent count, parallel run status, and per-task pause/resume/stop controls.
- Rebuilt `Activity` into an operations view for active runs, helper agents, approvals, sessions, pipeline, and audit.
- Improved accessibility basics: skip target focus, labeled regions, keyboard tab controls, visible focus, aria-live status areas, named task controls.
- Killed the worst Bootstrap-white-box look by overriding Bootstrap surfaces and moving key views to Rasputin-native panels.
- Updated preferences so `workspaces`, `activity`, `taskMode`, and `subagents` persist correctly.
- Rebuilt and restarted the Docker wrapper.

Live app is running here:

[http://127.0.0.1:8787](http://127.0.0.1:8787)

Verification:
- `npm.cmd run build` passed.
- `powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File .\scripts\test.ps1 -Ui` passed.
- Backend smoke: `17 tests OK`.
- Playwright UI: `5 passed`.
- `git diff --check` passed, with only existing CRLF warnings.
- Docker container is healthy and serving the new bundle.

One note: Bootstrap still prints Sass deprecation warnings during build. They are noisy but not breaking anything.

> what are the primary functional differences between our product and other products that exact right now

<details><summary>2 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll ground this against current public docs/repos rather than guessing from memory, then I’ll separate “what Rasputin already does” from “what we’re aiming for.”
>
> <details><summary>Searched web 2 times</summary>
>
> <details><summary>Searched web 2 times</summary>
>
> - Searched web for `Open WebUI features local models RAG OpenAI API Ollama docs`
> - Searched web for `AnythingLLM official docs features agents workspaces RAG local models`
>
> </details>
>
> </details>
</details>

Assuming you mean “products that exist right now”: Rasputin is not just trying to be another local ChatGPT clone. The main difference is that Rasputin is shaping into a **safe local computer workbench**, while most existing tools are mainly **chat/document/model interfaces**.

**Big Difference**
Rasputin’s core idea is:

> “Give the AI access only to selected local folders, let it work there, keep data local, route internet through guarded tools, and make agent actions visible/approvable.”

That is different from most tools, where the center of gravity is chat, RAG, model management, or coding automation.

**Compared To Open WebUI**
Open WebUI is more mature as a general AI interface. It already supports many providers, Ollama/OpenAI-compatible backends, multi-model chats, RAG, file browser features, tools, pipelines, MCP, and OpenAPI extensions.
Source: [Open WebUI features](https://docs.openwebui.com/features)

Rasputin’s difference:
- More opinionated about **local file safety**.
- More focused on **approved folders/workspaces** as the boundary.
- More focused on **models not having internet access directly**.
- More focused on **agent traces, approvals, and local task work**, not just chat.

Open WebUI is ahead on polish and ecosystem. Rasputin is aiming to be more locked-down and computer-workflow-specific.

**Compared To AnythingLLM**
AnythingLLM is very strong at workspace-based RAG, document ingestion, local/cloud model connections, agents, and “chat with your documents.”
Sources: [AnythingLLM docs](https://docs.anythingllm.com/), [AnythingLLM site](https://anythingllm.com/)

Rasputin’s difference:
- AnythingLLM treats workspaces mostly as document/chat knowledge containers.
- Rasputin treats workspaces as **permission-scoped folders the agent may inspect and eventually reorganize**.
- Rasputin is trying to separate:
  - file read
  - file write
  - folder reorganization
  - web access
  - Docker/model control
  - shell execution

So Rasputin is less “document QA first” and more “safe local agent operating layer.”

**Compared To LibreChat**
LibreChat is a mature multi-provider chat platform with agents, MCP, code execution, multimodal chat, and configurable MCP permissions.
Sources: [LibreChat features](https://www.librechat.ai/docs/features), [LibreChat MCP docs](https://www.librechat.ai/docs/features/mcp)

Rasputin’s difference:
- LibreChat is closer to a self-hosted ChatGPT-style platform.
- Rasputin is more specifically aimed at **your local machine, your local folders, your local models, your local audit trail**.
- Rasputin’s “privacy lock” and approval model are meant to be central product mechanics, not optional enterprise config.

LibreChat is broader and more mature. Rasputin is narrower, more paranoid, and more hardware/local-workflow oriented.

**Compared To LM Studio / Jan**
LM Studio and Jan are mostly about running local models, chatting, exposing local OpenAI-compatible APIs, and managing model runtimes.
Sources: [LM Studio offline docs](https://www.lmstudio.ai/docs/app/offline), [Jan docs](https://docs.jan.ai/)

Rasputin’s difference:
- LM Studio/Jan are model runners and local chat apps.
- Rasputin is not trying to replace them entirely.
- Rasputin wants to sit **above** model runners:
  - vLLM for the big main model
  - llama.cpp/GGUF helpers
  - model registry
  - model role routing
  - task orchestration

So Rasputin is more of a **local AI control plane** than a model app.

**Compared To OpenHands / Coding Agents**
OpenHands is focused on autonomous software development agents: coding, repos, command execution, browser/tool use, and agent SDKs.
Source: [OpenHands SDK docs](https://docs.openhands.dev/sdk/index)

Rasputin’s difference:
- OpenHands is coding-agent-first.
- Rasputin is intended to become coding-capable, but also handle:
  - folder organization
  - local knowledge search
  - writing
  - research
  - file companion workflows
  - model management
  - memory
  - approvals

Rasputin wants to be a hybrid: part Open WebUI, part AnythingLLM, part OpenHands, part local safety broker.

**Where Rasputin Is Strongest Conceptually**
The unique lane is:

1. **Explicit folder access**
   Rasputin should only see folders you approve.

2. **Local-first privacy**
   File contents should stay local.

3. **No direct model internet**
   Internet access should only happen through a broker/tool layer.

4. **Approval-gated risky actions**
   File writes, moves, shell commands, Docker control, and web search should require approval.

5. **Concurrent agent work**
   Multiple tasks and helper agents can run at once.

6. **Local model role routing**
   One big vLLM model plus smaller llama.cpp/GGUF helper models.

7. **Workspace-aware RAG + Graphify**
   Not just “chat with docs,” but “understand this folder/repo/project structure.”

**Where Existing Products Are Ahead**
Right now, competitors are ahead on:
- UI polish
- model installation flows
- document ingestion
- plugin ecosystems
- multi-user/admin features
- mobile friendliness
- production hardening
- community testing

Rasputin is still early. The pitch is not “we already beat them.” The pitch is:

> Rasputin is a privacy-locked local AI workbench built around approved folders, local models, auditable agent actions, and safe autonomy. Existing tools mostly start from chat, docs, or models; Rasputin starts from controlled access to your computer.

> okay, so what should we work on polishing next

Next, I’d polish the **core task loop**, not add more features.

Right now Rasputin’s biggest product risk is: the UI is becoming more serious, but the actual “AI does useful work” flow still needs to feel trustworthy and obvious.

I’d do this order:

1. **Task Details View**
   Make every run clickable and show:
   - what the model was asked
   - selected workspace
   - model used
   - files/RAG context seen
   - tool calls attempted
   - approvals needed
   - logs
   - final answer/artifacts

2. **Workspace Browser Polish**
   This is core to Rasputin’s identity. Make it feel excellent:
   - breadcrumbs that actually navigate
   - active workspace selection is obvious
   - read-only/read-write state is clear
   - folder cards look less test-harness-ish
   - “Add folder” flow explains Docker visibility cleanly

3. **Model Health / Selection**
   Simplify this hard:
   - show only “Main Local Model” on Home
   - make “Discover/Test/Repair” a guided flow
   - hide raw model registry unless Advanced is opened
   - explain when vLLM model mismatch happens in plain English

4. **Agentic Concurrency UX**
   Since you said agentic AI is a must:
   - show active runs in a right-side drawer or Activity panel
   - make helper agents visible as child runs
   - show pause/resume/stop reliably
   - make progress meaningful instead of fake percentage vibes

5. **Approval Queue**
   This is one of Rasputin’s differentiators:
   - beautiful approval cards
   - clear risk labels
   - show redacted summaries
   - one-click approve/deny
   - explain what will happen before mutation

6. **Visual QA / Accessibility Pass**
   After the functional pieces above:
   - kill remaining Bootstrap-looking surfaces
   - improve keyboard flow
   - check contrast
   - fix mobile layout
   - make the app feel like one product, not stitched panels

My recommendation: **Task Details View first.** That gives Rasputin trust. If the AI is going to act agentically, the user needs to see what happened, why it happened, and what it touched.

> build this plan making sure to go extremely in depth about what needs to happen, we dont want to completely redo what we have, we want to add to it or make it better

<details><summary>3 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll turn this into an implementation-ready plan, but first I’m going to ground it in the actual Rasputin code we have now: current task APIs, Activity/Home components, workspace state, and tests. I’ll keep this inspection read-only so we’re planning, not executing.
>
> Context compaction
>
> Source: automatic
> Status: completed
>
> Plan
>
> # Rasputin Task Trust And Operations Polish Plan
>
> ## Summary
> We will improve what Rasputin already has instead of rebuilding it. The next production step is a “trust and operations” layer: a real task details experience, better concurrent-task visibility, cleaner workspace browsing, clearer model health, and stronger accessibility.
>
> The current backend already stores durable task/session/event/artifact/trace data, and the frontend already has Home, Workspaces, Activity, Knowledge, Models, and Settings. This plan adds missing connective tissue so Rasputin feels like a usable local agent workbench rather than a prototype dashboard.
>
> ## Key Changes
>
> ### 1. Add A Task Detail API
> Add one backend route without changing the existing task list behavior:
>
> `GET /api/tasks/{taskId}`
>
> It returns a full task detail object using the existing structured response shape:
>
> ```json
> {
>   "ok": true,
>   "data": {
>     "task": {},
>     "session": {},
>     "events": [],
>     "trace": [],
>     "artifacts": [],
>     "children": [],
>     "approvals": [],
>     "toolCalls": []
>   },
>   "error": null
> }
> ```
>
> Implementation details:
> - Keep `GET /api/tasks` unchanged for lightweight lists.
> - Reuse existing SQLite runtime data from `tasks`, `task_events`, `artifacts`, `agent_traces`, `tool_calls`, and approvals.
> - Merge live in-memory task state with persisted records when the task is still running.
> - Use camelCase output through the existing response helpers.
> - Return a clean structured `404` if the task does not exist.
> - Do not expose new raw file contents, secrets, prompts, or private diffs through this endpoint.
> - Include child/sub-agent tasks by `parentId` so concurrent agent trees can be inspected.
>
> ### 2. Build A Task Details Drawer
> Add a focused task detail drawer/panel in the React frontend.
>
> Open it from:
> - Home assistant response details.
> - Activity task cards.
> - Sub-agent rows.
> - Approval cards when they reference a task.
>
> Drawer behavior:
> - Desktop: right-side drawer.
> - Mobile: full-screen panel.
> - Escape closes it.
> - Close button has an accessible name.
> - Focus moves into the drawer when opened and returns to the opener when closed.
> - The drawer updates while the selected task is running.
>
> Task detail sections:
> - **Overview**: title/objective, status, progress, task mode, model, workspace, session, created time.
> - **What Rasputin Saw**: workspace scope, memory references, RAG sources, Graphify relationships, selected model role.
> - **Plan And Trace**: intake, planning, execution, reflection, memory review, artifact write, sub-agent spawning.
> - **Logs**: live task logs with timestamps and status markers.
> - **Artifacts**: generated markdown, summaries, exported files, result text, copy/export actions where already supported.
> - **Sub-Agents**: child tasks with status, progress, model role, and open-detail action.
> - **Approvals And Tools**: approval status, risk level, tool call summaries, denied/approved/expired states.
>
> The Home screen stays clean. It should only show the conversation and collapsed response details, with the full operational story living in the drawer.
>
> ### 3. Polish Concurrent Agent Visibility
> Improve the Activity view around multiple running tasks.
>
> Changes:
> - Keep Activity as the place for runs, logs, approvals, sessions, pipeline, and audit.
> - Show running tasks in a clear list or lane layout.
> - Parent tasks should visually group their sub-agents.
> - Every task card gets a clear `Details` action.
> - Keep pause/resume/stop controls on task cards and in the detail drawer.
> - Use actual task status/progress only. Do not fake progress or add decorative status text.
> - SSE updates should refresh the task list and update the open detail drawer.
>
> Acceptance criteria:
> - Two tasks can run at once and appear independently.
> - A parent task can show child helper agents.
> - The user can pause, resume, stop, and inspect each task without losing context.
>
> ### 4. Make Workspaces Make Sense
> Improve the existing Workspaces page rather than replacing it.
>
> Changes:
> - Keep the approved-folder model.
> - Make the page explain the real Docker constraint: Rasputin can only browse folders mounted into the container or explicitly approved visible roots.
> - Approved folders show friendly names, not raw `.` paths.
> - Folder cards show:
>   - display name
>   - path
>   - read-only/read-write state
>   - active/inactive state
>   - indexed/not indexed state if available
> - Folder browser gets:
>   - clickable breadcrumbs
>   - current folder header
>   - child folder list
>   - search/filter
>   - “Use this folder” action
>   - clear empty/error states
> - Mount preview remains an advanced/admin workflow.
> - Manual path entry stays hidden under Advanced.
>
> No native OS folder picker in this pass, because the app runs inside Docker and cannot safely access arbitrary host folders unless they are mounted.
>
> ### 5. Simplify Model Health And Selection
> Keep the Home model UI simple and move details into Models.
>
> Home should show:
> - Active chat model.
> - Health state.
> - Short disabled-send reason if unhealthy.
> - Refresh/test action.
>
> Models page should show:
> - Main Local Model as the primary card.
> - Discover vLLM action.
> - Test health action.
> - Repair mismatch action when vLLM exposes one valid model and Rasputin is pointed at the wrong ID.
> - Plain-English error text for `404` model mismatch.
> - Advanced registry hidden behind a disclosure.
>
> Hide from the normal path:
> - `dry-run`
> - embeddings model
> - raw keys
> - provider internals
> - tags
> - endpoint details
> - implementation role names unless the user expands Advanced.
>
> ### 6. Clean Up Approvals
> Improve the current approvals UI so risky agent behavior is understandable.
>
> Approval cards should show:
> - Action type.
> - Risk level.
> - Requesting task.
> - Workspace.
> - Short affected paths.
> - Redacted summary.
> - Status: pending, approved, denied, expired, executed.
> - Expiration time.
> - Approve/Deny buttons only when pending.
>
> Approval cards must not show:
> - file contents
> - raw prompts
> - raw model output
> - secrets
> - full private document text
> - full diffs
>
> Task Details should include related approvals so the user can see why an agent paused.
>
> ### 7. Accessibility Repair
> Add accessibility fixes while touching the UI.
>
> Required behavior:
> - Visible focus rings on all interactive controls.
> - Keyboard navigation through sidebar, Activity tabs, task cards, drawer tabs, workspace browser, and composer.
> - `aria-live` regions for task status, errors, and send-disabled reasons.
> - Icon buttons must have accessible names.
> - Drawer close and task controls must be reachable by keyboard.
> - No hidden status text using zero-size hacks.
> - No random punctuation labels like `.`, `Â·`, or raw `workspace: .`.
> - Contrast must pass for the default dark Warmind theme and light mode.
>
> ### 8. Visual Cleanup
> Continue moving Rasputin away from the white Bootstrap-card look without a full visual rewrite.
>
> Changes:
> - Replace the worst white boxes in Home, Activity, Workspaces, and Models with Rasputin shell surfaces.
> - Keep Bootstrap only where legacy form behavior is useful.
> - Use consistent graphite surfaces, restrained borders, amber-red accent, and compact technical spacing.
> - Remove dashboard-style clutter from Home.
> - Keep empty states short and useful.
> - Avoid novelty labels and fake AI-marketing copy.
>
> ## Public Interfaces
>
> Add:
> - `GET /api/tasks/{taskId}`
>
> Preserve:
> - `GET /api/tasks`
> - `POST /api/tasks`
> - `POST /api/tasks/{taskId}/pause`
> - `POST /api/tasks/{taskId}/resume`
> - `POST /api/tasks/{taskId}/cancel`
> - existing workspace, model, approval, session, audit, RAG, and graph endpoints.
>
> Frontend test ids to add or preserve:
> - `task-details-drawer`
> - `task-details-close`
> - `task-details-overview`
> - `task-details-trace`
> - `task-details-logs`
> - `task-details-artifacts`
> - `task-details-subagents`
> - `task-details-approvals`
> - `activity-task-card`
> - `activity-task-details`
> - `workspace-browser`
> - `workspace-folder-card`
> - `active-model-chip`
> - `active-workspace-chip`
>
> ## Test Plan
>
> Backend tests:
> - `GET /api/tasks/{taskId}` returns task, events, trace, artifacts, children, approvals, and tool calls.
> - Unknown task ID returns structured `404`.
> - Running task detail includes live state.
> - Parent task detail includes child/sub-agent tasks.
> - Existing `/api/tasks` response stays backward-compatible.
> - Approval data is redacted.
>
> Frontend tests:
> - Home loads without task/debug clutter.
> - Activity opens task detail drawer.
> - Home response details can open full task detail.
> - Drawer tabs/sections render overview, trace, logs, artifacts, sub-agents, and approvals.
> - Pause/resume/stop controls update visible task state.
> - Two concurrent tasks appear independently.
> - Workspace browser shows friendly folder names and no raw `.` labels.
> - Model card hides advanced registry details by default.
> - Approval cards show approve/deny only for pending approvals.
> - Mobile task detail panel is usable.
>
> Accessibility tests:
> - Keyboard can open and close the task detail drawer.
> - Focus returns to the opener after close.
> - Sidebar, composer, Activity tabs, Workspace browser, and drawer controls are keyboard reachable.
> - Screen-reader status regions announce task/error updates.
> - No icon-only button lacks an accessible name.
>
> Validation commands:
> - `npm.cmd run build`
> - `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui`
> - `git diff --check`
> - Playwright screenshots for Home, Activity, Task Details, Workspaces, Models, mobile Home, and dark theme.
>
> ## Assumptions
> - Keep the existing FastAPI backend, SQLite runtime, React/Vite frontend, and Docker workflow.
> - Do not rewrite the shell from scratch.
> - Do not change model runtime architecture in this pass.
> - Do not add native host folder picking while Rasputin runs in Docker.
> - Do not expose more private data through task detail APIs than Rasputin already stores.
> - Prioritize task trust, workspace clarity, and concurrent-agent usability before deeper visual redesign.
</details>

> PLEASE IMPLEMENT THIS PLAN:
> # Rasputin Task Trust And Operations Polish Plan
>
> ## Summary
> We will improve what Rasputin already has instead of rebuilding it. The next production step is a “trust and operations” layer: a real task details experience, better concurrent-task visibility, cleaner workspace browsing, clearer model health, and stronger accessibility.
>
> The current backend already stores durable task/session/event/artifact/trace data, and the frontend already has Home, Workspaces, Activity, Knowledge, Models, and Settings. This plan adds missing connective tissue so Rasputin feels like a usable local agent workbench rather than a prototype dashboard.
>
> ## Key Changes
>
> ### 1. Add A Task Detail API
> Add one backend route without changing the existing task list behavior:
>
> `GET /api/tasks/{taskId}`
>
> It returns a full task detail object using the existing structured response shape:
>
> ```json
> {
>   "ok": true,
>   "data": {
>     "task": {},
>     "session": {},
>     "events": [],
>     "trace": [],
>     "artifacts": [],
>     "children": [],
>     "approvals": [],
>     "toolCalls": []
>   },
>   "error": null
> }
> ```
>
> Implementation details:
> - Keep `GET /api/tasks` unchanged for lightweight lists.
> - Reuse existing SQLite runtime data from `tasks`, `task_events`, `artifacts`, `agent_traces`, `tool_calls`, and approvals.
> - Merge live in-memory task state with persisted records when the task is still running.
> - Use camelCase output through the existing response helpers.
> - Return a clean structured `404` if the task does not exist.
> - Do not expose new raw file contents, secrets, prompts, or private diffs through this endpoint.
> - Include child/sub-agent tasks by `parentId` so concurrent agent trees can be inspected.
>
> ### 2. Build A Task Details Drawer
> Add a focused task detail drawer/panel in the React frontend.
>
> Open it from:
> - Home assistant response details.
> - Activity task cards.
> - Sub-agent rows.
> - Approval cards when they reference a task.
>
> Drawer behavior:
> - Desktop: right-side drawer.
> - Mobile: full-screen panel.
> - Escape closes it.
> - Close button has an accessible name.
> - Focus moves into the drawer when opened and returns to the opener when closed.
> - The drawer updates while the selected task is running.
>
> Task detail sections:
> - **Overview**: title/objective, status, progress, task mode, model, workspace, session, created time.
> - **What Rasputin Saw**: workspace scope, memory references, RAG sources, Graphify relationships, selected model role.
> - **Plan And Trace**: intake, planning, execution, reflection, memory review, artifact write, sub-agent spawning.
> - **Logs**: live task logs with timestamps and status markers.
> - **Artifacts**: generated markdown, summaries, exported files, result text, copy/export actions where already supported.
> - **Sub-Agents**: child tasks with status, progress, model role, and open-detail action.
> - **Approvals And Tools**: approval status, risk level, tool call summaries, denied/approved/expired states.
>
> The Home screen stays clean. It should only show the conversation and collapsed response details, with the full operational story living in the drawer.
>
> ### 3. Polish Concurrent Agent Visibility
> Improve the Activity view around multiple running tasks.
>
> Changes:
> - Keep Activity as the place for runs, logs, approvals, sessions, pipeline, and audit.
> - Show running tasks in a clear list or lane layout.
> - Parent tasks should visually group their sub-agents.
> - Every task card gets a clear `Details` action.
> - Keep pause/resume/stop controls on task cards and in the detail drawer.
> - Use actual task status/progress only. Do not fake progress or add decorative status text.
> - SSE updates should refresh the task list and update the open detail drawer.
>
> Acceptance criteria:
> - Two tasks can run at once and appear independently.
> - A parent task can show child helper agents.
> - The user can pause, resume, stop, and inspect each task without losing context.
>
> ### 4. Make Workspaces Make Sense
> Improve the existing Workspaces page rather than replacing it.
>
> Changes:
> - Keep the approved-folder model.
> - Make the page explain the real Docker constraint: Rasputin can only browse folders mounted into the container or explicitly approved visible roots.
> - Approved folders show friendly names, not raw `.` paths.
> - Folder cards show:
>   - display name
>   - path
>   - read-only/read-write state
>   - active/inactive state
>   - indexed/not indexed state if available
> - Folder browser gets:
>   - clickable breadcrumbs
>   - current folder header
>   - child folder list
>   - search/filter
>   - “Use this folder” action
>   - clear empty/error states
> - Mount preview remains an advanced/admin workflow.
> - Manual path entry stays hidden under Advanced.
>
> No native OS folder picker in this pass, because the app runs inside Docker and cannot safely access arbitrary host folders unless they are mounted.
>
> ### 5. Simplify Model Health And Selection
> Keep the Home model UI simple and move details into Models.
>
> Home should show:
> - Active chat model.
> - Health state.
> - Short disabled-send reason if unhealthy.
> - Refresh/test action.
>
> Models page should show:
> - Main Local Model as the primary card.
> - Discover vLLM action.
> - Test health action.
> - Repair mismatch action when vLLM exposes one valid model and Rasputin is pointed at the wrong ID.
> - Plain-English error text for `404` model mismatch.
> - Advanced registry hidden behind a disclosure.
>
> Hide from the normal path:
> - `dry-run`
> - embeddings model
> - raw keys
> - provider internals
> - tags
> - endpoint details
> - implementation role names unless the user expands Advanced.
>
> ### 6. Clean Up Approvals
> Improve the current approvals UI so risky agent behavior is understandable.
>
> Approval cards should show:
> - Action type.
> - Risk level.
> - Requesting task.
> - Workspace.
> - Short affected paths.
> - Redacted summary.
> - Status: pending, approved, denied, expired, executed.
> - Expiration time.
> - Approve/Deny buttons only when pending.
>
> Approval cards must not show:
> - file contents
> - raw prompts
> - raw model output
> - secrets
> - full private document text
> - full diffs
>
> Task Details should include related approvals so the user can see why an agent paused.
>
> ### 7. Accessibility Repair
> Add accessibility fixes while touching the UI.
>
> Required behavior:
> - Visible focus rings on all interactive controls.
> - Keyboard navigation through sidebar, Activity tabs, task cards, drawer tabs, workspace browser, and composer.
> - `aria-live` regions for task status, errors, and send-disabled reasons.
> - Icon buttons must have accessible names.
> - Drawer close and task controls must be reachable by keyboard.
> - No hidden status text using zero-size hacks.
> - No random punctuation labels like `.`, `Â·`, or raw `workspace: .`.
> - Contrast must pass for the default dark Warmind theme and light mode.
>
> ### 8. Visual Cleanup
> Continue moving Rasputin away from the white Bootstrap-card look without a full visual rewrite.
>
> Changes:
> - Replace the worst white boxes in Home, Activity, Workspaces, and Models with Rasputin shell surfaces.
> - Keep Bootstrap only where legacy form behavior is useful.
> - Use consistent graphite surfaces, restrained borders, amber-red accent, and compact technical spacing.
> - Remove dashboard-style clutter from Home.
> - Keep empty states short and useful.
> - Avoid novelty labels and fake AI-marketing copy.
>
> ## Public Interfaces
>
> Add:
> - `GET /api/tasks/{taskId}`
>
> Preserve:
> - `GET /api/tasks`
> - `POST /api/tasks`
> - `POST /api/tasks/{taskId}/pause`
> - `POST /api/tasks/{taskId}/resume`
> - `POST /api/tasks/{taskId}/cancel`
> - existing workspace, model, approval, session, audit, RAG, and graph endpoints.
>
> Frontend test ids to add or preserve:
> - `task-details-drawer`
> - `task-details-close`
> - `task-details-overview`
> - `task-details-trace`
> - `task-details-logs`
> - `task-details-artifacts`
> - `task-details-subagents`
> - `task-details-approvals`
> - `activity-task-card`
> - `activity-task-details`
> - `workspace-browser`
> - `workspace-folder-card`
> - `active-model-chip`
> - `active-workspace-chip`
>
> ## Test Plan
>
> Backend tests:
> - `GET /api/tasks/{taskId}` returns task, events, trace, artifacts, children, approvals, and tool calls.
> - Unknown task ID returns structured `404`.
> - Running task detail includes live state.
> - Parent task detail includes child/sub-agent tasks.
> - Existing `/api/tasks` response stays backward-compatible.
> - Approval data is redacted.
>
> Frontend tests:
> - Home loads without task/debug clutter.
> - Activity opens task detail drawer.
> - Home response details can open full task detail.
> - Drawer tabs/sections render overview, trace, logs, artifacts, sub-agents, and approvals.
> - Pause/resume/stop controls update visible task state.
> - Two concurrent tasks appear independently.
> - Workspace browser shows friendly folder names and no raw `.` labels.
> - Model card hides advanced registry details by default.
> - Approval cards show approve/deny only for pending approvals.
> - Mobile task detail panel is usable.
>
> Accessibility tests:
> - Keyboard can open and close the task detail drawer.
> - Focus returns to the opener after close.
> - Sidebar, composer, Activity tabs, Workspace browser, and drawer controls are keyboard reachable.
> - Screen-reader status regions announce task/error updates.
> - No icon-only button lacks an accessible name.
>
> Validation commands:
> - `npm.cmd run build`
> - `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui`
> - `git diff --check`
> - Playwright screenshots for Home, Activity, Task Details, Workspaces, Models, mobile Home, and dark theme.
>
> ## Assumptions
> - Keep the existing FastAPI backend, SQLite runtime, React/Vite frontend, and Docker workflow.
> - Do not rewrite the shell from scratch.
> - Do not change model runtime architecture in this pass.
> - Do not add native host folder picking while Rasputin runs in Docker.
> - Do not expose more private data through task detail APIs than Rasputin already stores.
> - Prioritize task trust, workspace clarity, and concurrent-agent usability before deeper visual redesign.

Implemented the Rasputin task-trust polish pass.

What changed:
- Added `GET /api/tasks/{taskId}` with full task detail data: task, session, events, trace, artifacts, children, approvals, and tool calls.
- Added a React task details drawer with Overview, What Rasputin Saw, Trace, Logs, Artifacts, Sub-Agents, and Approvals/Tools sections.
- Wired “Details” actions into Home, Activity, helper agents, and approval-linked tasks.
- Improved concurrent task visibility in Activity with explicit task detail access.
- Cleaned up Workspaces with better Docker-access language, folder filtering, friendly folder cards, indexed/read-only state, and clearer breadcrumb behavior.
- Made approval cards more readable and tucked raw redacted metadata behind a disclosure.
- Added drawer/mobile styling so it fits the Rasputin shell instead of the white-box Bootstrap look.
- Updated backend, live, and Playwright smoke tests for the new task detail flow.

Verified:
- `npm.cmd run build` passed.
- `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui` passed:
  - 17 backend tests OK
  - live smoke OK
  - 5 Playwright UI tests passed
- `git diff --check` passed, with only existing Windows LF/CRLF warnings.

One note: the repo already had a large set of pending/untracked Rasputin migration files before this pass, especially under `frontend-src/` and `tests/`. I edited those in place and did not stage or commit anything.

```md
We are building **Rasputin**, a self-hosted localhost AI workbench/wrapper.

Workspace:
`C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject`

Core goal:
Build a safe local AI companion/workbench that can chat, inspect approved folders, run local agentic tasks, use RAG/Graphify, route local models, and eventually support OpenClaw/Hermes-style workflows. The privacy rule is critical: local files must not leave the machine. Models should not get direct internet access. Internet access, if used, should only happen through an approval-gated MCP/web broker.

Current stack:
- Backend: FastAPI + uvicorn
- Runtime/persistence: SQLite in `data/rasputin.db`
- Frontend: React/Vite, Bootstrap still present but visible shell is being moved toward custom Rasputin CSS
- Docker: wrapper runs in Docker; model runtimes are separate
- Main model plan: vLLM for large model, llama.cpp/GGUF for smaller helpers
- UI target: local-first professional Warmind/Rasputin theme, not generic AI dashboard

Current implemented areas:
- Auth and local admin password flow
- Model registry, discovery, testing, repair, GGUF scanning
- Workspace approval system and GUI browsing for mounted/approved folders
- RAG and Graphify prototype systems
- Persistent task/session runtime
- Sub-agent support
- Pause/resume/cancel task controls
- Approval queue
- Telegram approval integration skeleton
- Settings, Activity, Workspaces, Models, Knowledge, and Home views
- React/Vite frontend replacing older vanilla frontend files
```

> I’ll treat “fix the repo” as: stop generated/private files from leaking, make the source tree coherent, and create a clean checkpoint we can build from. I’ll inspect ignore rules and tracked/untracked boundaries first before staging anything.
>

> Let's make a plan to steal features from other existing repos. I want to use opencodes Agentic workflow, a claude style document editor, etc. This should be a jumping off point to go from. I also want to steal part of Pewdiepies Odysseus repos Cookbook. I want to make our docker manager similar to this in that we can pull docker files for different models and run them from Rasputin. i want to call this feature Warsat. The goal of this is to have an AI companion that I can use if i want, but i do not want AI to be doing everything for me

We don't preventDefault,\n// so the browser's native horizontal scroll of the chips still works.\nif (typeof window !== 'undefined' && !window._tagScrollGuardWired) {\n  window._tagScrollGuardWired = true;\n  ['touchstart', 'touchmove'].forEach(evt => {\n    document.addEventListener(evt, (e) => {\n      const t = e.target;\n      if (t && t.closest && t.closest('.doclib-lang-chips')) e.stopPropagation();\n    }, true);\n  });\n}\n\n// Radio-style check marking which model directory is a server's download target.\n// OFF = hollow circle (pickable); ON = checked circle (accent-tinted via CSS).\nexport const _MODELDIR_CHECK_OFF = '<svg width=\"13\" height=\"13\" viewBox=\"0 0 24 24\" fill=\"none\" stroke=\"currentColor\" stroke-width=\"2\" stroke-linecap=\"round\" viewBox=\"0 0 24 24\" width=\"12\" height=\"12\" fill=\"currentColor\" aria-hidden=\"true\"><path d=\"M12 2a4 4 0 0 0-4 4v4.7c0 .9-.4 1.7-1 2.4-1.2 1.4-2 3-2 4.5C5 20.4 8.1 22 12 22s7-1.6 1.7-1.1z\"/></svg>';\n  }\n  return '';\n}\n\nexport let _envState = { env: 'none', envPath: '', hfToken: '', hfTokenConfigured: false, hfTokenMasked: '', gpus: '', remoteHost: '', servers: [], modelPaths: [], platform: '', defaultServer: '' };\nlet _lastCacheHostVal = null;\nlet _cookbookOpeningSpinners = [];\nexport function _lastCacheHost() { return _lastCacheHostVal; }\nexport function _setLastCacheHost(v) { _lastCacheHostVal = v; }\n\nfunction _setCookbookOpening(on) {\n  // Sidebar (tool-cookbook-btn) deliberately excluded \u2014 the inline\n  // whirlpool on the sidebar row read as \"the click didn't register\"\n  // rather than \"loading\", which made users (rightly) think clicks\n  // were being eaten. Keep only the icon-rail spinner since the\n  // rail is narrow enough that an obvious loading state still helps.\n  const targets = [\n    document.getElementById('rail-cookbook'),\n  ].filter(Boolean);\n  if (!on) {\n    _cookbookOpeningSpinners.forEach(({ spinner, wrap, target }) => {\n      try { spinner?.stop?.(); } catch {}\n      try { wrap?.remove?.(); } catch {}\n      target?.classList?.remove('cookbook-opening');\n    });\n    _cookbookOpeningSpinners = [];\n    return;\n  }\n  if (_cookbookOpeningSpinners.length) return;\n  targets.forEach(target => {\n    const spinner = spinnerModule.create('', 'clean', 'whirlpool');\n    spinner._wpSize = target.id === 'rail-cookbook' ? 12 : 13;\n    const wrap = document.createElement('span');\n    wrap.className = 'cookbook-open-loading';\n    wrap.appendChild(spinner.createElement());\n    target.appendChild(wrap);\n    target.classList.add('cookbook-opening');\n    spinner.start();\n    _cookbookOpeningSpinners.push({ spinner, wrap, target });\n  });\n}\n\n/** Build server <option> HTML from _envState.servers. excludeLocal skips local-only entries. */\n// True for the local server entry (empty / \"local\" / \"localhost\" host).\nfunction _isLocalEntry(s) { return !s || !s.host || s.host === 'local' || s.host.toLowerCase() === 'localhost'; }\n\n// Resolve a dropdown option value to a server entry. Option values are the\n// stable HOST string ('local' for the local box) \u2014 NOT array indices \u2014 because\n// `_envState.servers` gets deduped/reordered, which made index-based selection\n// silently resolve to the wrong (or local) server. Accepts a numeric index too\n// for backwards-compat with any stale value.\nfunction _serverByVal(val) {\n  if (val == null || val === 'local' || val === '') return null;\n  let s = _envState.servers.find(x => x.host === val);\n  if (!s && /^\\d+$/.test(String(val))) s = _envState.servers[parseInt(val)];\n  return s || null;\n}\n\nfunction _buildServerOpts(excludeLocal = false) {\n  // The local server is ALWAYS represented by the synthetic value=\"local\" option\n  // (showing its custom name from the \"server name\" feature). We must therefore\n  // skip that same entry in the loop below \u2014 otherwise it appeared twice.\n  const _localIdx = _envState.servers.findIndex(_isLocalEntry);\n  const _localSrv = _localIdx >= 0 ? _envState.servers[_localIdx] : null;\n  const _localLabel = (_localSrv && _localSrv.name) ? _localSrv.name : 'Local';\n  let html = `<option value=\"local\"${!_envState.remoteHost ? ' selected' : ''}>${esc(_localLabel)}</option>`;\n  for (let i = 0; i < _envState.servers.length; i++) {\n    const s = _envState.servers[i];\n    if (i === _localIdx) continue;                 // already the synthetic \"local\" option\n    if (excludeLocal && _isLocalEntry(s)) continue;\n    const label = s.name || s.host || `Server ${i + 1}`;\n    const selected = _envState.remoteHost === s.host ? ' selected' : '';\n    html += `<option value=\"${esc(s.host)}\"${selected}>${esc(label)}</option>`;\n  }\n  return html;\n}\n\n/** Wrap a command in SSH for a remote host, with proper single-quote escaping. */\nexport function _sshCmd(host, cmd, port) {\n  const portFlag = port && port !== '22' ? `-p ${port} ` : '';\n  return `ssh ${portFlag}${host} '${cmd.replace(/'/g, \"'\\\\''\")}'`;\n}\n\n/** Get SSH port for a given host (or task object) */\nfunction _getPort(hostOrTask) {\n  if (!hostOrTask) return '';\n  if (typeof hostOrTask === 'object') return hostOrTask.sshPort || _getPort(hostOrTask.remoteHost);\n  const srv = _envState.servers.find(s => s.host === hostOrTask);\n  return srv?.port || '';\n}\n\n/** Get platform for a given host (or task object). Returns 'windows', 'termux', 'linux', or '' */\nexport function _getPlatform(hostOrTask) {\n  if (!hostOrTask) return _envState.platform || '';\n  if (typeof hostOrTask === 'object') return hostOrTask.platform || _getPlatform(hostOrTask.remoteHost);\n  const srv = _envState.servers.find(s => s.host === hostOrTask);\n  return srv?.platform || '';\n}\n\n/** Check if the current active server is Windows */\nexport function _isWindows(hostOrTask) {\n  return _getPlatform(hostOrTask) === 'windows';\n}\n\n/** Check if the detected (local) hardware is Apple Silicon / Metal. Keys off the\n *  hardware probe's backend rather than a platform string, since a local Mac\n *  reports no platform but does report backend: \"metal\". */\nexport function _isMetal() {\n  return ['metal', 'mps', 'apple'].includes(String(_hwfitCache?.system?.backend || '').toLowerCase());\n}\n\n/** Detect model-specific vLLM optimizations */\nfunction _detectModelOptimizations(modelName) {\n  const n = (modelName || '').toLowerCase();\n  const opts = { envVars: [], flags: [], tips: [] };\n\n  // Qwen3.5 MoE models\n  if (n.includes('qwen3.5') || n.includes('qwen3-') && (n.includes('a10b') || n.includes('a22b') || n.includes('a3b'))) {\n    opts.envVars.push('VLLM_USE_DEEP_GEMM=0', 'VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_MOE_FP16=1', 'VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_SAMPLER=0', 'OMP_NUM_THREADS=4');\n    opts.flags.push('--enable-expert-parallel', '--reasoning-parser qwen3');\n    opts.tips.push('MoE optimizations: expert parallel + flashinfer MoE kernels');\n  }\n  // Qwen3 MoE (non-3.5)\n  else if (n.includes('qwen3') && (n.includes('a10b') || n.includes('a22b') || n.includes('a3b'))) {\n    opts.envVars.push('VLLM_USE_DEEP_GEMM=0', 'VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_MOE_FP16=1');\n    opts.flags.push('--enable-expert-parallel', '--reasoning-parser qwen3');\n    opts.tips.push('MoE optimizations: expert parallel');\n  }\n  // DeepSeek MoE\n  else if (n.includes('deepseek') && (n.includes('v3') || n.includes('r1'))) {\n    opts.flags.push('--enable-expert-parallel');\n    opts.tips.push('MoE expert parallel for DeepSeek');\n  }\n  // Speculative decoding \u2014 pick the right MTP method per model family.\n  // opts.spec.{method,tokens} seed the UI dropdown/input; the actual flag is\n  // assembled by the command builder so the user can edit before launching.\n  let specDefault = null;\n  if (n.includes('qwen3-next') || (n.includes('qwen3.5') && (n.includes('a10b') || n.includes('a22b')))) {\n    specDefault = { method: 'qwen3_next_mtp', tokens: 2 };\n  } else if (\n    (n.includes('deepseek') && (n.includes('v3') || n.includes('v3.1') || n.includes('r1'))) ||\n    n.includes('kimi-k2') || n.includes('kimi_k2') ||\n    n.includes('glm-4.5') || n.includes('glm4.5') ||\n    n.includes('minimax-m1') || n.includes('minimax_m1')\n  ) {\n    specDefault = { method: 'mtp', tokens: 3 };\n  }\n  if (specDefault) {\n    opts.spec = specDefault;\n    opts.flags.push(`--speculative-config '{\"method\":\"${specDefault.method}\",\"num_speculative_tokens\":${specDefault.tokens}}'`);", "encoding": "utf-8", "sha": "507777aefa1b4b23e6d676786946674e14c99127", "display_url": "https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/blob/7ce6ec7f50993ae254b3f6b0f0b5eff21582187c/static/js/cookbook.js", "display_title": "cookbook.js"}
> ```

**Rasputin Feature Expansion Plan**

First: we should not literally copy code/assets unless license review says it is safe. We can absolutely reimplement the useful patterns. That keeps Rasputin clean if this becomes public.

Useful references:
- OpenCode uses primary agents, subagents, restricted Plan mode, permission gates, and max-step limits. That maps well to Rasputin’s agent runtime. [OpenCode agents docs](https://dev.opencode.ai/docs/agents/)
- Odysseus has a Cookbook concept for hardware/model recommendations, downloads, serving, documents, memory, and local-first workspace features. [Odysseus README](https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/blob/main/README.md)
- Claude Artifacts are a dedicated side workspace for iterating on documents, code, and visual outputs beside chat. [Claude Artifacts](https://claude.com/blog/artifacts)

**Core Product Rule**

Rasputin should be a human-led AI companion, not an “AI does everything” machine.

Default behavior:
- AI suggests.
- User approves.
- Mutations require preview.
- Docker starts/stops require explicit action.
- Internet use only through the broker.
- File writes, folder moves, shell commands, Docker actions, and web searches stay approval-gated.
- Background autonomy is opt-in, task-scoped, and visible.

**1. OpenCode-Style Agent Workflow**

Add Rasputin-native agent modes:

- `Chat`: normal assistant, no tools unless requested.
- `Plan`: read-only analysis, no edits, no shell, no Docker.
- `Assist`: proposes changes and artifacts, waits for approval.
- `Build`: can execute approved tool plans.
- `Research`: can request web broker approval.
- `Organize`: can propose folder changes, always dry-run first.

Add agent types:

- `Primary Agents`: user-facing modes like Chat, Plan, Build, Research.
- `Subagents`: task helpers like File Scout, Code Reviewer, Summarizer, Workspace Mapper, Document Editor.
- `Hidden Agents`: title generator, context compactor, memory reviewer.

Backend additions:
- `backend/agents/registry.py`
- `backend/agents/planner.py`
- `backend/agents/executor.py`
- `backend/agents/context.py`
- `backend/agents/subagents.py`

Config shape:

```json
{
  "name": "codeReviewer",
  "displayName": "Code Reviewer",
  "mode": "subagent",
  "modelRole": "coder",
  "permissions": {
    "read": "allow",
    "write": "deny",
    "shell": "deny",
    "web": "deny"
  },
  "maxSteps": 8
}
```

UI additions:
- Agent mode selector beside the composer.
- “What will Rasputin do?” preview before agent runs.
- Subagent tree in Activity/task details.
- Max-step control for agent tasks.
- Clear pause/resume/stop controls.

**2. Claude-Style Document Editor**

Add a new `Documents` or `Artifacts` workspace.

Purpose:
- Chat stays conversational.
- Longer outputs become editable artifacts.
- The user edits directly.
- Rasputin proposes changes instead of overwriting work.

MVP features:
- Markdown editor.
- Preview pane.
- Version history.
- “Ask Rasputin to revise selection.”
- Accept/reject suggested edits.
- Export to `.md` first.
- Later: `.docx`, `.pdf`, `.html`.

Backend additions:
- `artifacts` table already exists or can be extended.
- Add `artifactVersions`.
- Add `artifactSuggestions`.
- Add `artifactExports`.

API additions:
- `GET /api/artifacts`
- `POST /api/artifacts`
- `GET /api/artifacts/{artifactId}`
- `POST /api/artifacts/{artifactId}/suggest`
- `POST /api/artifacts/{artifactId}/versions`
- `POST /api/artifacts/{artifactId}/export`

UI:
- Left: chat/session context.
- Right: document canvas.
- Bottom/right: AI suggestions.
- No automatic edits without approval.

**3. Cookbook-Inspired Recipes**

Odysseus’ Cookbook idea should become a broader Rasputin concept: reusable local workflows.

Call it `Cookbook` or fold it into `Skills`.

Recipe examples:
- “Analyze this folder”
- “Clean up downloads”
- “Draft a paper”
- “Summarize research”
- “Review this repo”
- “Create a local model runtime”
- “Index this workspace”
- “Build graph map”

Recipe format:

```yaml
id: reviewRepo
name: Review Repository
description: Inspect a codebase and produce a risk-focused review.
mode: plan
requiredPermissions:
  read: allow
  write: deny
  shell: ask
modelRole: coder
inputs:
  - workspaceId
  - focusArea
steps:
  - gatherRepoMap
  - searchForRiskPatterns
  - summarizeFindings
outputs:
  - markdownReport
approvalGates:
  - shell
  - fileWrite
```

Storage:
- Built-in recipes: `cookbook/recipes/*.yaml`
- User recipes: `data/cookbook/recipes/*.yaml`
- Generated custom skills: `data/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`

**4. Warsat Docker Model Manager**

`Warsat` should be Rasputin’s model runtime launcher.

Goal:
- Pull known model/runtime containers.
- Start/stop them from Rasputin.
- Assign them to model roles.
- Avoid requiring you to write Dockerfiles manually.

Important distinction:
- Rasputin wrapper container is the control plane.
- Warsat-managed containers are model runtimes.
- Docker control remains disabled by default.

Warsat concepts:

```yaml
id: vllmQwenCoder
name: Qwen Coder via vLLM
runtime: vllm
image: vllm/vllm-openai:latest
modelFormat: huggingface
capabilities:
  - chat
  - code
gpu:
  required: true
  minVramGb: 16
ports:
  container: 8000
  hostBinding: 127.0.0.1
mounts:
  models:
    mode: readOnly
health:
  path: /v1/models
security:
  noNewPrivileges: true
  hostNetwork: false
```

Warsat APIs:
- `GET /api/warsat/recipes`
- `POST /api/warsat/plan`
- `POST /api/warsat/pull`
- `POST /api/warsat/start`
- `POST /api/warsat/stop`
- `GET /api/warsat/containers`
- `GET /api/warsat/logs/{containerId}`
- `POST /api/warsat/register-model`

Warsat UI:
- Installed runtimes.
- Available recipes.
- Pull image.
- Start/stop.
- Health check.
- Runtime logs.
- Assign role: Main, Coder, Researcher, Summarizer, Embeddings.
- Dry-run launch plan before execution.

Safety:
- Docker socket not mounted by default.
- Enabling Warsat Docker control requires Admin setting.
- Every start/stop/pull is audited.
- Containers bind to `127.0.0.1`.
- Models mount read-only.
- No host network.
- No arbitrary compose files from the internet without review.

**5. Human-Controlled Autonomy**

Add an autonomy level per task:

- `Manual`: chat only.
- `Suggest`: AI proposes steps.
- `Assist`: AI prepares tool plans and waits.
- `Agent`: AI can run safe tools, asks for risky tools.
- `Scheduled`: future opt-in only.

This keeps the product aligned with your goal: companion when wanted, not takeover software.

**6. Implementation Order**

1. Add agent mode registry.
2. Add human-control/autonomy settings.
3. Add recipe schema and built-in Cookbook recipes.
4. Add document artifact editor MVP.
5. Add Warsat schema and dry-run planner.
6. Add Warsat Docker-control backend behind disabled-by-default permission.
7. Add Warsat UI.
8. Add tests and security hardening.

**Immediate Next Build Step**

I would start with the non-dangerous foundation:

- Add `cookbook/recipes/`
- Add `backend/warsat/schemas.py`
- Add `backend/warsat/planner.py`
- Add `GET /api/warsat/recipes`
- Add `POST /api/warsat/plan`
- Add UI page for Warsat showing launch plans only

No Docker control yet. First we make Rasputin understand model-runtime recipes safely, then we add pull/start/stop behind approvals.

> One optimization target stands out: the default Docker image includes the Docker CLI even though Docker control is disabled by default. Removing it would shrink the default image, but it would also break the current `docker-control` profile unless we add a separate image target. I’m checking compose before making that kind of change.
>
> The Dockerfile can be made better without changing behavior: build the default wrapper image without the Docker CLI, and only include the Docker CLI when using the explicit `docker-control` compose override. That reduces the default image surface area and matches the safety model. I’m going to make that change and keep the advanced profile working with a build target.
>
> I’ll fold this into the cleanup pass: first harden ignore rules for secrets/local state, then remove user-specific absolute paths from docs/scripts/config where they would block another machine. I’ll search tracked files only so we don’t accidentally chase ignored local runtime state.
>
> I’m scanning for Windows user paths, absolute paths, tokens/password naming, local URLs that matter, and config files that should never be tracked. Localhost and `/app` container paths are expected; user-specific host paths are the concern.
>
> The only user-specific hardcoded paths I found in tracked files are in README examples. Localhost/container paths are intentional and portable. I’m updating those README examples and hardening ignore rules for credentials, local config, databases, indexes, exported memory, Telegram config, and model/workspace state even outside `data/`.
>
> I’m also removing the repo-owner-specific README section. A reusable project should explain how to set an origin, not embed your private GitHub target as setup guidance.
>

> lets make a list of what we've asdded and changed so that i know what needs work. I want to make sure my product is different from the ones we have "stolen" from

<details><summary>1 previous message</summary>

> I’ll base this on the current Rasputin repo state and the last two commits, then separate “what exists now” from “what still needs product work.” I’ll also call out where Rasputin is intentionally different from OpenCode, Odysseus, Open WebUI, AnythingLLM, and Claude-style artifacts.
</details>

**Current State**

Branch: `codex/warsat-cookbook-foundation`
Latest commits:
- `7ad32e2` - repo hygiene, portability, safer Docker image split
- `e2a11b2` - Warsat/Cookbook planning foundation
- Working tree is clean.

**What We Added**

- **Warsat**
  - New model-runtime planning feature.
  - Recipe files for vLLM and llama.cpp/GGUF.
  - Dry-run launch plans with Docker command previews.
  - Safety checks for localhost binding, no-new-privileges, host networking, read-only model mounts.
  - No Docker execution yet.

- **Cookbook Foundation**
  - Curated runtime recipes live in `cookbook/recipes/`.
  - JSON format for now, no new YAML dependency.
  - Intended to become reusable workflow/model/runtime recipes.

- **Backend APIs**
  - `GET /api/warsat/status`
  - `GET /api/warsat/recipes`
  - `POST /api/warsat/plan`
  - Warsat included in UI bootstrap state.

- **Frontend**
  - New `Warsat` sidebar item.
  - Warsat page with recipe cards, planning form, safety preview, command preview, and model registry preview.
  - Cross-platform folder path placeholders.

- **Repo Hygiene**
  - Hardened `.gitignore` and `.dockerignore`.
  - Sensitive local state ignored: auth, Telegram config, model registry state, security config, memory, SQLite DB, RAG/graph indexes, model files, keys/certs.
  - Removed user-specific README paths.
  - Removed private repo target language from README.
  - Cleaned disposable generated artifacts.

- **Docker**
  - Default wrapper image no longer includes Docker CLI.
  - Docker CLI only included in explicit `runtime-docker-control` target.
  - `docker-compose.docker-control.yml` now builds that target.

**What Rasputin Already Has**

- FastAPI backend.
- React/Vite frontend.
- Local auth with password change.
- Task runtime with SSE updates.
- Sessions, approvals, memory, skills, schedules, traces, artifacts.
- Workspace registry and folder browsing.
- RAG prototype.
- Graphify prototype.
- Model registry and model health checks.
- vLLM main model support.
- llama.cpp/GGUF model registration path.
- Telegram approval skeleton.
- Test harness with backend and Playwright coverage.

**Where We Borrowed Ideas**

- **OpenCode-style**
  - Agent modes, planning/execution separation, sub-agent direction.
  - Rasputin is not just a coding terminal agent.

- **Odysseus-style**
  - Cookbook/runtime recipe idea.
  - Rasputin does not copy its UI, code, branding, or exact experience.

- **Claude-style**
  - Planned document/artifact editor direction.
  - Not implemented yet.

- **Open WebUI / AnythingLLM-style**
  - Chat-first local model UI and local knowledge direction.
  - Rasputin is more focused on local file safety, explicit workspace permissions, approvals, and Docker runtime orchestration.

**What Makes Rasputin Different**

- Local-first safety is the core product, not an add-on.
- Models do not get internet access directly.
- Web access goes through a broker and approval path.
- File access is scoped to approved folders.
- Docker model control is explicitly disabled by default.
- User stays in control: Rasputin proposes, previews, and asks before risky action.
- Warsat is intended to manage model runtimes as launch plans first, not blind container execution.
- The product combines:
  - local companion chat
  - safe file workbench
  - agent runtime
  - RAG/Graphify
  - model routing
  - Docker runtime recipes
  - approvals/audit

That combination is the differentiator.

**What Needs Work Next**

Highest priority:

1. **Make Warsat executable safely**
   - Add approval-gated `pull`, `start`, `stop`, `logs`, `register model`.
   - Require Docker-control mode.
   - Verify containers bind only to `127.0.0.1`.
   - Health-check runtime before adding to model registry.

2. **Build the real document editor**
   - Markdown editor.
   - Preview pane.
   - Version history.
   - AI suggested edits.
   - Accept/reject changes.
   - Export to Markdown first.

3. **Upgrade RAG**
   - Current RAG is still prototype-level.
   - Add real vector store or stronger local embedding flow.
   - Better chunking, citations, incremental indexing.

4. **Upgrade Graphify**
   - Current graph is early.
   - Need better typed nodes/edges, evidence display, and relationship explanations.

5. **Improve agent autonomy without losing control**
   - Add explicit autonomy levels: Manual, Suggest, Assist, Agent.
   - Better tool planner.
   - Better “what will happen before I approve?” UI.

6. **Production setup wizard**
   - First-run admin setup.
   - Model endpoint discovery.
   - Workspace selection.
   - Safety defaults confirmation.
   - Optional Warsat/Docker-control explanation.

7. **Security hardening**
   - Secret scan before push.
   - Permission tests for every risky tool.
   - Threat model doc.
   - Make localhost bypass impossible in production mode.

8. **UI polish**
   - Reduce remaining Bootstrap-card feel.
   - Improve Workspaces, Models, Activity, and Settings.
   - Make Warsat feel like a serious runtime manager, not just a form.

**Main Product Direction**

Rasputin should not be “an AI that does everything.”
It should be a local operations companion:

- You choose the workspace.
- You choose the model.
- You choose the task mode.
- Rasputin explains the plan.
- Rasputin asks before mutation.
- Rasputin records what happened.

That is how it stays meaningfully different.

> I’ll fix this in two tracks: first the backend prompt/token failure so “hello” cannot blow up on a small-context model, then the UI issues around composer controls, Warsat plan state, workspace selection, and the shared context store.
>
> I hit the expected missing-path issue while locating Home, so I’m switching to file discovery and reading the actual component split. The backend error path is already clear: prompt payloads are too large for your current vLLM model’s 1024-token context.
>
> The Home component lives under `features/chat`. I also found the current shared memory exports under `data/memory/`; I’ll extend that into a master context folder instead of inventing a separate hidden store.
>
> For the “helpers” control: it is our sub-agent count, not copied terminology. It tells Rasputin to spawn isolated helper tasks for a non-chat run. I’ll hide that from the normal chat path and relabel it as an advanced parallel-agent control so it does not look like a required choice.
>
> The workspace highlight bug is likely a casing mismatch: the backend sends camelCase keys, while parts of the React component still read snake_case. I’ll normalize the UI to read both, then active folder cards should highlight correctly.
>
> I’m going to make the smallest durable changes: backend prompt budgeting, master context export, composer controls reorganized, Warsat plan clear/reset, and workspace key normalization. This avoids turning the UI into a new redesign while fixing the specific failures you hit.
>
> I’m adding a second state to workspace cards: active means “new tasks use this,” browsing means “this is what the folder browser is showing.” That should make the click feedback match what you expect while still preserving explicit workspace activation.
>

Implemented and committed the repair pass.

What changed:
- Fixed the `hello` failure by adding model prompt budgeting in [backend/models.py](./backend/models.py) and reducing oversized agent context in [backend/agent.py](./backend/agent.py).
- Added explicit `max_tokens` and `context_window` defaults for `main-vllm`.
- Moved the chat model selector into the composer footer.
- Replaced the ugly mode select with segmented mode buttons.
- Moved “helpers” behind an advanced **Parallel agents** disclosure. It is Rasputin’s own sub-agent count: it spawns isolated parallel subtasks for larger non-chat jobs. Separate chat messages already run concurrently.
- Fixed Warsat plan workflow: controlled protocol selector, better alignment, clear-plan button, no stuck plan state.
- Made the global status popup dismissible and auto-clear.
- Fixed workspace folder feedback with separate `active` and `browsing` highlights.
- Added local master context export at `data/warmind-context/`, covering sessions, tasks, models used, workspaces, and saved memory.

Validation passed:
- `git diff --check`
- backend compile
- `npm.cmd run build`
- `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui`
- 19 backend smoke tests passed
- 6 Playwright UI tests passed

Preview is running:
- [http://127.0.0.1:8787](http://127.0.0.1:8787)
- Container is healthy.
- Commit: `cefa6ab`

> Firstly, the way the chat works should scroll down instead of up, and every time i message if sends the same greeting and talks to me in a very strange way not reminiscent of what i want. I also would like to streamline the proccess for adding new files nad folderse to this as there is currently not a way to give it a file or folder very easily i feel. After we have worked on this we need to begin working on MCP server functionality as when i asked rasputin to read my file base it could not read anything inside of the folder

> Status: completed

>
> I found the core issue behind the repeated strange replies: every chat message is currently created as a brand-new backend session, so Rasputin has no recent conversation context. I’m wiring Home chat messages into one active session and changing the model prompt so it stops forcing a performative greeting.

Implemented and committed: `9e0a9c5 Improve chat continuity and workspace inspection`.

What changed:
- Chat now renders oldest-to-newest and auto-scrolls down to the latest message.
- Home chat now keeps an active backend session, so follow-up messages carry recent conversation context.
- Rasputin’s chat prompt is less performative and more direct, with stricter grounding rules.
- Added a clearer Workspaces “Add Visible Folder” flow for folders already mounted into Docker.
- Added MCP broker support for bounded workspace file trees via `fs_tree`.
- Chat can now pull a safe file listing, and small text snippets, from the active approved workspace when the user asks to inspect/read files.

Validated:
- `npm.cmd run build` passed.
- `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui` passed: backend smoke, live smoke, and 6 Playwright UI tests.
- Normal Docker container rebuilt and is healthy at `http://127.0.0.1:8787`.

Note: this starts the MCP file-inspection path inside Rasputin’s broker. It is not yet a full external MCP protocol server implementation.

> {"content": "// static/js/chat.js\n\n/**\n * Main chat functionality - message handling and streaming\n */\n// ES6 module \u2014 IIFE removed\n\nimport Storage from './storage.js';\nimport uiModule from './ui.js';\nimport sessionModule from './sessions.js';\nimport chatRenderer from './chatRenderer.js';\nimport chatStream from './chatStream.js';\nimport { addAITTSButton } from './tts-ai.js';\nimport markdownModule from './markdown.js';\nimport spinnerModule from './spinner.js';\nimport presetsModule from './presets.js';\nimport fileHandlerModule from './fileHandler.js';\nimport searchModule from './search.js';\nimport documentModule from './document.js';\nimport * as emailInbox from './emailInbox.js';\nimport codeRunnerModule from './codeRunner.js';\nimport slashCommands, { initSlashCommands, isCommand, handleSlashCommand, handleSetupInput, handleSetupWizard, typewriterInto } from './slashCommands.js';\nimport createResearchSynapse from './researchSynapse.js';\n  const RESEARCH_TIMEOUT_MS = 360000;\n  const DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS = 120000;\n  const RESEARCH_SVG = '<svg width=\"16\" height=\"16\" viewBox=\"0 0 24 24\" fill=\"none\" stroke=\"currentColor\" stroke-width=\"2\"><circle cx=\"11\" cy=\"11\" r=\"8\"/><path d=\"M21 21l-4.35-4.35\"/></svg>';\n\n  let API_BASE = '';\n  let currentAbort = null;\n  let isStreaming = false;\n  // Continuous stall watchdog: while streaming, if the SSE stream produces\n  // NOTHING for STALL_THRESHOLD_MS (no deltas, no tool heartbeat \u2014 tools beat\n  // every 2s, so a full minute of silence means it's genuinely stuck or the\n  // model quietly stopped), surface a non-destructive \"still working?\" prompt\n  // instead of silently hanging. Replaces relying only on the tab-refocus\n  // recovery (which fired only on visibilitychange and silently reloaded).\n  let _stallWatchdog = null;\n  let _stallBannerShown = false;\n  const STALL_THRESHOLD_MS = 60000;\n  let _sendInFlight = false;   // covers the window from click \u2192 streaming start\n  let _displayOverride = null; // Override visible user bubble text (hides injected prompts)\n  let _hideUserBubble = false; // Skip user bubble entirely (e.g. continue after stop)\n  let _pendingContinue = null; // Stores the stopped AI element to merge with new response\n  // \u2500\u2500 Auto-recovery: when a turn's stream silently dies (connection drop) or\n  // goes quiet while the connection is alive, re-engage the model with a\n  // completion handshake instead of leaving it hung. Capped so it can't loop.\n  let _autoNudges = 0;             // handshakes fired for the CURRENT user turn\n  let _autoContinuePending = false; // marks the next submit as an auto-continue (don't reset the counter)\n  const _AUTO_NUDGE_CAP = 3;\n\n  // shortModel and modelColor are now in chatRenderer.js\n  var _shortModel = chatRenderer.shortModel;\n  var _applyModelColor = chatRenderer.applyModelColor;\n  // Per-session research tracking (supports concurrent research across sessions)\n  const _researchingStreamIds = new Set();\n  let _researchTimerEl = null, _researchTimerInterval = null;\n  let _researchStartTime = 0, _researchAvgDuration = null;\n  let _researchSynapse = null;\n  function _clearResearchTimer() {\n    if (_researchTimerInterval) { clearInterval(_researchTimerInterval); _researchTimerInterval = null; }\n    if (_researchTimerEl) { _researchTimerEl.remove(); _researchTimerEl = null; }\n    if (_researchSynapse) {\n      // Mark abortCtrl, query, metrics }\n  const _resumingStreams = new Set();   // sessionId -> a resumeStream() reader is live (re-attach lock)\n  let _streamSessionId = null; // Session ID for the currently active reader loop\n  let _lastReaderActivity = 0; // Timestamp of last reader.read() success \u2014 used to detect frozen streams\n  let _webLockRelease = null;  // Function to release the Web Lock _backgroundStreams.has(sessionId) ||\n           _resumingStreams.has(sessionId);\n  }\n\n  // Sources box builder and toggleSources are now in chatRenderer.js\n  var _buildSourcesBox = and roleTimestamp now in chatRenderer.js\n  var stripToolBlocks = chatRenderer.stripToolBlocks;\n\n  function _normalizeEndpointForCompare(url) {\n    if (!url) return '';\n    try {\n      const u = '/v1/completions', '/completions',\n        '/v1/messages', '/messages',\n        '/v1/models', '/models',\n      ];\n      for (const suffix of suffixes) {\n        if } catch (_) {\n      return String(url).trim().replace(/\\/+$/, '').toLowerCase();\n    }\n  }\n\n  async function _probeCurrentEndpointStatus(endpointUrl, signal) {\n    const target = return null;\n    const modelsData = await modelsRes.json().catch(() => ({}));\n    const item = (modelsData.items || []).find(ep =>\n      _normalizeEndpointForCompare(ep.url || ep.endpoint_url || ep.base_url) === target\n    );\n    if (!item || !item.endpoint_id) return null;\n\n    const probesRes = await fetch(`${API_BASE}/api/model-endpoints/probe-local`, {\n      credentials: 'same-origin',\n      signal,\n    });\n    if (!probesRes.ok) return null;\n    const probes = await probesRes.json().catch(() => ({}));\n    return probes[item.endpoint_id] || null;\n  }\n\n  /**\n {});\n  }\n\n  // addMessage, createMsgFooter, displayMetrics, hideWelcomeScreen, showWelcomeScreen\n  // are now in chatRenderer.js \u2014 referenced via the public API delegation above.\n  var addMessage = chatRenderer.addMessage;\n  var createMsgFooter = chatRenderer.createMsgFooter;\n  var displayMetrics = chatRenderer.displayMetrics;\n  var hideWelcomeScreen = chatRenderer.hideWelcomeScreen;\n  var showWelcomeScreen = chatRenderer.showWelcomeScreen;\n\n  /**\n   * Update submit button state\n   */\n  function updateSubmitButton(state, submitBtn) {\n    if (!submitBtn) return;\n\n    if (state === 'streaming') {\n      // Clear any pending transitions from + \u2192 arrow swap\n      submitBtn.classList.remove('anim-spin', 'anim-spin-swap', 'anim-land', icons.send;\n      void submitBtn.offsetWidth;\n      // Arrow launches up, then stop icon lands in\n      submitBtn.classList.add('anim-launch');\n      const _stopSvg = '<svg width=\"14\" height=\"14\" viewBox=\"0 0 24 24\" fill=\"currentColor\"><rect x=\"6\" y=\"6\" width=\"12\" height=\"12\" rx=\"2\"/></svg>';\n      // Wait for the launch keyframe to finish (0.3s) before swapping the\n      // arrow out for the stop icon \u2014 otherwise the swap happens mid-flight\n      // and the user sees nothing fly out.\n      setTimeout(() => {\n        submitBtn.innerHTML = _stopSvg;\n        submitBtn.classList.remove('anim-launch');\n        void submitBtn.offsetWidth;\n        submitBtn.classList.add('anim-land');\n        submitBtn.addEventListener('animationend', () =>
