> {"content": "// Odysseus UI \u2014 Initialization Scripts\n// ES6 module \u2014 extracted from index.html inline scripts\n\nimport Storage from './storage.js';\n\nfunction clearFreshComposerRestore() {\n  const msgInput = document.getElementById('message');\n  if (!msgInput) return;\n  const hasSessionTarget = !!(window.location.hash || Storage.get('lastSessionId'));\n  if (hasSessionTarget) return;\n  if (msgInput.value) {\n    msgInput.value = '';\n    msgInput.dispatchEvent(new Event('input', { bubbles: true }));\n  }\n}\n\nclearFreshComposerRestore();\nwindow.addEventListener('pageshow', clearFreshComposerRestore);\n\n// SECURITY: defense-in-depth state wipe on user switch. If the authenticated\n// user is different from the one whose state is cached in this browser,\n// wipe localStorage + sessionStorage so the new account doesn't inherit\n// the previous user's last session id, last-used model, draft chat input,\n// or cached lists. The settings-tab Logout button already wipes on\n// explicit logout; this catches the cases where a different user signs\n// in without the previous one logging out cleanly.\n(async () => {\n  try {\n    const res = await fetch('/api/auth/status', { credentials: 'same-origin' });\n    if (!res.ok) return;\n    const data = await res.json().catch(() => ({}));\n    const liveUser = (data && data.username) || '';\n    if (!liveUser) return;\n    const KEY = 'odysseus-auth-user';\n    const cachedUser = localStorage.getItem(KEY);\n    if (cachedUser && cachedUser !== liveUser) {\n      const _keepKeys = new Set(['odysseus-last-user', KEY]);\n      const toRemove = [];\n      for (let i = 0; i < localStorage.length; i++) {\n        const k = localStorage.key(i);\n        if (k && !_keepKeys.has(k)) toRemove.push(k);\n      }\n      toRemove.forEach(k => localStorage.removeItem(k));\n      sessionStorage.clear();\n      clearFreshComposerRestore();\n    }\n    localStorage.setItem(KEY, liveUser);\n    // Apply per-user privilege gates to the UI. The backend enforces these\n    // independently \u2014 this is purely cosmetic / \"don't dangle controls the\n    // user can't actually use.\" Privileges come from /api/auth/status; admins\n    // always get the full set so this is a no-op for them.\n    try {\n      const privs = (data && data.privileges) || {};\n      const hideOn = (selector, allowed) => {\n        if (allowed === undefined || allowed === true) return;\n        document.querySelectorAll(selector).forEach(el => {\n          el.style.display = 'none';\n        });\n      };\n      // Document editor \u2014 overflow menu button + the docs panel rail/tool button.\n      hideOn('#overflow-doc-btn, #tool-doc-btn', privs.can_use_documents);\n      // Research \u2014 sidebar tool + the in-input deep-research toggle.\n      hideOn('#tool-research-btn, #research-toggle-btn', privs.can_use_research);\n      // Memory & skills (rail/tool button only \u2014 UI/API entry).\n      hideOn('#tool-memory-btn', privs.can_manage_memory);\n      // Agent mode toggle \u2014 force chat mode by hiding the Agent toggle button.\n      if (privs.can_use_agent === false) {\n        const _agent = document.getElementById('mode-agent-btn');\n        const _chat = document.getElementById('mode-chat-btn');\n        if (_agent) _agent.style.display = 'none';\n        if (_chat) { _chat.classList.add('active'); _chat.click?.(); }\n      }\n    } catch (_) { /* DOM not ready or unexpected shape \u2014 UI gates are non-fatal */ }\n  } catch (_) { /* anonymous / loopback mode \u2014 nothing to do */ }\n})();\n\n/* Sidebar section default-collapsed setup. The click-to-toggle handlers\n   themselves live in js/section-management.js \u2014 attaching them in BOTH\n   places caused two toggles per click, which read as \"clicks aren't doing\n   anything\" (even-count parity). Keep only the initial-state-apply here. */\n{\n  const KEY = Storage.KEYS.SIDEBAR_COLLAPSED;\n  const saved = Storage.getJSON(KEY, {});\n  const _defaultCollapsed = { 'sessions-section': true };\n  document.querySelectorAll('.sidebar .section').forEach((section) => {\n    const id = section.id;\n    if (!id) return;\n    const shouldCollapse = (id in saved) ? saved[id] : !!_defaultCollapsed[id];\n    if (shouldCollapse) section.classList.add('collapsed');\n  });\n  // Sessions-section notification dot: clear when the section becomes\n  // expanded. Watch the class with MutationObserver so we don't need a\n  // click handler (which would race the section-management one).\n  const sessionsSection = document.getElementById('sessions-section');\n  if (sessionsSection) {\n    new MutationObserver(() => {\n      if (!sessionsSection.classList.contains('collapsed')) {\n        const dot = document.getElementById('chats-notif-dot');\n        if (dot) dot.style.display = 'none';\n      }\n    }).observe(sessionsSection, { attributes: true, attributeFilter: ['class'] });\n  }\n}\n\n/* Publish the icon rail's + wide sidebar's current widths as CSS vars so\n   fullscreen panels can reserve space on the left for whichever is\n   currently visible (the two are mutually exclusive \u2014 see\n   sidebar-layout.js:57). Updates live as either resizes; toggles to 0\n   when hidden so the fullscreen view reclaims the space. */\n{\n  const rail = document.getElementById('icon-rail');\n  const sidebar = document.getElementById('sidebar');\n  const root = document.documentElement;\n  const _measure = (el) => {\n    if (!el) return null;\n    const cs = window.getComputedStyle(el);\n    const hidden = cs.display === 'none' || cs.visibility === 'hidden';\n    if (hidden) return 0;\n    return Math.round(el.getBoundingClientRect().width);\n  };\n  const _sync = () => {\n    // Icon rail width\n    const rw = _measure(rail);\n    if (rw === null) {\n      root.style.removeProperty('--icon-rail-w');\n    } else if (rw > 0) {\n      root.style.setProperty('--icon-rail-w', rw + 'px');\n    } else {\n      // 0 from a visible-but-not-yet-laid-out rail: don't shadow the\n      // CSS fallback; re-sync on the next frame instead.\n      const cs = rail && window.getComputedStyle(rail);\n      const hidden = !cs || cs.display === 'none' || cs.visibility === 'hidden';\n      if (hidden) {\n        root.style.setProperty('--icon-rail-w', '0px');\n      } else {\n        root.style.removeProperty('--icon-rail-w');\n        requestAnimationFrame(_sync);\n        return;\n      }\n    }\n    // Sidebar width \u2014 `.sidebar.hidden` collapses to width: 0 so the\n    // measurement is naturally 0 in the hidden state.\n    const sw = _measure(sidebar);\n    if (sw === null) {\n      root.style.removeProperty('--sidebar-w');\n    } else {\n      root.style.setProperty('--sidebar-w', sw + 'px');\n    }\n  };\n  _sync();\n  if (typeof ResizeObserver !== 'undefined') {\n    const ro = new ResizeObserver(_sync);\n    if (rail) ro.observe(rail);\n    if (sidebar) ro.observe(sidebar);\n  }\n  // Class flips (sidebar.hidden \u2194 visible) don't trigger ResizeObserver\n  // until layout settles a frame later; also watch the class attribute\n  // so we re-sync immediately when the user toggles the hamburger.\n  if (sidebar && typeof MutationObserver !== 'undefined') {\n    new MutationObserver(_sync).observe(sidebar, { attributes: true, attributeFilter: ['class', 'style'] });\n  }\n  if (rail && typeof MutationObserver !== 'undefined') {\n    new MutationObserver(_sync).observe(rail, { attributes: true, attributeFilter: ['class', 'style'] });\n  }\n  window.addEventListener('resize', _sync);\n}\n\n/* Keep minimized tool chips above the composer. Both the current modalManager\n   dock and the legacy fallback dock consume this root-level clearance. */\n{\n  const root = document.documentElement;\n  const chatBar = document.querySelector('.chat-input-bar');\n  const attachStrip = document.getElementById('attach-strip');\n  const chatContainer = document.getElementById('chat-container');\n  const _syncComposerClearance = () => {\n    let top = window.innerHeight;\n    for (const el of [attachStrip, chatBar]) {\n      if (!el) continue;\n      const rect = el.getBoundingClientRect();\n      if (rect.height > 0) top = Math.min(top, rect.top);\n    }\n    const clearance = Math.max(12, Math.ceil(window.innerHeight - top + 8));\n    root.style.setProperty('--composer-clearance', clearance + 'px');\n  };\n  requestAnimationFrame(_syncComposerClearance);\n  if (typeof ResizeObserver !== 'undefined') {\n    const ro = new ResizeObserver(_syncComposerClearance);\n    if (chatBar) ro.observe(chatBar);\n    if (attachStrip) ro.observe(attachStrip);\n  }\n  if (chatContainer && typeof MutationObserver !== 'undefined') {\n    new MutationObserver(_syncComposerClearance).observe(chatContainer, {\n      attributes: true,\n      attributeFilter: ['class'],\n    });\n  }\n  if (chatBar) chatBar.addEventListener('transitionend', _syncComposerClearance);\n  window.addEventListener('resize', _syncComposerClearance);\n}\n\n/* ---- Resizable sidebar \u2014 drag edge to resize, collapse if small, drag rail edge to expand ---- */\n{\n  const sidebar = document.getElementById('sidebar');\n  const handle = document.getElementById('sidebar-resize-handle');\n  const railHandle = document.getElementById('rail-resize-handle');\n  const iconRail = document.getElementById('icon-rail');\n  if (sidebar && handle) {\n\n  const STORAGE_KEY = Storage.KEYS.SIDEBAR_WIDTH;\n  const MIN_WIDTH = 200;\n  const MAX_WIDTH = 700;\n  const COLLAPSE_THRESHOLD = 150;\n\n  function getSavedWidth() {\n    const w = parseInt(Storage.get(STORAGE_KEY, '340'), 10);\n    return (w >= MIN_WIDTH && w <= MAX_WIDTH) ? w : 340;\n  }\n\n  // Restore saved width\n  const savedWidth = Storage.get(STORAGE_KEY);\n  if (savedWidth) {\n    const w = parseInt(savedWidth, 10);\n    if (w >= MIN_WIDTH && w <= MAX_WIDTH) sidebar.style.width = w + 'px';\n  }\n\n  let startX, startWidth, isRight, collapsed, expanding;\n\n  // --- Drag from sidebar edge to resize / collapse ---\n  handle.addEventListener('mousedown', (e) => {\n    e.preventDefault();\n    expanding = false;\n    isRight = sidebar.classList.contains('right-side');\n    startX = e.clientX;\n    startWidth = sidebar.getBoundingClientRect().width;\n    collapsed = false;\n    sidebar.classList.add('resizing');\n    handle.classList.add('dragging');\n    document.addEventListener('mousemove', onDrag);\n    document.addEventListener('mouseup', stopDrag);\n  });\n\n  // --- Drag from icon rail edge to expand sidebar ---\n  if (railHandle) {\n    railHandle.addEventListener('mousedown', (e) => {\n      e.preventDefault();\n      expanding = true;\n      isRight = sidebar.classList.contains('right-side') ||\n                iconRail.classList.contains('right-side');\n      startX = e.clientX;\n      collapsed = false;\n\n      // Unhide sidebar at 0 width so we can grow it\n      sidebar.classList.remove('hidden');\n      sidebar.classList.add('resizing');\n      sidebar.style.width = '0px';\n      sidebar.style.opacity = '0.3';\n      railHandle.classList.add('dragging');\n\n      document.addEventListener('mousemove', onExpandDrag);\n      document.addEventListener('mouseup', stopExpandDrag);", "encoding": "utf-8", "sha": > ```text
> {"content": "// ============================================\n// Odysseus UI \u2014 Main Application Orchestrator\n// ES6 module \u2014 entry point, no exports (wires all modules together)\n// ============================================\nimport Storage from './js/storage.js';\nimport uiModule from './js/ui.js';\nimport fileHandlerModule from './js/fileHandler.js';\nimport modelsModule from './js/models.js';\nimport ragModule from './js/rag.js';\nimport presetsModule from './js/presets.js';\nimport searchModule from './js/search.js';\nimport chatModule from './js/chat.js';\nimport compareModule from './js/compare/index.js';\nimport documentModule from './js/document.js';\nimport searchChatModule from './js/search-chat.js';\nimport { makeWindowDraggable } from './js/windowDrag.js';\nimport markdownModule from './js/markdown.js';\nimport chatRenderer from './js/chatRenderer.js';\nimport sessionModule from './js/sessions.js';\nimport memoryModule from './js/memory.js';\nimport voiceRecorderModule from './js/voiceRecorder.js';\nimport censorModule from './js/censor.js';\nimport galleryModule from './js/gallery.js';\nimport tasksModule from './js/tasks.js';\nimport calendarModule from './js/calendar.js';\nimport notesModule from './js/notes.js';\nimport adminModule from './js/admin.js';\nimport settingsModule from './js/settings.js';\n// Eagerly bind unified minimize/restore behavior across all tool modals.\nimport './js/modalManager.js';\n// Desktop window tiling \u2014 drag a modal near an edge/corner to snap.\nimport './js/tileManager.js';\nimport themeModule from './js/theme.js';\n// IMPORTANT: import cookbook.js with NO ?v= query \u2014 the same plain specifier\n// every other importer (cookbook-hwfit.js / cookbook-diagnosis.js) uses. A query\n// mismatch makes the browser load cookbook.js twice as separate modules (two\n// _envState objects), which broke server selection. Keep all cookbook imports\n// unversioned so this can't recur.\nimport cookbookModule from './js/cookbook.js';\nimport groupModule from './js/group.js';\nimport * as researchPanelModule from './js/research/panel.js';\nimport ttsModule from './js/tts-ai.js';\nimport spinnerModule from './js/spinner.js';\nimport { initKeyboardShortcuts } from './js/keyboard-shortcuts.js';\nimport { initSidebarLayout, syncRailSide } from './js/sidebar-layout.js';\nimport { initSectionCollapse, initSectionDrag } from './js/section-management.js';\n\nconst API_BASE = window.location.origin;\nwindow.themeModule = themeModule;\nwindow.sessionModule = sessionModule;\nwindow.uiModule = uiModule;\nwindow.adminModule = adminModule;\nwindow.cookbookModule = cookbookModule;\n\n// Redirect to login on 401 from any fetch\nconst _origFetch = window.fetch;\nwindow.fetch = async function(...args) {\n  const res = await _origFetch.apply(this, args);\n  if (res.status === 401 && !String(args[0]).includes('/api/auth/')) {\n    window.location.href = '/login';\n  }\n  return res;\n};\n\n// Search settings\n\n\nconst el = uiModule.el;\n\n// Default chat config \u2014 refreshed on every new-chat action so settings\n// changes take effect immediately (previously cached once at page load and\n// went stale when the user changed their default model).\nlet _defaultChat = null;\nasync function _refreshDefaultChat() {\n  try {\n    const d = await (await fetch('/api/default-chat')).json();\n    if (d && d.endpoint_url && d.model) {\n      _defaultChat = d;\n      try { window.__odysseusDefaultChat = d; } catch (_) {}\n      return d;\n    }\n  } catch (_) {}\n  return null;\n}\n// Prime the cache once at load for initial paint paths that read _defaultChat\n// synchronously; later reads should call _refreshDefaultChat() first.\n_refreshDefaultChat();\n\nasync function _createDirectChatFromPreferredModel() {\n  if (!sessionModule) return false;\n\n  const pending = sessionModule.getPendingChat && sessionModule.getPendingChat();\n  if (pending && pending.url && pending.modelId) {\n    sessionModule.createDirectChat(pending.url, pending.modelId, pending.endpointId);\n    return true;\n  }\n\n  const sessions = sessionModule.getSessions();\n  const currentId = sessionModule.getCurrentSessionId();\n  const current = sessions.find(s => s.id === currentId);\n  if (current && current.endpoint_url && current.model) {\n    sessionModule.createDirectChat(current.endpoint_url, current.model, current.endpoint_id);\n    return true;\n  }\n\n  const dc = await _refreshDefaultChat();\n  if (dc) {\n    sessionModule.createDirectChat(dc.endpoint_url, dc.model, dc.endpoint_id);\n    return true;\n  }\n\n  const withModel = sessions.filter(s => s.endpoint_url && s.model);\n  if (withModel.length > 0) {\n    const last = withModel[0]; // sessions are sorted by recent\n    sessionModule.createDirectChat(last.endpoint_url, last.model, last.endpoint_id);\n    return true;\n  }\n\n  return false;\n}\n\n// ============================================\n// EVENT LISTENERS INITIALIZATION\n// ============================================\nfunction initializeEventListeners() {\n  // Chat form submission\n//  document.getElementById('chat-form').addEventListener('submit', chatModule.handleChatSubmit);\n\n  // File attachments (inside overflow menu)\n  const _overflowAttach = el('overflow-attach-btn');\n  if (_overflowAttach) _overflowAttach.addEventListener('click', fileHandlerModule.openPicker);\n  el('file-input').addEventListener('change', (e)=>{\n    for (const f of e.target.files) fileHandlerModule.addFiles([f]);\n    fileHandlerModule.renderAttachStrip();\n    // Refocus textarea after file picker closes (mobile keyboard)\n    const ta = el('message');\n    if (ta) setTimeout(() => ta.focus(), 100);\n  });\n\n  // Paste handler\n  window.addEventListener('paste', async (e)=>{\n    if (!e.clipboardData) return;\n    let changed = false;\n    for (const item of e.clipboardData.items){\n      if (item.kind === 'file'){\n        const f = item.getAsFile();\n        if (f) {\n          fileHandlerModule.addFiles([f]);\n          changed = true;\n        }\n      }\n    }\n    if (changed) fileHandlerModule.renderAttachStrip();\n  });\n\n  // Message count in the header \u2014 recount on any DOM change in\n  // #chat-history and write \"\u00b7 N msgs\" next to the title. Counts top-\n  // level .msg elements (one per user/assistant turn); excludes the\n  // welcome screen since it isn't inside chat-history.\n  const _metaCountEl = el('current-meta-count');\n  const _chatHistEl = el('chat-history');\n  if (_metaCountEl && _chatHistEl) {\n    let _countScheduled = false;\n    const _updateMsgCount = () => {\n      _countScheduled = false;\n      const n = _chatHistEl.querySelectorAll(':scope > .msg').length;\n      _metaCountEl.textContent = n ? `\u00b7 ${n} msg${n === 1 ? '' : 's'}` : '';\n    };\n    const _scheduleCount = () => {\n      if (_countScheduled) return;\n      _countScheduled = true;\n      requestAnimationFrame(_updateMsgCount);\n    };\n    new MutationObserver(_scheduleCount).observe(_chatHistEl, { childList: true });\n    _updateMsgCount();\n  }\n\n  // Scrolling\n  el('chat-history').addEventListener('scroll', uiModule.debounce(() => {\n    const box = el('chat-history');\n    const atBottom = box.scrollHeight - box.scrollTop - box.clientHeight < 80;\n    uiModule.setAutoScroll(atBottom);\n  }, 100));\n  // Close all footer popups immediately on any scroll\n  el('chat-history').addEventListener('scroll', () => {\n    document.querySelectorAll('.ctx-popup, .memory-used-detail, .msg-overflow-menu').forEach(p => p.remove());\n    document.querySelectorAll('.memory-used-pill').forEach(p => { p._openDetail = null; });\n  }, { passive: true });\n\n  el('chat-history').addEventListener('wheel', (e) => {\n    // Only disable auto-scroll when user scrolls UP (deltaY < 0)\n    if (e.deltaY < 0) uiModule.setAutoScroll(false);\n  });\n  let _touchThrottled = false;\n  el('chat-history').addEventListener('touchmove', () => {\n    if (_touchThrottled) return;\n    _touchThrottled = true;\n    uiModule.setAutoScroll(false);\n    requestAnimationFrame(() => { _touchThrottled = false; });\n  }, { passive: true });\n\n  // Internal #session-id links from AI search results\n  el('chat-history').addEventListener('click', (e) => {\n    const link = e.target.closest('a.chat-link');\n    if (!link) return;\n    const href = link.getAttribute('href');\n    if (href && href.startsWith('#') && sessionModule) {\n      e.preventDefault();\n      sessionModule.selectSession(href.slice(1));\n    }\n  });\n\n  // Export dropdown button\n  const exportDlBtn = el('export-dl-btn');\n  // \u2500\u2500 Unified popup dismissal \u2500\u2500\n  // Lightweight popups (header dropdowns, kebab menus, pickers) should vanish\n  // on any \"other action\" \u2014 opening the sidebar, opening a tool window, etc.\n  // Each popup used to wire its own outside-click/Escape close but missed\n  // non-click actions. closeAllPopups() centralizes it: toggled menus drop\n  // their `.open`; ephemeral body-appended menus are removed. Full modals/\n  // windows are deliberately NOT touched here \u2014 those close via their own\n  // controls.\n  window.closeAllPopups = function closeAllPopups(except) {\n    document.querySelectorAll(\n      '.export-dropdown-menu.open, .overflow-menu.open, .model-picker-menu.open, .doc-overflow-menu.open'\n    ).forEach(m => { if (m !== except) m.classList.remove('open'); });\n    document.querySelectorAll(\n      '.skill-kebab-menu, .note-reminder-menu, .task-dropdown, .doclib-card-dropdown, .email-card-dropdown, .msg-overflow-menu'\n    ).forEach(m => { if (m !== except) m.remove(); });\n  };\n  // Window-opening / nav controls (rail buttons, sidebar tool rows + session\n  // rows, section headers) count as \"other actions\" \u2014 dismiss popups when one\n  // is clicked. Bubble phase so it runs after the control's own handler (the\n  // window is already opening; we just clear stray popups). Popup triggers\n  // themselves aren't these selectors, so toggles aren't broken.\n  document.addEventListener('click', (e) => {\n    if (e.target.closest('.icon-rail-btn, #sidebar .list-item, .section-header-flex')) {\n      window.closeAllPopups();\n    }\n  });\n\n  const exportMenu = el('export-dropdown-menu');\n  if (exportDlBtn && exportMenu) {\n    exportDlBtn.addEventListener('click', (e) => {\n      e.stopPropagation();\n      if (exportMenu.classList.contains('open')) {\n        exportMenu.classList.remove('open');\n      } else {\n        // Move menu to body so it's not affected by ancestor transforms\n        if (exportMenu.parentElement !== document.body) document.body.appendChild(exportMenu);\n        const rect = exportDlBtn.getBoundingClientRect();\n        exportMenu.style.top = (rect.bottom + 4) + 'px';\n        exportMenu.style.left = 'auto';\n        exportMenu.style.right = (window.innerWidth - rect.right) + 'px';\n        exportMenu.classList.add('open');\n      }\n    });\n    document.addEventListener('click', () => exportMenu.classList.remove('open'));\n    document.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {\n      if (e.key === 'Escape' && exportMenu.classList.contains('open')) {", "encoding": "utf-8", "sha": "8593da357552139ca3e19b4304383820dbc04b13", "display_url": "https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/blob/dev/static/app.js", "display_title": "app.js"}

> The useful Odysseus mechanics I’m adapting are: persistent/recoverable sessions, a sidebar section for recent chats, scroll behavior that respects the user when they scroll up, and composer-adjacent file/workspace actions. I’m not using its styling, naming, assets, or layout mood.
>
> I’m making the UI pass now. The main changes are recoverable recent chats in the sidebar, better home quick actions, a cleaner workspace/action strip near the composer, and a jump-to-latest control instead of forcing scroll while you inspect older messages.

Implemented and committed: `b9a0e14 Polish Rasputin chat shell UX`.

What changed:
- Added a Recent Chats section to the sidebar so prior sessions can be resumed.
- Added session restore behavior: clicking a recent chat brings its task thread back into Home.
- Added Home quick actions: inspect workspace, add folder, plan coding work, draft document.
- Added composer workspace actions for “Add folder” and “Inspect workspace.”
- Improved chat scrolling: follows latest only when you’re already near the bottom, otherwise shows “Jump to latest.”
- Tightened sidebar and composer layout so it functions more like a mature chat workbench without cloning Odysseus’ look.

Validated:
- `npm.cmd run build` passed.
- `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui` passed twice after layout fixes.
- Docker container is running and healthy at `http://127.0.0.1:8787`.

Remaining known cleanup: Bootstrap Sass deprecation warnings still appear during builds; they are pre-existing and not blocking.

> Currently what we have is a very early prototype. I would like to make a plan to get this to a testing stage, you have access to all open source repos of other ai wrappers to use at your disposal.
>
> A few notes:
>
> The model tab in the chat box should not have default be main local model, not matter what it should say what the model it is running is. The UI is also overlapping slightly in places, specifically the boxes for the different modes. I would like the modes to be a collapsed menu that you have to open up to select a different mode, and have that mode be a popped out side panel inside of the UI potentially even running a different model designed for that specific purpose.
>
> The workspaces also needs a bit of work. I want the workspaces to show the available folders that are maounted to the rasputin container and be able to navigate the files as i would a file explorer esque GUI. There is also overlap and UI issues in the workspaces section.
>
> The font being used is not very interesting. Rememebr first and foremost the "Theme" of our application is the warmind Rasputin from the video game destiny, if you have to split and do research about rasputin feel free.

> I’ll ground this in the current Rasputin code first, then use other AI-wrapper repos as reference for interaction patterns only. I’m not going to mutate files in Plan Mode; this pass is to produce a decision-complete testing-stage plan.
>
> Current facts from the repo: the chat model label is hardcoded through `displayModelName()` to show `Main Local Model` for `main-vllm`, even when the backend knows the real model id. The workspace backend already exposes approved/mounted roots and a safe browse API, but the UI only lists directories and has several card/grid patterns that can overlap at desktop and mobile widths.

> Context compaction

> Source: automatic
> Status: completed
>
> Plan
>
> # Rasputin Testing-Stage UI/UX Stabilization Plan
>
> ## Summary
> Move Rasputin from early prototype to a usable testing build without replacing the current React/FastAPI/Docker architecture. The next pass focuses on the pieces blocking real testing: truthful model display, non-overlapping mode controls, a real mounted-folder explorer, stronger Warmind-inspired visual identity, and test coverage for the core local workflow.
>
> The implementation should borrow proven mechanics from existing AI wrappers, especially chat shell ergonomics, collapsible side panels, streaming/task visibility, and file navigation patterns, but Rasputin must keep its own product language and Warmind-inspired identity.
>
> ## Key Changes
>
> ### 1. Fix Model Truth In Chat
> - Remove hardcoded chat labels like `Main Local Model` from the primary model display.
> - The chat composer must show the actual configured or discovered model ID, such as `llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf`.
> - Friendly names may appear only as secondary labels, never as the source of truth.
> - If Rasputin is configured for `local-main` but vLLM exposes a different model, show a plain mismatch warning and provide a repair action.
> - Home should hide implementation details like raw keys, tags, provider internals, embeddings, and dry-run unless the user opens Advanced model settings.
> - Update UI tests that currently expect `Main Local Model`.
>
> ### 2. Replace Inline Modes With A Right-Side Mode Panel
> - Replace the current row of mode boxes with one compact composer chip: `Mode: Chat`, `Mode: Code`, etc.
> - Clicking the chip opens a right-side panel inside the app shell.
> - The panel lists modes as clean selectable rows/cards:
>   - Chat
>   - Analyze
>   - Research
>   - Code
>   - Write
>   - Organize
> - Each mode shows:
>   - what it is for
>   - current routed model
>   - model role used
>   - whether sub-agents/helpers are available
>   - permission notes where relevant
> - Default role routing:
>   - Chat -> `main`
>   - Analyze -> `executor`
>   - Research -> `researcher`
>   - Code -> `coder`
>   - Write -> `summarizer`, fallback `main`
>   - Organize -> `executor`
> - Add per-mode model override support in preferences, falling back to role routing when unset.
> - The selected mode panel must be keyboard accessible, closable with Escape, and must not cause composer overlap.
>
> ### 3. Build A Mounted Workspace File Explorer
> - Keep Docker constraints explicit: Rasputin can only browse folders mounted into the container or approved visible roots.
> - Extend workspace browsing so it returns both folders and files, not folders only.
> - Add safe file metadata:
>   - display name
>   - kind: folder/file
>   - relative path
>   - extension
>   - size
>   - modified time
>   - read-only/read-write state
>   - previewable flag
> - Rebuild Workspaces as a file-explorer-style page:
>   - left column: mounted roots / approved folders
>   - center: current folder contents
>   - top: breadcrumbs and search/filter
>   - right panel: selected item details and preview
> - Add read-only preview for safe text/code files under a size limit.
> - Do not add file mutation actions in this pass. Rename, move, reorganize, and write operations stay future/approval-gated work.
> - Fix active folder highlighting so clicking approved folders updates the selected visual state correctly.
>
> ### 4. Warmind-Inspired Visual Pass
> - Replace the remaining white box/card look in Home, Workspaces, Models, and mode UI with Rasputin shell surfaces.
> - Use graphite/dark technical surfaces, restrained amber-red accents, thin borders, and tighter spacing.
> - Bundle an open-licensed technical font locally for headings, nav, chips, and controls. Keep body text readable with a normal sans-serif stack.
> - Do not use Destiny/Bungie names, logos, icons, screenshots, or assets.
> - Fix visible overlap in:
>   - mode controls
>   - composer chips
>   - workspace folder cards
>   - workspace browser columns
>   - mobile sidebar/content layout
>
> ### 5. Improve Testing Readiness
> - The minimum testing flow should work end-to-end:
>   - start Docker container
>   - open Rasputin
>   - log in
>   - discover/test vLLM model
>   - see actual model ID in chat
>   - send a message
>   - switch modes through the right panel
>   - browse mounted folders/files
>   - preview safe files
>   - verify UI remains usable on desktop and mobile
> - Keep Home chat-first. Debug logs, task internals, and registry details stay outside the normal chat path.
>
> ## Public Interfaces
>
> ### Backend
> - Extend `POST /api/workspace/browse` to return folders and files with safe metadata.
> - Add `POST /api/workspace/preview-file` for read-only preview of safe text/code files inside approved roots.
> - Preserve existing workspace endpoints for backward compatibility.
> - Preserve existing model registry endpoints, but ensure discovery/repair data can support the actual model label in the frontend.
>
> ### Frontend
> - Replace hardcoded model display with actual model ID display.
> - Add persisted preferences for:
>   - selected mode
>   - per-mode model override
>   - active workspace/root
>   - workspace explorer layout state
> - Add or preserve test IDs:
>   - `active-model-chip`
>   - `chat-mode-chip`
>   - `mode-side-panel`
>   - `mode-option`
>   - `workspace-browser`
>   - `workspace-root-card`
>   - `workspace-file-row`
>   - `workspace-preview-panel`
>
> ## Test Plan
> - `npm.cmd run build`
> - `git diff --check`
> - Backend tests:
>   - workspace browse returns folders and files
>   - file preview rejects paths outside approved roots
>   - file preview rejects unsupported/large/binary files
>   - model registry exposes actual configured/discovered model IDs
> - UI tests:
>   - Home shows actual model ID, not `Main Local Model`
>   - mode chip opens/closes side panel
>   - mode selection updates composer without overlap
>   - workspace roots display friendly names
>   - folder navigation updates breadcrumbs and highlight state
>   - file preview works for safe text files
>   - mobile layout shows chat/composer without overlap
> - Visual screenshots:
>   - Home desktop
>   - Home mobile
>   - mode side panel
>   - Workspaces explorer
>   - Models page
>   - dark Warmind theme
>
> ## Assumptions
> - Keep the current React/Vite frontend and FastAPI backend.
> - Keep Docker as the normal runtime.
> - No native OS folder picker in this pass because Docker cannot access arbitrary host folders unless mounted.
> - No file writes, renames, moves, or reorganizing in this pass.
> - Use a bundled open-licensed font, not CDN fonts.
> - Use open-source AI wrappers only as interaction references, not as copied branding, terminology, or visual identity.
</details>

I inspected the current React/FastAPI implementation and the main issue is not just styling. The model label is hardcoded in the frontend, the mode selector is structurally prone to overlap, and the workspace API currently behaves more like a folder selector than a file explorer. I need to lock three product choices before I write the implementation plan.

> # Rasputin Testing-Stage UI/UX Stabilization Plan
>
> ## Summary
> Move Rasputin from early prototype to a usable testing build without replacing the current React/FastAPI/Docker architecture. The next pass focuses on the pieces blocking real testing: truthful model display, non-overlapping mode controls, a real mounted-folder explorer, stronger Warmind-inspired visual identity, and test coverage for the core local workflow.
>

> ### 1. Fix Model Truth In Chat

> - Friendly names may appear only as secondary labels, never as the source of truth.
> - If Rasputin is configured for `local-main` but vLLM exposes a different model, show a plain mismatch warning and provide a repair action.

>   - Code -> `coder`

>
> ### 3. Build A Mounted Workspace File Explorer
> - Keep Docker constraints explicit: Rasputin can only browse folders mounted into the container or approved visible roots.
> - Extend workspace browsing so it returns both folders and files, not folders only.
> - Add safe file metadata:

>   - kind: folder/file
>   - relative path
>   - extension

>   - read-only/read-write state
>   - previewable flag
> - Rebuild Workspaces as a file-explorer-style page:
>   - left column: mounted roots / approved folders
>   - center: current folder contents
>   - top: breadcrumbs and search/filter
>   - right panel: selected item details and preview
> - Add read-only preview for safe text/code files under a size limit.
> - Do not add file mutation actions in this pass. Rename, move, reorganize, and write operations stay future/approval-gated work.
> - Fix active folder highlighting so clicking approved folders updates the selected visual state correctly.
>
> ### 4. Warmind-Inspired Visual Pass
> - Replace the remaining white box/card look in Home, Workspaces, Models, and mode UI with Rasputin shell surfaces.
> - Use graphite/dark technical surfaces, restrained amber-red accents, thin borders, and tighter spacing.
> - Bundle an open-licensed technical font locally for headings, nav, chips, and controls. Keep body text readable with a normal sans-serif stack.
> - Do not use Destiny/Bungie names, logos, icons, screenshots, or assets.
> - Fix visible overlap in:
>   - mode controls
>   - composer chips
>   - workspace folder cards
>   - workspace browser columns
>   - mobile sidebar/content layout
>
> ### 5. Improve Testing Readiness
> - The minimum testing flow should work end-to-end:
>   - start Docker container
>   - open Rasputin
>   - log in
>   - discover/test vLLM model
>   - see actual model ID in chat
>   - send a message
>   - switch modes through the right panel
>   - browse mounted folders/files
>   - preview safe files
>   - verify UI remains usable on desktop and mobile
> - Keep Home chat-first. Debug logs, task internals, and registry details stay outside the normal chat path.
>
> ## Public Interfaces
>
> ### Backend
> - Extend `POST /api/workspace/browse` to return folders and files with safe metadata.
> - Add `POST /api/workspace/preview-file` for read-only preview of safe text/code files inside approved roots.
> - Preserve existing workspace endpoints for backward compatibility.
> - Preserve existing model registry endpoints, but ensure discovery/repair data can support the actual model label in the frontend.
>
> ### Frontend
> - Replace hardcoded model display with actual model ID display.
> - Add persisted preferences for:
>   - selected mode
>   - per-mode model override
>   - active workspace/root
>   - workspace explorer layout state
> - Add or preserve test IDs:
>   - `active-model-chip`
>   - `chat-mode-chip`
>   - `mode-side-panel`
>   - `mode-option`
>   - `workspace-browser`
>   - `workspace-root-card`
>   - `workspace-file-row`
>   - `workspace-preview-panel`
>
> ## Test Plan
> - `npm.cmd run build`
> - `git diff --check`
> - Backend tests:
>   - workspace browse returns folders and files
>   - file preview rejects paths outside approved roots
>   - file preview rejects unsupported/large/binary files
>   - model registry exposes actual configured/discovered model IDs
> - UI tests:
>   - Home shows actual model ID, not `Main Local Model`
>   - mode chip opens/closes side panel
>   - mode selection updates composer without overlap
>   - workspace roots display friendly names
>   - folder navigation updates breadcrumbs and highlight state
>   - file preview works for safe text files
>   - mobile layout shows chat/composer without overlap
> - Visual screenshots:
>   - Home desktop
>   - Home mobile
>   - mode side panel
>   - Workspaces explorer
>   - Models page
>   - dark Warmind theme
>
> ## Assumptions
> - Keep the current React/Vite frontend and FastAPI backend.
> - Keep Docker as the normal runtime.
> - No native OS folder picker in this pass because Docker cannot access arbitrary host folders unless mounted.
> - No file writes, renames, moves, or reorganizing in this pass.
> - Use a bundled open-licensed font, not CDN fonts.
> - Use open-source AI wrappers only as interaction references, not as copied branding, terminology, or visual identity.

Implemented the testing-stage UI/UX stabilization pass.

What changed:
- Chat now shows the actual configured/discovered model ID instead of hardcoded `Main Local Model`.
- Replaced the inline mode boxes with a compact `Mode` chip and a right-side mode panel.
- Added per-mode routing/override state in preferences.
- Rebuilt Workspaces into a mounted-folder explorer with roots, breadcrumbs, folder/file rows, selected item details, and safe read-only file preview.
- Extended backend workspace browsing to return files plus safe metadata.
- Added `POST /api/workspace/preview-file`.
- Added a bundled local technical font via `@fontsource/rajdhani`.
- Tightened the Warmind-style UI surfaces and removed more of the white Bootstrap-card look.
- Updated backend and Playwright tests for the new model display, mode panel, workspace browsing, and file preview behavior.

Validation passed:
- `npm.cmd run build`
- `powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File ./scripts/test.ps1`
- `powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File ./scripts/test.ps1 -Ui`
- `git diff --check`

One remaining note: the build still prints existing Bootstrap Sass deprecation warnings. They are non-blocking, but replacing the remaining Bootstrap dependency should stay on the cleanup list. The earlier 1024-token model context error is also still a backend/runtime issue to address next; this pass focused on the UI/workspace/model-display testing blockers.

Implemented the pass.

Changed:
- Workspaces spacing and layout are tightened.
- Workspaces now has a real folder-explorer flow plus a right-side Knowledge panel.
- RAG is no longer treated like a top-level separate app tab. It is now workspace-scoped through Workspaces, with Settings > Knowledge kept as an admin/global view.
- Top nav now removes the standalone Knowledge item.
- Workspace active/browsing highlight logic is fixed.
- Warsat now supports model strength profiles: `small`, `balanced`, `large`.
- Warsat launch plans now generate:
  - `docker run` preview
  - Composefile preview
  - Dockerfile preview
  - generated file-name previews
- Fixed a Warsat GGUF mount issue: `.gguf` plans now mount the parent model folder and point llama.cpp to `/models/model.gguf`.

Validation passed:
- `npm.cmd run build`

> what is the most efficient way for us to make changes to this

The most efficient way is to stop doing broad UI passes and switch to small, testable vertical slices.

Use this workflow:

1. **Pick one user-facing problem**
   Example: “Workspaces folder browser spacing is wrong” or “Warsat should save generated compose plans.”

2. **Define the expected behavior**
   Keep it concrete:
   - what screen changes
   - what backend endpoint changes, if any
   - what should not change
   - what test proves it works

3. **Patch only the affected files**
   For Rasputin, that usually means:
   - UI: `frontend-src/src/features/...`
   - styling: `frontend-src/src/styles/rasputin.css`
   - backend: `backend/...`
   - tests: `tests/testBackendSmoke.py`, `tests/ui/rasputinSmoke.spec.mjs`

4. **Run the minimum test first**
   - Frontend only: `npm.cmd run build`
   - Backend/API: `powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File ./scripts/test.ps1`
   - UI behavior: `powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File ./scripts/test.ps1 -Ui`

5. **Only then do visual polish**
   Use screenshots from:
   `test-results/rasputin-screenshots/`

For us specifically, the best change cadence is:

- **One feature branch per slice**
- **One focused commit per completed slice**
- **No more “make the whole UI better” passes unless we define exact screens**
- **Keep Playwright screenshots as the visual review source of truth**
- **Promote repeated UI patterns into reusable components only after they appear 2-3 times**

The next most efficient slices would be:

1. **Warsat persistence**
   Save generated compose/Dockerfile plans locally under ignored `data/warsat/plans`.

2. **Workspace indexing UX**
   Show per-workspace indexed status instead of global RAG doc/chunk counts.

3. **Model context/runtime guard**
   Fix the 1024-token model context problem properly so “hello” never overflows.

4. **Bootstrap removal**
   Start removing React-Bootstrap from visible production screens to reduce CSS weight and visual inconsistency.

Implemented the Warsat pass.

Changed:
- Expanded Warsat from a basic two-protocol picker into a recipe-style launcher with hardware profiles, runtime tuning, and container limits.
- Added tuning support to backend launch plans: context length, max model length, GPU utilization, GPU layers, tensor parallelism, dtype, quantization, KV cache dtype, batch size, parallel sequences, swap, memory cap, CPU cap, shared memory, and GPU device.
- Fixed the unreadable “approval required” warning by replacing Bootstrap warning badges with Rasputin-native high-contrast pills.
- Enriched vLLM and llama.cpp protocol metadata with best-use cases and tradeoffs.
- Updated generated Docker run and compose previews so they include the selected tuning and limits.

> explain to me why our repo is majority powershell and dockerfile whereas most other wrappers are majority python

<details><summary>3 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll verify the repo composition instead of guessing. GitHub language bars are line-count based and can be skewed heavily by generated files, scripts, Docker assets, vendored code, or large config files.
>
> File count says Python is actually the largest by file count, but language stats usually use lines, not file count. I’m checking line counts now because one large PowerShell script or Dockerfile can dominate the visible percentage.
>
> The tracked local repo is not majority PowerShell/Dockerfile by line count. It is mostly Python, JSON, React JSX, and CSS. If GitHub is showing otherwise, it is probably reading a different branch, stale cache, generated files, or files that are not tracked locally.
</details>

Locally, our repo is **not** majority PowerShell/Dockerfile.

Tracked line count right now is roughly:

- Python: `6071`
- JSON: `4120`
- JSX/React: `4115`
- CSS: `3442`
- PowerShell: `96`
- Dockerfile: `29`

So if GitHub is showing mostly PowerShell/Dockerfile, likely causes are:

1. **GitHub is reading a different branch**
   You’re currently on `codex/warsat-cookbook-foundation`, but GitHub language stats usually show the default branch.

2. **GitHub Linguist stats are cosmetic and weird**
   GitHub counts languages using its Linguist classifier. Small repos can get skewed if key app folders are ignored, untracked, generated, or not pushed.

3. **Our repo has Windows/Docker launch tooling**
   We use PowerShell because you’re testing on Windows, and Docker because Rasputin is meant to run locally in a controlled container. That does not mean the app is built in PowerShell/Dockerfile.

4. **Other wrappers are often backend-heavy**
   A lot of AI wrappers are mostly Python because they are FastAPI/Flask/agent-runtime projects with lighter frontend code. Rasputin is more full-stack: Python backend, React frontend, Docker runtime manager, local safety layer, Warsat model orchestration.

For production perception, we can add a `.gitattributes` file so GitHub’s language bar emphasizes real source code and ignores helper launch scripts if needed. But architecturally, the current split is normal for what Rasputin is becoming.

Say: **“approve replacing remote main with local Rasputin main”** and I’ll do the safe non-force merge/push path.

> whats the safest option that pushes our repo to the main tree of the shingenn5/rasputin repo?

<details><summary>1 previous message</summary>

> The safest path is not a force-push. It is to preserve the current remote `main` as a backup, then create a normal merge commit that records the remote `main` history while keeping the validated local Rasputin tree. That gives us a fast-forwardable push and a rollback point.
</details>

Safest direct option:

1. **Back up current GitHub `main` first**
   - Create `backup/main-before-rasputin-20260605` from `origin/main`.
   - This preserves whatever is currently on GitHub.

2. **Push our validated local tree to a review branch**
   - Push local `main` to something like `codex/main-ready`.
   - This gives you a remote copy before touching GitHub `main`.

3. **Merge remote `main` using an “ours” merge**
   - This records `origin/main` in history but keeps our local Rasputin files as the final tree.
   - No force push.
   - No deletion/import of stale remote files.
   - GitHub `main` becomes Rasputin, while old remote `main` remains recoverable.

4. **Push `main` normally**
   - Since the merge commit includes `origin/main` as a parent, the push should be accepted as a normal update.

The key command is:

```powershell
git merge -s ours --allow-unrelated-histories origin/main -m "Merge remote main while preserving Rasputin tree"
```

This is safer than force-pushing because it preserves history and creates a rollback path. It is still a real replacement of the GitHub `main` file tree, so I need explicit approval before doing it.

Say: **approve safe main replacement with backup branch**.

<details><summary>11 previous messages</summary>

> Context compaction
>
> Source: automatic
> Status: completed
>
> I’m going to run the approved safe path: verify local state, fetch remote `main`, create a backup branch from the current remote `main`, push that backup, push our validated tree to a review branch, then merge remote history with `ours` and push `main` normally.
>
> Local `main` is clean. I’m refreshing `origin/main` before creating the safety branch so the backup reflects the current remote state, not a stale local reference.
>
> Remote refs are current. Next I’m checking whether the backup branch name already exists so I don’t overwrite a previous safety point.
>
> No local backup branch with that name exists. I’m creating it from `origin/main` now.
>
> The local backup branch exists. I’m pushing it to GitHub now so the old remote `main` has a recoverable branch before we touch `main`.
>
> Backup is on GitHub. I’m also pushing the current Rasputin tree to a review branch before updating `main`, which gives us a second rollback/inspection point.
>
> Both safety branches are pushed. Now I’m recording the old remote `main` as a merge parent while preserving the validated local Rasputin tree. This avoids force-pushing and keeps the remote history recoverable.
>
> The merge commit is created and the working tree should still be the Rasputin tree. I’m doing the quick validation checks before pushing `main`.
>
> Validation is clean. I’m pushing `main` now; because the old remote `main` is included as a merge parent, this should be a normal non-force push.

</details>

Also the output bar in settings is effectively useless as the only thing it does is tell us that there is output. Also the themes needs to include more than just one theme, preferably based off bootswatch to allow for easier switching between themes
>
> Please make these changes and test these features wthout asking for permission, however do not run any sensitive commands in powershell.

<details><summary>71 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll make this as a focused product pass: chat folders, safer settings with explicit save/apply behavior, a useful Output settings section, and broader theme options inspired by Bootswatch without adding external CDN dependencies. I’ll inspect the current frontend/backend shape first, then patch and test locally.
>
> The app already has persisted preferences, sessions in SQLite, and settings sections wired through React. The missing pieces are mostly product behavior: session folder metadata, explicit dirty/saved state for Safety, more meaningful Output controls, and theme token support beyond the three current values.
>
> I’m reading the relevant backend and frontend files now. I’m specifically looking for where session rows are created, where preferences are saved, and how Settings currently submits security/output changes.
>
> Session storage is currently a flat `sessions` table, and preferences/security are JSON-backed under ignored `data/`. I’ll add chat folders as local metadata on sessions rather than touching the message model, so existing chats remain compatible.
>
> For chat folders, I’m going to add a small durable folder table and a nullable `folderId` on sessions. The UI can then group recent chats and let you file a session without changing message storage or breaking existing chat history.
>
> The settings UI still uses uncontrolled switches, so toggles visually change immediately even though the backend only changes on form submit. I’ll convert Safety to a draft/apply model with a dirty banner, Reset, and Save, which makes the “not saved yet” state explicit.
>
> Output settings are only showing the configured folder today. I’ll make that section useful by adding the actual Markdown export folder, local storage behavior, a test/export permission check, and a save flow for changing the output folder when file-write permission is enabled.
>
> For themes, I’ll keep the current Rasputin identity but add Bootswatch-style theme presets as local CSS variable maps. That gives fast switching without pulling remote CSS or changing the app shell architecture.

Implemented the requested pass.

What changed:
- Added durable chat folders:
  - New `chat_folders` table.
  - Sessions now support `folderId`.
  - Added `/api/chat-folders` and `/api/sessions/{sessionId}/folder`.
  - Sidebar now filters recent chats by folder.
  - Sessions view can create folders, filter chats, move chats, and resume chats.

- Made Safety settings safer:
  - Switches are now draft-only until `Save Safety`.
  - Added visible unsaved/saved state.
  - Added `Reset`.
  - Added clearer descriptions for risky settings.

- Made Output settings useful:
  - Editable Markdown export folder.
  - Shows resolved local path, export format, and Safety write permission.
  - Save gives visible confirmation.
  - Save is blocked when file write is disabled.

- Added more themes:
  - `Rasputin Light`
  - `Rasputin Dark`
  - `High Contrast`
  - `Slate Ops`
  - `Cyborg Redline`
  - `Darkly Forge`

> where is user data being stored? do we have a database being hosted to hold logins and preferences for themes, settings, etc?

<details><summary>3 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll verify this from the current backend code rather than guessing. I’m checking the runtime store, preferences, auth, and Docker volume paths so the answer maps to the actual build.
>
> I found the relevant storage paths. I’m checking the exact schema/list of SQLite tables now so I can separate what is in the database from what is still JSON-file based.
>
> Storage is split: SQLite for runtime history, JSON files for configuration/auth, and browser localStorage for a small amount of client-side preference bootstrapping. I’m checking auth persistence details now because login storage is the sensitive part.
</details>

Yes. Rasputin currently uses **local persistent storage**, not an external hosted database.

The main storage location is:

`C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\data`

Inside Docker, that maps to:

`/app/data`

From [docker-compose.yml](<./docker-compose.yml:22>):

```yaml
volumes:
  - ./data:/app/data
```

So when the container restarts, your data stays on your machine in `./data`.

**What Uses SQLite**
Rasputin has a local SQLite database at:

`C:\Users\elliott\OneDrive\Documents\WrapperProject\data\rasputin.db`

Defined in [runtime_store.py](<./backend/runtime_store.py:10>).

It stores the agent/runtime data:

- chat sessions
- messages
- tasks
- task events/logs
- tool calls
- approvals
- outputs/artifacts
- agent traces
- memory items
- skills
- schedules
- full-text search tables

This is not a hosted database server. It is just a local `.db` file.

**What Uses JSON Files**
Some app configuration is still stored as local JSON files:

- `data/auth.json`
  Admin username, salt, and password hash. No plaintext password after first boot.

- `data/preferences.json`
  Theme, sidebar collapsed state, selected model, active workspace, selected mode, active chat folder, etc.

- `data/models.json`
  Registered model/runtime config.

- `data/security.json`
  Safety/privacy settings.

- `data/workspace.json`
  Approved folders / workspace state.

- `data/audit.jsonl`
  Audit log events.

- `data/output.json`
  Output/export settings.

- `data/rag_index.json` and `data/graph.json`
  Current local RAG/Graphify index data.

**What Is Stored In Browser**
The browser currently keeps only small UI boot preferences:

- `rasputin-theme`
- `rasputin-sidebar-collapsed`

Those are in `localStorage` so the page can apply theme/sidebar state immediately before the backend finishes loading. The backend also saves preferences in `data/preferences.json`.

Login uses an HTTP-only cookie named:

`rasputin_session`

The actual session tokens are currently held in backend memory, so logging in does **not** create a durable session table. If the server/container restarts, active login sessions are lost and you may need to log in again.

**Important**
`data/` is ignored by git, so these private files should not be committed:

[.gitignore](<./.gitignore:30>)

Current architecture summary:

```text
Browser localStorage
  small UI boot preferences only

Browser cookie
  login session token

data/auth.json
  admin account + password hash

data/preferences.json
  user settings/theme/sidebar/model/workspace preferences

data/rasputin.db
  chats, messages, tasks, memory, traces, approvals, skills, schedules

data/*.json
  model registry, safety config, workspace config, RAG/Graphify indexes, audit logs
```

For production, I would eventually consolidate more of this into SQLite, especially preferences, workspace registry, model registry, and auth metadata. Right now it works, but it is a hybrid local-storage design rather than one clean database-backed settings system.

Started the consolidation.

What changed:
- User preferences now persist in SQLite under `runtime_kv.userPreferences` inside [data/rasputin.db](<./backend/preferences.py:64>).
- Existing `data/preferences.json` is now treated as a legacy import source only. New preference saves no longer write to that JSON file.
- Added a repo safety checker: [check-repo-safety.ps1](<./scripts/check-repo-safety.ps1:1>).
- Added `npm run checkRepoSafety` in [package.json](<./package.json:12>).
- Wired the safety check into the Docker test harness: [test.ps1](<./scripts/test.ps1:25>).
- Updated backend smoke tests to assert preferences are actually stored in SQLite: [testBackendSmoke.py](<./tests/testBackendSmoke.py:58>).
- Updated README repo hygiene/storage notes: [README.md](<./README.md:23>).

Validation passed:
- `npm run checkRepoSafety`
- Docker test harness: `19 tests OK`
- `git diff --check`
- Main Rasputin container rebuilt and healthy at `http://127.0.0.1:8787/`

I also verified the running container can import existing preferences into SQLite. Next consolidation targets should be `security.json`, `workspace.json`, `models.json`, and `output.json`. I would leave `auth.json` until after we decide whether we want auth in SQLite or a deliberately separate local credential file.

> {
>   "title": "Inspect mobile Warsat overflow",
>   "timeout_ms": 120000,
>   "code": "const { chromium } = await import('playwright');\nconst browser = await chromium.launch({ headless: true });\nconst page = await browser.newPage({ viewport: { width: 390, height: 844 } });\nawait page.goto('http://127.0.0.1:8877/', { waitUntil: 'domcontentloaded' });\nawait page.waitForSelector('body[data-ready=\"true\"]', { timeout: 15000 });\nawait page.click('[data-testid=\"mobile-sidebar-toggle\"]');\nawait page.click('[data-testid=\"nav-warsat\"]');\nawait page.waitForSelector('#warsatView.active', { timeout: 15000 });\nconst data = await page.evaluate(() => {\n  const header = document.querySelector('#warsatView .page-header');\n  const all = [...header.querySelectorAll('*')].map((node) => {\n    const rect = node.getBoundingClientRect();\n    const style = getComputedStyle(node);\n    return {\n      tag: node.tagName,\n      cls: node.className,\n      text: node.textContent?.trim().replace(/\\s+/g, ' ').slice(0, 80),\n      width: Math.round(rect.width),\n      left: Math.round(rect.left),\n      right: Math.round(rect.right),\n      scrollWidth: Math.round(node.scrollWidth),\n      clientWidth: Math.round(node.clientWidth),\n      display: style.display,\n      flexBasis: style.flexBasis,\n      flex: style.flex,\n      whiteSpace: style.whiteSpace,\n    };\n  }).filter((item) => item.right > window.innerWidth + 2 || item.scrollWidth - item.clientWidth > 2 || item.width > window.innerWidth);\n  const hr = header.getBoundingClientRect();\n  return {\n    viewport: window.innerWidth,\n    header: { width: Math.round(hr.width), scrollWidth: header.scrollWidth, clientWidth: header.clientWidth, css: getComputedStyle(header).cssText },\n    offenders: all,\n    html: header.outerHTML,\n  };\n});\nawait browser.close();\nnodeRepl.write(JSON.stringify(data, null, 2));"

Implemented the Warsat deployment path.

What changed:
- Added `POST /api/warsat/deploy`.
- Warsat now validates a generated launch plan before executing Docker.
- Deployment is gated by:
  - `allowDockerControl`
  - `allowModelRegistryEdit`
  - Docker CLI availability inside the wrapper
  - localhost-only binding
  - no host networking
  - no privileged Docker options
  - `no-new-privileges`
- The UI now has a real `Pull and start` action after creating a Warsat launch plan.
- Successful deploys register the new model in Rasputin’s model registry.
- Rasputin was restarted with the Docker-control overlay:
  - `rasputin-rasputin-wrapper-1` is healthy on `127.0.0.1:8787`
  - Docker CLI works inside the wrapper
  - Safety still has Docker control disabled, intentionally

Validation:
- Docker rebuild passed.
- Backend compile check passed inside the rebuilt container.
- Backend smoke tests passed: `20 tests OK`.
- `git diff --check` passed, only CRLF warnings.

To run your first Warsat test:
1. Open `http://127.0.0.1:8787`.
2. Go to `Settings` / `Safety`.
3. Enable Docker control and save.
4. Go to `Models` / `Warsat`.
5. Create a vLLM plan:
   - Protocol: `vLLM CUDA OpenAI Server`
   - Model id: `Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct`
   - Host port: `8031`
   - Profile: `Low VRAM` or `Balanced`
6. Click `Create plan`.
7. Review the plan, then click `Pull and start`.
8. After it starts, go to `Models`, refresh/discover/test the new Warsat model, then select it in chat.

Important constraint: your `models/` folder currently only has `.gitkeep`, so llama.cpp/GGUF deployment cannot run until you place a `.gguf` model in the mounted `models/` folder.

Odysseus is a broad target: its README advertises chat, agent tools, Cookbook model management, deep research, model comparison, documents, memory/skills, email, notes/tasks, calendar, mobile/PWA, file uploads, PDF, web search, presets, sessions, and 2FA. That is the benchmark we should measure against, not just “does the page load.” Source: [Odysseus README](https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/blob/main/README.md).

Needs work:
- Commit or deliberately shelve the current Warsat changes before building more.
- Update stale docs: [README.md](./README.md) and [docs/WARSAT_PROTOCOLS_PLAN.md](./docs/WARSAT_PROTOCOLS_PLAN.md) still describe Warsat as plan-only.
- Fix stale Models copy: [ModelsView.jsx](./frontend-src/src/features/models/ModelsView.jsx) still says deploy flow is pending.
- Run a real Warsat deploy test before calling the feature functional.

**P1: Model Runtime / Warsat**
Works:
- Model registry supports discover, repair, test, GGUF scan/import, start/stop/log routes.
- Warsat now has protocols, plan generation, deploy route, Docker command validation, and registry upsert in [backend/warsat/__init__.py](./backend/warsat/__init__.py).
- vLLM image is already local, so image pull should not redownload 35GB.
- Safety still blocks Docker deploy until intentionally enabled.

Needs work:
- No `.gguf` exists in `models/`, so llama.cpp cannot be tested yet.
- Actual vLLM Warsat deployment has not been proven from the UI.
- Warsat deploy is gated by Safety, but not yet routed through the persistent approval queue. That is a serious trust gap.
- Add stop/restart/logs/status controls specifically for Warsat containers.
- Add hardware inventory: GPU visibility, VRAM, Docker GPU runtime, model cache status.

**P2: Safety / Trust**
Works:
- Auth, password change, session cookie, safety flags, audit log, local endpoint guard, and workspace path checks exist.
- Risky file/web/Docker concepts are represented in backend permissions.
- Approvals system exists.

Needs work:
- Warsat deploy must create an approval record before pulling/running.
- Approval IDs need one-time execution enforcement across all high-risk actions.
- Docker socket access is powerful; UI needs clearer warnings and current runtime status.
- Security docs need a real threat model before public repo release.

**P3: Workspaces / Knowledge**
Works:
- Workspaces page has mounted-root browser, file/folder listing, breadcrumbs, previewable text files, active workspace selection, and mount-plan preview.
- Index button writes RAG to `data/rag_index.json` and Graphify to `data/graph.json`.
- UI now explains where indexed data sits.
- Workspace code is in [WorkspacesView.jsx](./frontend-src/src/features/workspaces/WorkspacesView.jsx) and [workspace.py](./backend/workspace.py).

Needs work:
- RAG is still lightweight hash-vector JSON, not Chroma/LanceDB-grade retrieval.
- Graphify is basic relationship extraction, not a deep code/document knowledge graph.
- PDF, DOCX, XLSX ingestion is not production-ready.
- Mounting new host folders still requires Docker/compose awareness. No native folder picker yet.

**P4: Agentic Runtime**
Works:
- Durable SQLite runtime exists for sessions, tasks, messages, approvals, tool calls, traces, outputs, memory, skills, schedules.
- Tasks support concurrent runs, pause/resume/cancel, SSE updates, task details drawer.
- Basic Plan -> Execute -> Reflect exists in [agent.py](./backend/agent.py).
- Sub-agent spawning exists.

Needs work:
- Agent tooling is still shallow compared to opencode-level workflows.
- No robust shell/test/patch loop yet.
- Coding workflow does not yet support model-proposed patch -> diff preview -> user approval -> apply -> test.
- Sub-agents are functional but not deeply isolated workspaces/tool contexts yet.

**P5: Product/UI**
Works:
- Home is now usable.
- Sidebar, chat folders, themes, model health gate, mode panel, task drawer, and responsive workspaces are present.
- UI smoke coverage exists in [rasputinSmoke.spec.mjs](./tests/ui/rasputinSmoke.spec.mjs).

Needs work:
- Models and Warsat should be merged into one coherent model-management flow.
- Activity needs continued polish for long-running/multi-agent work.
- Accessibility needs a full keyboard/screen-reader pass after functionality stabilizes.
- Missing Odysseus-level modules: document editor, compare, deep research, email, calendar, notes/tasks, file upload/PDF viewer, PWA/mobile polish.

**Recommended Order**
1. Clean current dirty state: update stale docs/UI copy, run tests, commit Warsat deploy foundation.
2. Run one real Warsat deploy test with a small vLLM model on a new port.
3. Add approval-queue enforcement to Warsat deploy/stop/restart/log actions.
4. Merge Models + Warsat into one production model-management experience.
5. Upgrade RAG/Graphify and add document ingestion.
6. Build the opencode-style coding workbench.
7. Add document editor, compare, deep research, and broader workflow modules.

Right now Rasputin has a solid skeleton and several working subsystems. The biggest gap versus Odysseus is not UI anymore; it is depth of finished workflows. Warsat and agentic coding are the two highest-leverage areas to harden next.

Implemented the requested pass.

What changed:
- Moved safe first-paint/theme browser logic into [frontend-src/index.html](./frontend-src/index.html), exposed as `window.rasputinTheme`.
- Simplified React theme handling in [frontend-src/src/app/App.jsx](./frontend-src/src/app/App.jsx) so it no longer duplicates theme-color/favicon logic.
- Fixed the Warsat top “Runtime recipes / Docker control / Execution” area in [frontend-src/src/features/runtime/RuntimeViews.jsx](./frontend-src/src/features/runtime/RuntimeViews.jsx). It now uses a proper summary grid instead of Bootstrap row gutters bleeding into the header/content boundary.
- Reduced some custom Warsat visual treatment in [frontend-src/src/styles/rasputin.css](./frontend-src/src/styles/rasputin.css), including removing the extra gradient override on every Warsat card and leaning on Bootstrap utility classes for the new summary tiles.
- Kept the actual React app JavaScript in component files. Moving core React/Vite component logic into inline `<script>` tags would make the frontend harder to maintain, harder to test, and worse for codebase readability.

One note: the Sass warnings during build are Bootstrap deprecation warnings from Bootstrap’s own SCSS imports. They are not failures.

Implemented and committed: `112f364 Expand Warsat runtime and local model support`.

What changed:
- Warsat now has a clearer managed runtime section with container listing, logs, and approval-gated stop/restart controls.
- Added an Ollama OpenAI-compatible Warsat protocol: [ollamaOpenaiServer.json](./warsat/protocols/ollamaOpenaiServer.json).
- Models tab now has a “Local Endpoint” form for any localhost OpenAI-compatible model server: LM Studio, Ollama `/v1`, text-generation-webui, vLLM, llama.cpp server, or a custom local `/v1` endpoint.
- Backend model registration now normalizes endpoints like `127.0.0.1:1234` into `http://127.0.0.1:1234/v1` and still blocks remote URLs while privacy lock is enabled.
- Added backend routes for Warsat runtimes/logs/stop/restart in [backend/main.py](./backend/main.py).
- Updated docs and tests around Warsat protocols and local model registration.

Validation:
- `npm.cmd run build` passed.
- `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui` passed: backend tests, live smoke, and all 9 Playwright UI tests.
- Repo safety check passed.
- Rebuilt and restarted the normal container.
- Rasputin is healthy at `http://127.0.0.1:8787`.

Caveat: “any localhosted model” currently means any local server exposing an OpenAI-compatible `/v1` API. Native non-OpenAI APIs can be added later as adapters, but this is the right compatibility layer for now.

The repo is clean after the commit. I did not push to GitHub.

Implemented and committed.

What changed:
- Added OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, and generic OpenAI-compatible remote API provider support.
- API keys are no longer stored in `models.json`.
- Keys can come from env vars like `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `GEMINI_API_KEY`.
- If pasted through the UI, keys are saved only in ignored local secret storage at `data/model_secrets.json`.
- `.gitignore` and `.dockerignore` now explicitly ignore `model_secrets.json`.
- Frontend Models tab now has an external API provider registration panel.
- Remote providers stay blocked unless Safety disables Privacy Lock and enables Remote Models.

Validation:
- Full harness passed: 24 backend tests, 9 UI tests.
- Docker container is running healthy at `http://127.0.0.1:8787`.
- Repo working tree is clean.
- Commit created: `aca328f Add remote API model providers`.

Important note: `data/model_secrets.json` is local ignored storage, not an encrypted OS vault. For a production release, the next step is wiring this into Docker secrets or OS credential storage.

> lets work on the warsat workflow. I want the readability of warsat to be improved as well as the current generated models. I don't want to have to scroll constantly to find what i want. Also, when i created a plan and requested deploy approval there was no option to actually approve deployment.

<details><summary>39 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll trace the Warsat UI and backend approval path first, then make a focused pass: reduce scrolling, clarify generated model plans, and wire the deployment approval action into the UI instead of leaving it as a dead end.
>
> The backend already creates a `warsat_deploy` approval, but the copy says to approve elsewhere and rerun deployment. The missing piece is frontend state: it needs to show the approval, approve it, and then replay deploy with the approval id.
>
> The main UI debt is concentrated in one React component: `WarsatView`. I’m opening that section with line numbers now so edits stay surgical.
>
> The current deployment flow only reuses an approval id if `deployWarsatPlan` is called again after approval. I’m going to expose that directly in Warsat: request approval, approve from the launch panel, then deploy from the same panel.
>
> For readability, I’m going to restructure Warsat into a two-column operations layout on wider screens: left side for active containers and recipe form, right side for the current generated plan. Advanced protocol cards and long compose previews will collapse by default so the page stops turning into a scroll hunt.
>
> The backend approval list returns more than pending, so once Warsat approves a request the same launch card can detect the approved state and enable deployment. I’m adding the buttons and tightening the layout now.
>
> The data path supports this. I’m changing only the frontend interaction and layout; the deploy safety checks stay server-side and unchanged.
>
> I’m also changing the status copy so it no longer tells you to leave Warsat just to approve something Warsat created. The same launch plan should own that workflow.
>
