> The default container is rebuilt and restarted without Docker-control. I’m checking health and final repo state.
</details>

Implemented and committed: `bd3d900 Polish Warsat deployment workflow`.

What changed:
- Warsat no longer wastes space when there is no launch plan.
- Generated launch plans now sit in a right-side plan rail on wide screens, then stack cleanly on split/mobile screens.
- Added a compact generated-model summary: model, container, endpoint, profile.
- Collapsed the hardware presets, runtime protocol library, command preview, and compose preview by default so you are not constantly scrolling.
- Added the missing deployment approval controls inside Warsat:
  - `Approve deploy`
  - `Deny`
  - then `Run approved deploy`
- Updated deployment status copy so it no longer tells you to leave Warsat just to approve a Warsat-created deployment.

Validation:
- `npm.cmd run build` passed.
- Full `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui` harness passed: 24 backend tests, 9 UI tests.
- Default Rasputin container rebuilt and is healthy at `http://127.0.0.1:8787`.

I did not restart with the `docker-control` overlay because that mounts Docker control into the wrapper and is a sensitive capability. The UI is updated now; actual container deployment testing still requires you to intentionally start Rasputin with Docker control enabled.

> okay, im going to be honest, before we even begin working on functionality we really need to get this GUI locked down, and currently i still dont feel that its accessibility and usability is not up to par with what i expect. The home page is the one that needs the least work, although im still not a fan of the model select and mode select buttons. I also want to make sure the side bar on the left actually changes with the themes in the settings.
>
> I want to generate a multi optioned plan that prepares different previews of the GUI then allows me to select which one i like best. i want multiple options for each tab and panel.

<details><summary>2 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll ground this in the current React UI before planning. Specifically I’m checking the shell, sidebar/theme wiring, tab components, and the home composer controls so the plan is based on what actually exists.
>
> Plan
>
> # Rasputin GUI Preview Lab Plan
>
> ## Summary
> Build a non-production “Design Lab” inside Rasputin so we can compare multiple GUI directions before locking the final interface. The goal is not to rewrite functionality; it is to preview, test, and select better layouts for every major screen while keeping the current React/FastAPI architecture intact.
>
> Chosen defaults:
> - Preview format: in-app Design Lab.
> - Scope: full shell, all major tabs, common panels/drawers.
> - No backend behavior changes unless needed for saved UI preferences.
> - Accessibility and theme behavior are first-class acceptance criteria.
>
> ## Key Changes
>
> ### 1. Add A Design Lab View
> - Add a hidden/internal GUI preview area accessible from `Settings > Appearance > Open Design Lab`.
> - The Design Lab shows three complete UI directions side-by-side or one-at-a-time:
>   - **Warmind Console**: darker, technical, high-density, strong amber/red accents.
>   - **Operator Desk**: cleaner professional workbench, more spacing, clearer panels.
>   - **Archive Studio**: calmer document/chat workspace, best for readability and long sessions.
> - Add a preview toolbar:
>   - screen selector: Home, Workspaces, Activity, Models, Warsat, Settings, Task Drawer, Model Panel, Mode Panel.
>   - viewport selector: desktop, split-screen, tablet, mobile.
>   - theme selector: all current themes.
>   - accessibility overlay: focus states, contrast warnings, keyboard path notes.
> - Store preview-only choices in local state/localStorage until explicitly applied.
>
> ### 2. Generate Multiple Options Per Screen
> - **Shell/sidebar**
>   - Option A: current left rail improved with theme-aware colors, stronger active states, better chat folder spacing.
>   - Option B: compact icon rail plus expandable chat/session drawer.
>   - Option C: persistent sidebar for desktop, bottom nav/drawer hybrid for mobile.
> - **Home**
>   - Option A: keep current chat layout but redesign model/mode buttons as compact segmented chips.
>   - Option B: combine model + mode into one “Run Config” button with a single right panel.
>   - Option C: bottom composer with inline model dropdown and mode menu, no floating panels unless advanced.
> - **Workspaces**
>   - Option A: file explorer layout with roots left, files center, preview right.
>   - Option B: simplified workspace cards first, explorer opens on selection.
>   - Option C: split “Folders / Knowledge / Mounts” into clearer sub-tabs.
> - **Activity**
>   - Option A: run timeline with task drawer.
>   - Option B: kanban lanes for queued/running/paused/done/error.
>   - Option C: session-centered activity grouped by chat.
> - **Models**
>   - Option A: active model first, advanced registry collapsed.
>   - Option B: runtime cards grouped by local/API/Warsat.
>   - Option C: model health dashboard with “fix next” actions.
> - **Warsat**
>   - Option A: current plan builder with sticky launch plan.
>   - Option B: stepper workflow: choose runtime, tune, review, approve, deploy.
>   - Option C: recipe library first, selected recipe opens a focused builder.
> - **Settings**
>   - Option A: current left subnav refined.
>   - Option B: card grid landing page into focused settings pages.
>   - Option C: grouped accordion pages for smaller screens.
> - **Panels/drawers**
>   - Model selector, mode selector, task details, approvals, and output panels each get three layout variants matching the selected direction.
>
> ### 3. Theme And Accessibility Repair
> - Make sidebar colors fully driven by the active theme tokens, not hardcoded dark rail values.
> - Add theme-specific sidebar variables:
>   - rail background
>   - rail text
>   - muted rail text
>   - active nav background
>   - active nav border
>   - focus ring
>   - chat/session row background
> - Audit all interactive controls:
>   - visible focus ring
>   - accessible names
>   - keyboard open/close behavior
>   - Escape closes panels
>   - focus returns to opener
>   - `aria-current` for active nav
>   - `aria-live` for status and send-disabled messages.
> - Add a Design Lab accessibility checklist per preview screen.
>
> ### 4. Selection And Apply Flow
> - In Design Lab, allow:
>   - choose global direction
>   - override individual tabs if desired
>   - save as “Candidate A/B/C”
>   - export screenshots for review
> - Add a final “Apply selected GUI” action that writes UI preferences:
>   - `uiShellVariant`
>   - `homeVariant`
>   - `workspaceVariant`
>   - `activityVariant`
>   - `modelsVariant`
>   - `warsatVariant`
>   - `settingsVariant`
>   - `panelVariant`
> - Production screens read those preferences but default to current behavior until applied.
>
> ## Public Interfaces
> - Add frontend-only preference fields to existing preferences save/load:
>   - `uiShellVariant`
>   - `tabUiVariants`
>   - `panelUiVariant`
>   - `designLabLastScreen`
>   - `designLabViewport`
> - No new model, workspace, task, or Warsat backend APIs are required.
> - Add stable test ids:
>   - `design-lab-view`
>   - `design-lab-screen-select`
>   - `design-lab-variant-option`
>   - `design-lab-theme-select`
>   - `design-lab-viewport-select`
>   - `design-lab-apply`
>   - `design-lab-accessibility-checks`
>
> ## Test Plan
> - Build:
>   - `npm.cmd run build`
>   - `git diff --check`
> - UI smoke:
>   - Design Lab opens from Appearance settings.
>   - Every major screen preview renders for all three variants.
>   - Theme switching updates sidebar and preview surfaces.
>   - Applying a variant persists across reload/login.
>   - Production UI remains unchanged until a variant is applied.
> - Accessibility:
>   - keyboard navigation through Design Lab controls.
>   - focus visible on all preview controls.
>   - Escape closes preview panels.
>   - sidebar active state is announced and visually clear.
>   - contrast passes for default dark, light, and high contrast themes.
> - Visual QA:
>   - screenshots for each variant on Home, Workspaces, Activity, Models, Warsat, Settings, and mobile Home.
>   - screenshot comparison verifies no obvious overlap or horizontal overflow.
>
> ## Assumptions
> - We will not replace React/Vite or the current backend.
> - We will not make the Design Lab public-facing by default.
> - We will preview first, then apply only after you pick the preferred direction.
> - We will keep the Home page mostly intact, focusing there on model/mode controls and theme-aware polish.
> - The first implementation should optimize comparison speed over perfect final styling.
</details>

I found the current shape: one React shell, theme options already in constants, sidebar is a custom component, and model/mode controls are side panels from Home. I’m checking the theme CSS now because your sidebar concern is probably variable coverage rather than component logic.

> no, i dont want to build the design lab inside rasputin, i would rather build it on a seperate test container called like RasputinTest that way we have isolated devlopment, deploy branches, etc.

<details><summary>2 previous messages</summary>

> Understood. I’ll revise the plan around an isolated `RasputinTest` preview environment instead of adding Design Lab into the production app. I’m going to inspect the current Docker/branch/test setup so the plan uses the repo’s actual deployment paths.
>
> Plan
>
> # Isolated RasputinTest GUI Preview Plan
>
> ## Summary
> Build the GUI preview system outside the production Rasputin container. Instead of adding a Design Lab inside Rasputin, create an isolated `RasputinTest` preview workflow with its own Compose project, port, test data, preview routes, and deploy branches. This lets us compare multiple GUI directions without risking the main local app or production UI state.
>
> Chosen defaults:
> - Container/project name: `rasputin-gui-test`.
> - Browser URL: `http://127.0.0.1:8899`.
> - Production Rasputin stays on `http://127.0.0.1:8787`.
> - Preview branches use `codex/gui-preview/...`.
> - Preview code lives in the frontend only and is excluded from production routing unless `RASPUTIN_UI_PREVIEW=1`.
>
> ## Key Changes
>
> ### 1. Add An Isolated Preview Container
> - Add `docker-compose.gui-test.yml` with:
>   - project name `rasputin-gui-test`
>   - service `rasputin-gui-test`
>   - image `rasputin-wrapper:gui-test`
>   - port `127.0.0.1:${RASPUTIN_GUI_TEST_PORT:-8899}:8787`
>   - environment `RASPUTIN_ENV=gui-test`, `RASPUTIN_TEST_AUTH_BYPASS=1`, `RASPUTIN_UI_PREVIEW=1`
>   - isolated volumes under `./testdata/gui-preview/*`
>   - read-only `./models:/app/models:ro`
>   - no Docker socket mount by default
> - Add scripts:
>   - `scripts/start-gui-preview.ps1`
>   - `scripts/stop-gui-preview.ps1`
>   - optional `scripts/test-gui-preview.ps1`
> - Keep existing `docker-compose.test.yml` for automated harness tests.
>
> ### 2. Add A Preview-Only Frontend Entry Path
> - Add a preview shell that renders only when `RASPUTIN_UI_PREVIEW=1` is exposed through bootstrap/config.
> - The preview shell must not replace production views.
> - Add URL routes or hash routes inside the test container:
>   - `/preview/home`
>   - `/preview/workspaces`
>   - `/preview/activity`
>   - `/preview/models`
>   - `/preview/warsat`
>   - `/preview/settings`
>   - `/preview/panels`
> - Each preview route renders mocked but realistic local data:
>   - running tasks
>   - approvals
>   - workspace roots/files
>   - model health states
>   - Warsat plans
>   - chat folders and sessions.
> - Mock data should be static frontend fixtures, not pulled from your real `data/`.
>
> ### 3. Generate Multiple GUI Options Per Tab And Panel
> - Build three selectable directions:
>   - **Option A: Warmind Console**: darker technical command center, compact density, stronger amber-red active states.
>   - **Option B: Operator Desk**: clean professional workbench, better spacing, less visual noise.
>   - **Option C: Archive Studio**: calmer readability-first layout for documents, workspaces, memory, and long chats.
> - For each direction, provide variants for:
>   - sidebar and chat folder list
>   - Home model selector and mode selector
>   - Workspaces explorer
>   - Activity/runs view
>   - Models tab
>   - Warsat workflow
>   - Settings shell
>   - task details drawer
>   - model selector panel
>   - mode selector panel
>   - approval cards.
> - Add a preview controller:
>   - screen selector
>   - variant selector
>   - theme selector
>   - viewport width presets
>   - accessibility checklist toggle.
>
> ### 4. Fix Theme-Aware Sidebar In The Preview First
> - Add sidebar-specific CSS variables:
>   - `--ras-sidebar-bg`
>   - `--ras-sidebar-bg-soft`
>   - `--ras-sidebar-text`
>   - `--ras-sidebar-muted`
>   - `--ras-sidebar-active-bg`
>   - `--ras-sidebar-active-border`
>   - `--ras-sidebar-hover-bg`
> - Wire every current theme to those variables.
> - Preview all themes against all sidebar variants.
> - After the selected design wins, port the chosen sidebar variable system into production.
>
> ### 5. Branch And Selection Workflow
> - Create one branch per major preview direction:
>   - `codex/gui-preview-warmind-console`
>   - `codex/gui-preview-operator-desk`
>   - `codex/gui-preview-archive-studio`
> - Also create one integration branch:
>   - `codex/gui-preview-hub`
> - The preview test container runs from the integration branch and can switch displayed variants without rebuilding.
> - After you select a preferred direction:
>   - create `codex/gui-apply-selected-shell`
>   - port only the chosen components/styles into production views
>   - remove or keep preview fixtures behind `RASPUTIN_UI_PREVIEW=1`.
>
> ## Public Interfaces
> - Add optional backend/bootstrap field:
>   - `uiPreviewEnabled: true | false`
> - Add frontend-only preview fixture types:
>   - `PreviewScreen`
>   - `PreviewVariant`
>   - `PreviewTheme`
>   - `PreviewViewport`
> - Add npm scripts:
>   - `preview:gui` to build/run the GUI test container if practical
>   - `test:gui-preview` for preview smoke tests.
> - No production task/model/workspace/Warsat API changes.
>
> ## Test Plan
> - Container:
>   - `docker compose -f docker-compose.gui-test.yml up --build -d`
>   - GUI preview opens at `http://127.0.0.1:8899`
>   - production Rasputin remains available at `http://127.0.0.1:8787`
>   - preview uses only `testdata/gui-preview`
> - UI preview tests:
>   - each preview route loads.
>   - all three variants render for every major tab/panel.
>   - theme selector changes sidebar and main surfaces.
>   - viewport presets show no horizontal overflow.
>   - keyboard navigation works through preview controller, sidebar, panels, and cards.
> - Regression:
>   - existing `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui` still passes.
>   - production build does not expose preview routes unless `RASPUTIN_UI_PREVIEW=1`.
>   - no real user data appears in screenshots or fixtures.
> - Visual output:
>   - generate screenshot folders:
>     - `test-results/gui-preview/warmind-console`
>     - `test-results/gui-preview/operator-desk`
>     - `test-results/gui-preview/archive-studio`
>
> ## Assumptions
> - `RasputinTest` is an isolated preview environment, not a production feature.
> - We will use fake/test fixture data only.
> - Docker-control remains disabled in the GUI preview container.
> - The selected GUI direction will be applied in a later production branch after you compare the previews.
> - The first implementation should optimize side-by-side comparison and accessibility validation, not final production polish.
</details>

There is already a `docker-compose.test.yml`, so the isolated preview plan can reuse that pattern rather than inventing a second runtime model. I’m checking what it currently does before producing the revised plan.

Implemented the requested Rasputin Candidate changes.

Updated:
- Workspaces: replaced the index/local-info cards with a cleaner mounted-folder explorer, fixed the left panel formatting, and kept folder/file details focused on actual actions.
- Activity: added more top buffer before the first run row.
- Models: rebuilt it as a model deployment catalog for Warsat, with use-case categories, VRAM target, runtime filter, model cards, and `Send to Warsat` actions.
- Used the current models.dev API shape as the basis: `https://models.dev/api.json`, `models.json`, and `catalog.json` expose provider/model metadata suitable for this catalog workflow. Source: [models.dev README](https://github.com/anomalyco/models.dev).

> The backend already has the important Warsat contract: `/api/warsat/plan` takes `protocolId`, `modelRef` or `modelPath`, resource fields, and returns a launch plan. I’m going to add a catalog layer that normalizes models.dev into that contract instead of inventing a second deployment path.
>
> models.dev’s public API is a provider/model catalog, not a deployment system. I’m wiring it as a read-only catalog source, then translating selected catalog entries into Warsat plans locally; no prompts, file contents, or user data are sent to models.dev.

Implemented the test-first production pass and committed it.

What changed:
- Added a safe backend model catalog layer in [backend/model_catalog.py](./backend/model_catalog.py).
- Added `/api/model-catalog` and `/api/model-catalog/refresh` in [backend/main.py](./backend/main.py).
- Wired the Models tab to show a catalog, filter by use/runtime/VRAM estimate, and send deployable models into Warsat planning.
- The catalog uses local curated Warsat-ready entries first, and can refresh public metadata from [models.dev](https://models.dev/api.json) / [models.dev repo](https://github.com/sst/models.dev).
- No user prompts, files, secrets, or local data are sent to models.dev.
- Added backend and UI tests for catalog-to-Warsat planning.

> i want to be able to find models from the warsat GUI without having to leave it, currently there are only 5 models available. I would also like to add small buttons for every little toggle so its obvious whats buttons and whats not, for instance, if clicking something is going to hide something it should be obvious it is going to do so. Also when i click new chat it should actually create a new chat instead of just bringing me back to the home page. I would also like to reduce the amount of null space between the folder titles and the use as workspace button on the workspaces tab so that we can expand the left panel on the workspaces tab to ensure proper centering and text formatting rrather than having issues with spilling over

I’m going to inspect the current implementation points first so the edits stay targeted.

> The current New Chat is only a frontend reset. I’m going to make it create a durable empty session in SQLite, then bind the composer to that session so the first message belongs to the new chat. That also fixes the sidebar behavior instead of masking it.
>
> For Workspaces I’m going to adjust the layout at the CSS level rather than moving components around: widen the approved-folder rail, make folder cards use a denser two-column action layout, and keep responsive breakpoints intact.
>
> I’m also going to expose the model catalog in Warsat directly. The Models tab can still be a broader registry/catalog page, but Warsat should be able to search, filter, refresh, and create the plan without a detour.

Implemented and committed the focused pass on `codex/gui-preview-hub`.

Changes made:
- Warsat now has an in-tab model finder with search, category filter, local catalog reload, and `Refresh models.dev`.
- Warsat catalog selections can create a launch plan directly from the Warsat tab.
- New Chat now creates a real durable backend session instead of just resetting the home view.
- First real message renames placeholder chats like `New chat`.
- Disclosure/toggle rows now show explicit `Show` / `Hide` pills.
- Workspace root cards are wider/denser with less dead space before the workspace action buttons.
- Added backend and Playwright coverage for the new session flow and Warsat model finder.

Validation:
- `npm.cmd run build` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui` passed:
  - 26 backend smoke tests passed.
  - live smoke passed.
  - 9 Playwright UI tests passed.
- RasputinTest is running and healthy at `http://127.0.0.1:8899`.

Commit:
- `4eb11d0 Improve Warsat model discovery and chat creation`

Note: the in-app browser bridge failed to initialize due a local sandbox startup issue, but the Docker health check and Playwright UI harness both verified the running app.

For the more stable curated branch, use [`main`](https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/tree/main).\n\n```\n\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n \u22b9 \u08ea \u02d6 \u0aee( \u02f6\u1d54 \u1d55 \u1d54\u02f6 )\u3063  Odysseus vers. 1.0\n\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n```\n\n![Odysseus](docs/odysseus.jpg)\n\nA self-hosted AI workspace -- meant to be the self-hosted version of the UI experience you get from ChatGPT and Claude. But with more jank and fun. Running on your own hardware, with your own data -- local-first, privacy-first, and no trojan.\n\n## Features\n  - **Chat** -- chat with any local model or API; adding them is super simple.<br>\u3000<sub>vLLM \u00b7 llama.cpp \u00b7 Ollama \u00b7 OpenRouter \u00b7 OpenAI \u00b7 GitHub Copilot</sub>\n  - **Agent** -- hand it tools and let it run the whole task itself.<br>\u3000<sub>built on [opencode](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode) \u00b7 MCP \u00b7 web \u00b7 files \u00b7 shell \u00b7 skills \u00b7 memory</sub>\n  - **Cookbook** -- Scans your hardware, recommends models, click to download and serve.. easy!<br>\u3000<sub>built on [llmfit](https://github.com/AlexsJones/llmfit) \u00b7 VRAM-aware \u00b7 GGUF / FP8 / AWQ \u00b7 fit scoring \u00b7 vLLM / llama.cpp serving</sub>\n  - **Deep Research** -- multi-step runs that gather, read, and synthesize sources into a nice visual report.<br>\u3000<sub>adapted from [Tongyi DeepResearch](https://github.com/Alibaba-NLP/DeepResearch)</sub>\n  - **Compare** -- a fun tool to compare models side by side. Test completely blind, no bias!<br>\u3000<sub>multi-model \u00b7 blind test \u00b7 synthesis</sub>\n  - **Documents** -- YOU write the text, AI is there to assist, not the opposite.<br>\u3000<sub>multi-tab editor \u00b7 markdown \u00b7 HTML \u00b7 CSV \u00b7 syntax highlighting \u00b7 AI edits \u00b7 suggestions</sub>\n  - **Memory / Skills** -- Persistent memory and skills, your agent evolves over time as it better understands you and your tasks!<br>\u3000<sub>ChromaDB \u00b7 fastembed (ONNX) \u00b7 vector + keyword retrieval \u00b7 import/export</sub>\n  - **Email** -- IMAP/SMTP inbox with AI triage built in: urgency reminders, auto-tag, auto-summary, auto-reply drafts, auto-spam.<br>\u3000<sub>IMAP \u00b7 SMTP \u00b7 per-account routing \u00b7 CalDAV-aware</sub>\n  - **Notes & Tasks** -- Quick notes with reminders, a todo list, and scheduled tasks the agent can act on.<br>\u3000<sub>note pings \u00b7 checklist \u00b7 cron-style tasks \u00b7 ntfy / browser / email channels</sub>\n  - **Calendar** -- Local-first calendar with CalDAV sync to Radicale / Nextcloud / Apple / Fastmail.<br>\u3000<sub>CalDAV pull \u00b7 .ics import/export \u00b7 per-calendar colors \u00b7 agent-aware</sub>\n  - **Works on mobile** -- looks and runs great on your phone, not just desktop.<br>\u3000<sub>responsive \u00b7 installable (PWA) \u00b7 touch gestures</sub>\n  - **Extras** -- more to explore, happy if you give it a go!<br>\u3000<sub>image editor \u00b7 theme editor \u00b7 file uploads (vision + PDF) \u00b7 web search \u00b7 presets \u00b7 sessions \u00b7 2FA</sub>\n\n## Demo\nA full, hover-to-play tour lives on the landing page (`docs/index.html`).\n\n<details>\n<summary>Screenshots / clips</summary>\n\n### Chat & Agents\n![Chat & Agents](docs/chat.gif)\n### Deep Research\n![Deep Research](docs/research.gif)\n### Compare\n![Compare](docs/compare.gif)\n### Documents\n![Documents](docs/document.gif)\n### Notes & Tasks\n![Notes & Tasks](docs/notes.gif)\n\n</details>\n\n## Quick Start\n\nDefaults work out of the box: clone, run, then configure models/search/email\ninside **Settings**. Only edit `.env` for deployment-level overrides like\n`APP_BIND`, `APP_PORT`, `AUTH_ENABLED`, `DATABASE_URL`, or a pre-seeded admin password.\n\nOn first setup, Odysseus creates an admin account (`admin` unless\n`ODYSSEUS_ADMIN_USER` is set) and prints a temporary password in the terminal.\nFor Docker installs, the same line is in `docker compose logs odysseus`.\nUse that for the first login, then change it in **Settings**.\n\nContributing? See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for setup, testing, and\npull request guidelines.\n\n### Docker (recommended)\n```bash\ngit clone https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus.git\ncd odysseus\ncp .env.example .env       # optional, but recommended for explicit defaults\ndocker compose up -d --build\n```\nTo include optional extras in the image (PDF viewer, Office extraction; includes AGPL PyMuPDF), build with `docker compose build --build-arg INSTALL_OPTIONAL=true` before `up`.\n\nOpen `http://localhost:7000` when the containers are healthy. Docker Compose\nbinds the web UI to `127.0.0.1` by default. If the port is taken, set\n`APP_PORT=7001` in `.env` and recreate the container. Set `APP_BIND=0.0.0.0`\nonly when you intentionally want LAN/reverse-proxy access.\n\n### Native Linux / macOS\n```bash\ngit clone https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus.git\ncd odysseus\npython3 -m venv venv\nsource venv/bin/activate\npip install -r requirements.txt\npython setup.py\npython -m uvicorn app:app --host 127.0.0.1 --port 7000\n```\nRequirements: Python 3.11+. Cookbook also needs `tmux` for background model\ndownloads and serves. The app itself is lightweight; local model serving is the\nheavy part and depends on the model, runtime, GPU, and VRAM, so small hosts can\nconnect to API or remote model servers instead. Use `--host 0.0.0.0` only when you intentionally want LAN/reverse-proxy access.\n\n### Apple Silicon\nDocker on macOS cannot use the Metal GPU. For GPU-accelerated Cookbook on an\nM-series Mac, run Odysseus natively:\n\n```bash\ngit clone https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus.git\ncd odysseus\n./start-macos.sh\n```\n\nIt launches at `http://127.0.0.1:7860`. To expose it to your phone over a trusted LAN/VPN such as Tailscale, bind all interfaces:\n\n```bash\nODYSSEUS_HOST=0.0.0.0 ./start-macos.sh\n# then open http://<tailscale-ip>:7860\n```\n\nThe script also reads `.env` at startup, so `APP_BIND=0.0.0.0` and `APP_PORT`\nset there are picked up automatically without a command-line override each run.\n\nKeep `AUTH_ENABLED=true` (the default) before binding outside loopback. Do not\nexpose this port directly to the public internet. To build a clickable app wrapper:\n\n```bash\n./build-macos-app.sh\n```\n\n<details>\n<summary>Cookbook, GPU, Ollama, and troubleshooting notes</summary>\n\n**Docker bundled services.** Compose starts Odysseus, ChromaDB, SearXNG, and\nntfy. Odysseus and the bundled service ports bind to `127.0.0.1` by default, so\nthey are reachable from the host but not exposed to your LAN/public internet\nunless you opt in.\n\n**Cookbook storage in Docker.** Downloads live in `./data/huggingface`\n(`~/.cache/huggingface` in the container). Cookbook-installed Python CLIs and\nserve engines live in `./data/local` (`~/.local` in the container), so they\nsurvive container recreation.\n\n**Remote servers.** In **Cookbook -> Settings -> Servers**, generate the\nOdysseus SSH key and add the public key to the remote server's\n`~/.ssh/authorized_keys`. From the host you can also run:\n\n```bash\nssh-copy-id -i data/ssh/id_ed25519.pub user@server\n```\n\n**Docker GPU overlays.** CPU-only users can skip this section. Cookbook can\nonly detect GPUs that Docker exposes to the container \u2014 if the host runtime or\ndevice passthrough is not configured, Cookbook sees the iGPU, another card, or\nCPU instead of your intended GPU.\n\nFor NVIDIA, `scripts/check-docker-gpu.sh` diagnoses GPU passthrough and can\noptionally install the host runtime or update `.env`.\n\n```bash\n# Read-only diagnostic (default \u2014 installs nothing, never edits .env):\nscripts/check-docker-gpu.sh\n\n# Print OS-specific install commands without running them:\nscripts/check-docker-gpu.sh --print-install-commands\n\n# Install NVIDIA Container Toolkit on Ubuntu/Debian (requires sudo):\nscripts/check-docker-gpu.sh --install-nvidia-toolkit\n\n# Write COMPOSE_FILE to .env (only when GPU passthrough is confirmed working):\nscripts/check-docker-gpu.sh --enable-nvidia-overlay\n\n# Full assisted setup \u2014 install toolkit, then enable overlay if passthrough works:\nscripts/check-docker-gpu.sh --install-nvidia-toolkit --enable-nvidia-overlay\n```\n\nSafety notes:\n- The app never installs host GPU runtime automatically.\n- The app never edits `.env` automatically.\n- `.env` is only modified when `--enable-nvidia-overlay` is explicitly passed,\n  and only after GPU passthrough succeeds. `--yes` skips prompts but does not\n  bypass the passthrough gate.\n- `.env.bak.*` backups created by `--enable-nvidia-overlay` are ignored by\n  Git and the Docker build context.\n\nTo enable manually without the script, add this to `.env`:\n\n```bash\nCOMPOSE_FILE=docker-compose.yml:docker/gpu.nvidia.yml\n```\n\n**AMD / ROCm.** AMD setup is read-only diagnostic plus manual `.env` edit. Run:\n\n```bash\nscripts/check-docker-amd-gpu.sh\n```\n\nThen add the reported values to `.env`, replacing `RENDER_GID` with your host's\nnumeric render group id:\n\n```bash\nCOMPOSE_FILE=docker-compose.yml:docker/gpu.amd.yml\nRENDER_GID=989\n```\n\nFor NVIDIA/AMD GPU support, also read the comments in the selected overlay file: docker/gpu.nvidia.yml or docker/gpu.amd.yml.\n\n**Stack-management UIs (Portainer, Coolify, Dockhand, etc.).** These tools\noften accept only a single Compose file and do not reliably honor `COMPOSE_FILE`\nor multiple `-f` overlays. CLI users should keep using the `COMPOSE_FILE`\noverlay workflow above. For stack UIs, point the stack at one of the standalone\nfiles instead, which bundle the base stack plus the GPU settings:\n\n- `docker-compose.gpu-nvidia.yml` \u2014 still requires the NVIDIA Container Toolkit\n  on the host.\n- `docker-compose.gpu-amd.yml` \u2014 still requires host ROCm/kfd/DRI setup, the\n  `video`/`render` group membership, and `RENDER_GID` when needed.\n\nThe base `docker-compose.yml` plus the `docker/gpu.*.yml` overlays remain the\nsource of truth; the standalone files mirror them for single-file deployments.\n\nVerify after enabling either overlay:\n\n```bash\ndocker compose exec odysseus nvidia-smi -L   # NVIDIA\ndocker compose exec odysseus sh -lc 'test -e /dev/kfd && test -d /dev/dri && ls -l /dev/kfd /dev/dri/renderD*'  # AMD\n```\n\n> **GPU passthrough \u2260 llama.cpp CUDA.** `nvidia-smi` passing inside the\n> container confirms Docker GPU access, but llama.cpp also needs `cudart` and\n> the CUDA Toolkit at runtime. If Cookbook logs show `Unable to find cudart\n> library`, `Could NOT find CUDAToolkit`, `CUDA Toolkit not found`, or\n> tensors/layers assigned to CPU, that is a Cookbook/llama.cpp build issue \u2014", "encoding": "utf-8", "sha": "c99e7031e36204a12ca6df5044a4c12f747fedd9", "display_url": "https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/blob/dev/README.md", "display_title": "README.md"}
> ````

Short answer: Rasputin is past “toy prototype,” but not close to a full Odysseus-class wrapper yet.

I’d call it:

- **45-55% of the application shell**: auth, Docker startup, UI tabs, workspace browsing, models, Warsat plans, tasks, settings, preferences.
- **25-35% of the agent/workflow depth**: we have Plan -> Execute -> Reflect, sessions, memory, traces, subagents, approvals, but it is still simple and not a mature tool-calling runtime.
- **15-25% of the “all bells and whistles” product**: Odysseus advertises chat, agents, MCP/files/shell/web/skills/memory, Cookbook model serving, deep research, compare mode, document editor, email, notes, calendar, mobile/PWA, file uploads, vision/PDF, and more. Rasputin only has early versions of several of those.

Where Rasputin is strongest right now:
- Safety-first local architecture.
- Dockerized localhost wrapper.
- Workspace approval model.
- React/FastAPI foundation.
- SQLite runtime state.
- Basic RAG/Graphify/memory.
- Warsat launch-plan concept.
- Model registry and catalog plumbing.
- Task history, traces, approvals, and UI testing harness.

Where Rasputin is still behind:
- Real MCP server/tool ecosystem.
- Real agent tool-calling loop with durable workers, retries, tool schemas, and tool result injection.
- Strong document editor.
- True vector RAG with embeddings, file ingestion, PDF/DOCX/XLSX parsing, citations, and reindexing.
- Hardware-aware model deployment: VRAM detection, fit scoring, downloads, progress, logs, health, rollback.
- Deep research workflow.
- Compare/blind model testing.
- File operations with safe preview/approval.
- Mobile/PWA polish.
- Production-level onboarding and setup.

Practical estimate:

- **Usable private testing build**: we are close, maybe **2-4 solid passes** away.
- **Serious local companion MVP**: probably **4-8 weeks** of focused work.
- **Comparable to Odysseus feature breadth**: realistically **3-6 months**, depending how polished each workflow needs to be.
- **Comparable quality and reliability**: longer, because agent tooling, model deployment, file editing, and RAG all need hardening.

The next highest-leverage work is not more UI polish. It is:

1. Real tool broker / MCP layer.
2. Warsat deployment lifecycle.
3. Proper RAG ingestion and file parsing.
4. Workspace file actions with approval preview.
5. Strong task/agent traces so you can trust what the AI actually did.

Rasputin has the skeleton. It does not yet have the muscle.

> whats the best way for us to pull features out of odysseus that we might not have yet and incorporate them into our product? i dont know if i want to spend the next 3-6 months prompting, but if we can generate a long plan to carry out multiple passes

<details><summary>2 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll answer this as an implementation strategy, not just a feature wishlist. I’m going to ground it in the Odysseus feature surface we already inspected, then map it into Rasputin-native passes so we avoid copying names/branding/code blindly.
>
> <details><summary>Searched web 1 time</summary>
>
> <details><summary>Searched web 1 time</summary>
>
> - Searched web for `https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/tree/dev`
>
> </details>

</details>

Best approach: do **feature harvesting**, not copy-paste cloning.

Use Odysseus as a reference product, then reimplement the useful mechanics in Rasputin’s architecture, naming, safety model, and UI language. Odysseus advertises major areas we do not fully have yet: agent tools/MCP, Cookbook model serving, deep research, compare mode, documents, memory/skills with vector retrieval, email, notes/tasks, calendar, mobile/PWA, file uploads, vision/PDF, web search, presets, and 2FA. Source: [Odysseus README](https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/tree/dev#features). Its repo also has top-level areas like `core`, `mcp_servers`, `integrations`, `services`, `routes`, and `docker`, which is a good sign that its features are separated by domain rather than dumped into one file.

**The Right Method**

1. **Inventory Odysseus feature areas**
   Build a matrix:
   - Feature
   - User workflow
   - Backend services needed
   - Frontend screens needed
   - Safety/approval requirements
   - Rasputin equivalent name
   - Must-have vs later
   - Whether we can reimplement cleanly or need attribution/licensing review

2. **Map every feature into Rasputin-native names**
   We should not use their product terms directly.
   - Cookbook -> **Warsat**
   - Agent -> **Operations Runtime** or **Warmind Runtime**
   - Deep Research -> **Recon**
   - Compare -> **Trials**
   - Documents -> **Archive Editor**
   - Memory/Skills -> **Warmind Memory / Protocols**
   - Notes/Tasks -> **Directives**
   - Calendar -> **Timeline**
   - MCP servers -> **Tool Relays** or just MCP internally

3. **Implement in vertical passes**
   Do not spend months prompting one tiny UI fix at a time. Each pass should produce a working end-to-end slice:
   - backend API
   - database schema
   - frontend view
   - tests
   - Docker validation
   - docs

4. **Keep Rasputin’s differentiator**
   Odysseus is broader and playful. Rasputin should become:
   - local-first
   - security-first
   - approval-first
   - Docker/model-runtime aware
   - workspace/file companion focused
   - less “AI does everything,” more “AI assists with real local workflows”

**Multi-Pass Plan**

**Pass 0: Feature Harvest Matrix**
Create `docs/FEATURE_HARVEST_MATRIX.md`.

Catalog:
- Odysseus feature
- Rasputin status
- implementation difficulty
- user value
- risk
- required backend modules
- required UI surfaces
- test requirements

Output: a ranked roadmap, not vibes.

**Pass 1: Agent Tool Runtime**
This is the biggest missing foundation.

Current Rasputin has [backend/agent.py](./backend/agent.py), but it is still a controlled Plan -> Execute -> Reflect loop. We need a real tool runtime.

Build:
- tool registry
- tool schemas
- tool permissions
- tool call queue
- tool result injection
- approval-gated risky tools
- durable tool traces
- per-task tool budget
- retry/failure handling
- “what did Rasputin actually do?” view

Tools first:
- workspace browse
- file read
- RAG search
- Graphify search
- memory search
- model health test

Risky tools later:
- file write
- move/rename
- shell
- Docker
- web search

**Pass 2: Real MCP Server Layer**
Right now Rasputin has [backend/mcp_layer.py](./backend/mcp_layer.py), but we need production MCP behavior.

Build:
- MCP server registry
- MCP tool discovery
- local-only tool transport
- per-server permissions
- enable/disable UI
- audit logs
- approval gates
- tool timeout controls
- sandboxed filesystem roots

Goal: models never get arbitrary system access. Rasputin brokers every tool call.

**Pass 3: Warsat as Superior Cookbook**
This is high value and distinct.

Current Warsat has planning and some model catalog plumbing. Next:
- hardware profile screen
- GPU/VRAM detection where possible
- model fit scoring
- model search from models.dev/Hugging Face/Ollama catalogs
- download queue
- deployment queue
- container logs
- start/stop/restart
- health check
- rollback/remove
- generated Compose preview
- approval before Docker execution

This should become one of Rasputin’s flagship features.

**Pass 4: RAG v2**
Current RAG is not enough for professional use.

Build:
- ChromaDB or LanceDB
- local embedding model role
- incremental indexing
- PDF parser
- DOCX parser
- XLSX/CSV parser
- code-aware chunking
- citations with file path + line/page
- index status per workspace
- clear/reindex controls
- “what context was retrieved?” panel

This unlocks real file intelligence.

**Pass 5: Archive Editor**
This is the Claude-style document/editor workflow.

Build:
- Markdown editor first
- side-by-side preview
- AI suggestion mode
- accept/reject changes
- export markdown
- later DOCX import/export
- later PDF parse + annotate
- citation insertion from RAG
- save to selected output folder

This directly supports your “any workflow” goal.

**Pass 6: Recon / Deep Research**
Only after MCP/web broker and RAG are solid.

Build:
- research task mode
- approved web-search broker
- source collection
- source summaries
- citation report
- local-only notes
- exportable markdown/PDF report
- clear privacy warning: queries may leave machine, local files do not

**Pass 7: Compare / Trials**
Useful for testing your local models.

Build:
- blind model comparison
- same prompt to multiple models
- hidden labels until vote
- latency/token stats
- scoring notes
- “use winner as default” action

This helps you tune model roles.

**Pass 8: Directives, Schedules, Notifications**
Rasputin already has schedules scaffolding. Make it useful.

Build:
- reminders
- recurring tasks
- pending approvals
- local notifications
- optional Telegram approval only
- task calendar view later

**Pass 9: Mobile/PWA + Onboarding**
Before public release.

Build:
- first-run wizard
- admin password reset flow polish
- model setup wizard
- workspace setup wizard
- installable PWA
- mobile side drawer
- offline-ready static UI
- setup validation checklist

**Recommended Order**

Do this sequence:

1. Feature harvest matrix.
2. Tool runtime.
3. MCP server layer.
4. Warsat deployment lifecycle.
5. RAG v2.
6. Archive editor.
7. Deep research.
8. Compare mode.
9. Schedules/notifications.
10. PWA/onboarding.

Do not start with email/calendar/image editor. Those are broad distractions until the local agent, tool, model, and file workflows are solid.

**How We Avoid 3-6 Months Of Random Prompting**

We should create a single execution document with epics, then work branch-by-branch:

- `codex/harvest-feature-matrix`
- `codex/tool-runtime-v1`
- `codex/mcp-relay-v1`
- `codex/warsat-runtime-v1`
- `codex/rag-v2`
- `codex/archive-editor-v1`

Each branch should have:
- implementation checklist
- acceptance tests
- UI smoke test
- Docker test
- no new feature unless previous pass is stable

Best next move: create the feature harvest matrix and convert it into a staged implementation backlog for Rasputin. That gives us one controlling document so future work is execution, not constant re-deciding.

> # Rasputin Feature Harvest Matrix And Staged Backlog Plan
>
> ## Summary
> Create a decision-grade product planning layer that compares Rasputin against Odysseus-style wrapper capabilities, identifies missing feature gaps, and converts those gaps into an ordered implementation backlog. This pass is documentation/planning only; it should not modify runtime behavior.

> ## Key Changes

>   - reference product/source
>   - reference feature behavior
>   - Rasputin-native name

>   - user value: `1-5`
>   - implementation effort: `S`, `M`, `L`, `XL`

>
> - Use Odysseus as the primary harvest source, but reframe features into Rasputin terminology:
>   - Cookbook -> Warsat

>   - Deep Research -> Recon

>   - Documents -> Archive Editor
>   - Notes/Tasks -> Directives

>
> - Convert the matrix into a staged backlog:
>   - **Stage 1: Foundation**: tool broker, MCP relay, durable tool calls, approval gates.
>   - **Stage 2: Warsat**: model search, hardware fit, downloads, deployment lifecycle, logs.
>   - **Stage 3: Knowledge**: RAG v2, document parsing, Graphify evidence, citations.
>   - **Stage 4: Workflows**: Archive Editor, Recon, Trials.
>   - **Stage 5: Companion Layer**: Directives, schedules, notifications, optional Telegram.
>   - **Stage 6: Release Polish**: onboarding, PWA/mobile, docs, threat model, setup tests.
>
> - Add backlog entries with implementation-ready metadata:
>   - branch name
>   - goal
>   - scope
>   - affected subsystems

>   - UI changes
>   - security requirements
>   - tests required

> ````text
> {"content": "# Odysseus\n\n> **Branch note:** `dev` is the default branch and contains the latest development changes, but it may be unstable. For the more stable curated branch, use [`main`](https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/tree/main).\n\n```\n\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n \u22b9 \u08ea \u02d6 \u0aee( \u02f6\u1d54 \u1d55 \u1d54\u02f6 )\u3063  Odysseus vers. 1.0\n\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n```\n\n![Odysseus](docs/odysseus.jpg)\n\nA self-hosted AI workspace -- meant to be the self-hosted version of the UI experience you get from ChatGPT and Claude. But with more jank and fun. Running on your own hardware, with your own data -- local-first, privacy-first, and no trojan.\n\n## Features\n  - **Chat** -- chat with any local model or API; adding them is super simple.<br>\u3000<sub>vLLM \u00b7 llama.cpp \u00b7 Ollama \u00b7 OpenRouter \u00b7 OpenAI \u00b7 GitHub Copilot</sub>\n  - **Agent** -- hand it tools and let it run the whole task itself.<br>\u3000<sub>built on [opencode](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode) \u00b7 MCP \u00b7 web \u00b7 files \u00b7 shell \u00b7 skills \u00b7 memory</sub>\n  - **Cookbook** -- Scans your hardware, recommends models, click to download and serve.. easy!<br>\u3000<sub>built on [llmfit](https://github.com/AlexsJones/llmfit) \u00b7 VRAM-aware \u00b7 GGUF / FP8 / AWQ \u00b7 fit scoring \u00b7 vLLM / llama.cpp serving</sub>\n  - **Deep Research** -- multi-step runs that gather, read, and synthesize sources into a nice visual report.<br>\u3000<sub>adapted from [Tongyi DeepResearch](https://github.com/Alibaba-NLP/DeepResearch)</sub>\n  - **Compare** -- a fun tool to compare models side by side. Test completely blind, no bias!<br>\u3000<sub>multi-model \u00b7 blind test \u00b7 synthesis</sub>\n  - **Documents** -- YOU write the text, AI is there to assist, not the opposite.<br>\u3000<sub>multi-tab editor \u00b7 markdown \u00b7 HTML \u00b7 CSV \u00b7 syntax highlighting \u00b7 AI edits \u00b7 suggestions</sub>\n  - **Memory / Skills** -- Persistent memory and skills, your agent evolves over time as it better understands you and your tasks!<br>\u3000<sub>ChromaDB \u00b7 fastembed (ONNX) \u00b7 vector + keyword retrieval \u00b7 import/export</sub>\n  - **Email** -- IMAP/SMTP inbox with AI triage built in: urgency reminders, auto-tag, auto-summary, auto-reply drafts, auto-spam.<br>\u3000<sub>IMAP \u00b7 SMTP \u00b7 per-account routing \u00b7 CalDAV-aware</sub>\n  - **Notes & Tasks** -- Quick notes with reminders, a todo list, and scheduled tasks the agent can act on.<br>\u3000<sub>note pings \u00b7 checklist \u00b7 cron-style tasks \u00b7 ntfy / browser / email channels</sub>\n  - **Calendar** -- Local-first calendar with CalDAV sync to Radicale / Nextcloud / Apple / Fastmail.<br>\u3000<sub>CalDAV pull \u00b7 .ics import/export \u00b7 per-calendar colors \u00b7 agent-aware</sub>\n  - **Works on mobile** -- looks and runs great on your phone, not just desktop.<br>\u3000<sub>responsive \u00b7 installable (PWA) \u00b7 touch gestures</sub>\n  - **Extras** -- more to explore, happy if you give it a go!<br>\u3000<sub>image editor \u00b7 theme editor \u00b7 file uploads (vision + PDF) \u00b7 web search \u00b7 presets \u00b7 sessions \u00b7 2FA</sub>\n\n## Demo\nA full, hover-to-play tour lives on the landing page (`docs/index.html`).\n\n<details>\n<summary>Screenshots / clips</summary>\n\n### Chat & Agents\n![Chat & Agents](docs/chat.gif)\n### Deep Research\n![Deep Research](docs/research.gif)\n### Compare\n![Compare](docs/compare.gif)\n### Documents\n![Documents](docs/document.gif)\n### Notes & Tasks\n![Notes & Tasks](docs/notes.gif)\n\n</details>\n\n## Quick Start\n\nDefaults work out of the box: clone, run, then configure models/search/email\ninside **Settings**. Only edit `.env` for deployment-level overrides like\n`APP_BIND`, `APP_PORT`, `AUTH_ENABLED`, `DATABASE_URL`, or a pre-seeded admin password.\n\nOn first setup, Odysseus creates an admin account (`admin` unless\n`ODYSSEUS_ADMIN_USER` is set) and prints a temporary password in the terminal.\nFor Docker installs, the same line is in `docker compose logs odysseus`.\nUse that for the first login, then change it in **Settings**.\n\nContributing? See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for setup, testing, and\npull request guidelines.\n\n### Docker (recommended)\n```bash\ngit clone https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus.git\ncd odysseus\ncp .env.example .env       # optional, but recommended for explicit defaults\ndocker compose up -d --build\n```\nTo include optional extras in the image (PDF viewer, Office extraction; includes AGPL PyMuPDF), build with `docker compose build --build-arg INSTALL_OPTIONAL=true` before `up`.\n\nOpen `http://localhost:7000` when the containers are healthy. Docker Compose\nbinds the web UI to `127.0.0.1` by default. If the port is taken, set\n`APP_PORT=7001` in `.env` and recreate the container. Set `APP_BIND=0.0.0.0`\nonly when you intentionally want LAN/reverse-proxy access.\n\n### Native Linux / macOS\n```bash\ngit clone https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus.git\ncd odysseus\npython3 -m venv venv\nsource venv/bin/activate\npip install -r requirements.txt\npython setup.py\npython -m uvicorn app:app --host 127.0.0.1 --port 7000\n```\nRequirements: Python 3.11+. Cookbook also needs `tmux` for background model\ndownloads and serves. The app itself is lightweight; local model serving is the\nheavy part and depends on the model, runtime, GPU, and VRAM, so small hosts can\nconnect to API or remote model servers instead. Use `--host 0.0.0.0` only when you intentionally want LAN/reverse-proxy access.\n\n### Apple Silicon\nDocker on macOS cannot use the Metal GPU. For GPU-accelerated Cookbook on an\nM-series Mac, run Odysseus natively:\n\n```bash\ngit clone https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus.git\ncd odysseus\n./start-macos.sh\n```\n\nIt launches at `http://127.0.0.1:7860`. To expose it to your phone over a trusted LAN/VPN such as Tailscale, bind all interfaces:\n\n```bash\nODYSSEUS_HOST=0.0.0.0 ./start-macos.sh\n# then open http://<tailscale-ip>:7860\n```\n\nThe script also reads `.env` at startup, so `APP_BIND=0.0.0.0` and `APP_PORT`\nset there are picked up automatically without a command-line override each run.\n\nKeep `AUTH_ENABLED=true` (the default) before binding outside loopback. Do not\nexpose this port directly to the public internet. To build a clickable app wrapper:\n\n```bash\n./build-macos-app.sh\n```\n\n<details>\n<summary>Cookbook, GPU, Ollama, and troubleshooting notes</summary>\n\n**Docker bundled services.** Compose starts Odysseus, ChromaDB, SearXNG, and\nntfy. Odysseus and the bundled service ports bind to `127.0.0.1` by default, so\nthey are reachable from the host but not exposed to your LAN/public internet\nunless you opt in.\n\n**Cookbook storage in Docker.** Downloads live in `./data/huggingface`\n(`~/.cache/huggingface` in the container). Cookbook-installed Python CLIs and\nserve engines live in `./data/local` (`~/.local` in the container), so they\nsurvive container recreation.\n\n**Remote servers.** In **Cookbook -> Settings -> Servers**, generate the\nOdysseus SSH key and add the public key to the remote server's\n`~/.ssh/authorized_keys`. From the host you can also run:\n\n```bash\nssh-copy-id -i data/ssh/id_ed25519.pub user@server\n```\n\n**Docker GPU overlays.** CPU-only users can skip this section. Cookbook can\nonly detect GPUs that Docker exposes to the container \u2014 if the host runtime or\ndevice passthrough is not configured, Cookbook sees the iGPU, another card, or\nCPU instead of your intended GPU.\n\nFor NVIDIA, `scripts/check-docker-gpu.sh` diagnoses GPU passthrough and can\noptionally install the host runtime or update `.env`.\n\n```bash\n# Read-only diagnostic (default \u2014 installs nothing, never edits .env):\nscripts/check-docker-gpu.sh\n\n# Print OS-specific install commands without running them:\nscripts/check-docker-gpu.sh --print-install-commands\n\n# Install NVIDIA Container Toolkit on Ubuntu/Debian (requires sudo):\nscripts/check-docker-gpu.sh --install-nvidia-toolkit\n\n# Write COMPOSE_FILE to .env (only when GPU passthrough is confirmed working):\nscripts/check-docker-gpu.sh --enable-nvidia-overlay\n\n# Full assisted setup \u2014 install toolkit, then enable overlay if passthrough works:\nscripts/check-docker-gpu.sh --install-nvidia-toolkit --enable-nvidia-overlay\n```\n\nSafety notes:\n- The app never installs host GPU runtime automatically.\n- The app never edits `.env` automatically.\n- `.env` is only modified when `--enable-nvidia-overlay` is explicitly passed,\n  and only after GPU passthrough succeeds. `--yes` skips prompts but does not\n  bypass the passthrough gate.\n- `.env.bak.*` backups created by `--enable-nvidia-overlay` are ignored by\n  Git and the Docker build context.\n\nTo enable manually without the script, add this to `.env`:\n\n```bash\nCOMPOSE_FILE=docker-compose.yml:docker/gpu.nvidia.yml\n```\n\n**AMD / ROCm.** AMD setup is read-only diagnostic plus manual `.env` edit. Run:\n", "encoding": "utf-8", "sha": "c99e7031e36204a12ca6df5044a4c12f747fedd9", "display_url": "https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/blob/dev/README.md", "display_title": "README.md"}
> ````

> ```text
> {"content": "# Roadmap / Help Wanted\n\nOdysseus is on a voyage, but not home yet. It works great for me (lol), but this ship is moving fast and feedback/help would be appreciated! (I don't know what I'm doing, help).\n\nIf you see weird CSS, strange layout behavior, or a suspiciously murky corner of\nthe codebase, you are probably right to stay away.\n\n## High Priority\n\n- SQUASH BUGS\n- Fresh install smoke tests on Linux, macOS, and Windows. Docker, native Python,\n  and WSL all need coverage.\n\n- Integration audit: do integrations even work? Confirm what works, what needs setup docs, and what should be removed or hidden. \n- Self-host troubleshooting cookbook. Document the weird 30-second fixes that otherwise become 30-minute searches: Dovecot cleartext auth for local stacks, ntfy Android Instant Delivery for non-ntfy.sh servers, clipboard limits on plain-HTTP Tailscale URLs, Radicale collection URLs, and similar traps.\n- Cookbook reliability on other computers. This is probably the area most likely to need work across different machines, GPUs, drivers, shells, and Python environments.\n- Cookbook SGLang support across platforms. Make sure SGLang setup/serve works\n  predictably on Linux, Windows/WSL, macOS where possible, Docker, and common\n  NVIDIA/AMD hardware paths.\n- Deep Research model presets by hardware. Recommend approved model/parameter\n  profiles for small, medium, and large local setups so people with different\n  hardware can use Deep Research without guessing. Surface this either in Deep\n  Research settings or as a Cookbook scan/dropdown suggestion.\n- Cookbook model scan/download ranking. Prioritize newer architectures and\n  better hardware-fit models instead of scoring everything almost the same.\n  Ranking should account for architecture age, quant format, VRAM/RAM fit,\n  backend support, vision/mmproj requirements, and likely serve reliability.\n- Cookbook error feedback and logging. Failed downloads, dependency installs,\n  preflights, and serve jobs should show the actual command/output/error in the\n  UI, with copyable logs and clear next steps instead of just \"crashed\".\n- Agent prompt/context bloat. Agent mode is too heavy for smaller local models:\n  tool schemas, skills, memory, documents, and instructions can eat the context\n  before the user request really starts. We need slimmer prompts, better tool\n  selection, smaller default tool sets, and clearer guidance for models with\n  4k/8k/16k context windows.\n- Skill/tool prompt-injection audit. User-editable skills, notes, documents,\n  fetched pages, and memories should be treated as untrusted data. Keep testing\n  whether models follow malicious instructions from those surfaces.\n- Better degraded-state reporting for ChromaDB, SearXNG, email, ntfy, and provider probes.\n- Email performance audit. Fetching, searching, opening, deleting, and sending\n  email can feel slow, especially over IMAP/SMTP providers with high latency.\n  Need someone who knows mail performance to profile the current flow, identify\n  whether the bottleneck is IMAP folder select/fetch, cache invalidation,\n  attachment/body loading, SMTP handshakes, or frontend refresh behavior, then\n  propose safer caching/prefetch/batching without breaking multi-account state.\n- Provider setup/probing audit for Anthropic, Gemini, Groq, xAI, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and DeepSeek.\n\n## Refactor Targets\n- CSS cleanup. `static/style.css` basically Calypso's island atm.\n- Tour core helper. The onboarding tours have too much copy-pasted scaffolding; promote a shared `tour-core.js` helper before adding more tours.\n- Modal/window positioning cleanup. Some window controls have improved, but the\n  underlying popup/dropdown/fixed-position behavior is still too fragile.\n- Mobile media override discoverability. A lot of \"CSS did not move\" bugs are mobile `@media` overrides of the same selector; comments or linting around desktop/mobile paired rules would help.\n- Dead code pass for old routes, stale feature flags, and unused UI states.\n\n## Frontend\n\n- Expand the Editor for quicker, more robust everyday use. Better file/document\n  handling, smoother window behavior, clearer save/export flows, stronger image\n  editing affordances, and fewer brittle edge cases.\n- Better AI integration for Notes and Todos. Notes should be easier for the\n  agent to read, update, summarize, and turn into actions. Todos should be\n  assignable to an agent from the UI, possibly through a button, task action,\n  or dedicated skill/tool flow.\n- Mobile gallery/editor polish. Easier to launch/download inpaint model or any missing pieces.\n- Accessibility pass: keyboard navigation, focus states, contrast, reduced motion.\n- Improve empty states and error messages on fresh installs.\n- Tighten first-run setup, hints, and tours so they do not repeat or fight each other.\n- Vendor CDN assets eventually for a more fully self-hosted/offline mode.\n\n## Backend\n\n- More tests around endpoint probing and provider setup.\n- Better task scheduler defaults and visibility.\n- Backup/restore guide and helper flow for `data/`.\n- Security hardening around admin-only tools and clear docs for their risk.\n\n## Not The Focus Right Now\n\nI prob shouldnt add more themes.", "encoding": "utf-8", "sha": "7c59c1f6a6f520308a5c9f7c57a118793c7a5369", "display_url": "https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/blob/dev/ROADMAP.md", "display_title": "ROADMAP.md"}
> ```

> Plan
>
> # Rasputin Next Pass: Tool Relay V1
>
> ## Summary
> Implement the first backlog branch, `codex/tool-relay-v1`. Rasputin already has a basic MCP layer, `TOOL_SPECS`, and persisted `tool_calls`; this pass formalizes that into a typed Tool Relay without granting new dangerous powers. The goal is visibility, schema, permission policy, and durable traces for tools Rasputin already uses.
>
> ## Key Changes
> - Add a first-class Tool Relay registry:
>   - tool id, display name, description, category, risk level, permission flag, enabled state, input schema, output summary policy, timeout, and approval behavior.
>   - include current tools: RAG search, Graphify search, workspace browse/list/tree, file preview/read, memory search, model health test.
>   - register risky tools as approval-required but keep write, move, shell, Docker, and web execution behavior unchanged.
>
> - Add backend API:
>   - `GET /api/tools` returns grouped tool definitions and current availability.
>   - Existing `/api/tasks/{taskId}` continues returning `toolCalls`.
>   - Tool calls keep using structured response and camelCase fields.
>
> - Refactor current MCP calls through the registry:
>   - existing `McpLayer.call_tool` checks registry metadata before execution.
>   - persist normalized tool call records with risk, status, redacted args, redacted result, approval id, timestamps, and task id.
>   - safe tools run only if their permission flag allows them.
>   - guarded/approval-required tools do not bypass existing approval logic.
>
> - Improve UI visibility:
>   - add a Tools section in Task Details instead of burying tools only under Approvals.
>   - show tool name, risk, status, permission, redacted args, redacted result summary, and related approval.
>   - add a Settings or Activity read-only Tool Relay panel showing available tools and disabled/blocked reasons.
>
> - Keep scope tight:
>   - no external MCP server registry yet.
>   - no new file mutation capability.
>   - no shell execution.
>   - no Docker execution changes.
>   - no direct model internet access.
>
> ## Test Plan
> - Backend:
>   - `GET /api/tools` returns expected safe, guarded, and approval-required tools.
>   - disabled permission blocks relevant tool execution cleanly.
>   - safe RAG/Graphify/workspace tools still work through the relay.
>   - tool calls persist redacted args/results.
>   - unknown tool returns structured error.
>   - approval-required tool cannot execute without existing approval path.
>
> - Frontend:
>   - Tool Relay panel renders grouped tools.
>   - Task Details shows tool calls in a dedicated section.
>   - Risk/status labels are readable and accessible.
>   - No raw file contents, secrets, prompts, or full private document text appear in tool summaries.
>
> - Harness:
>   - `git diff --check`
>   - `npm.cmd run build`
>   - `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui`
>   - Add/update smoke tests for `/api/tools` and task detail tool display.
>
> ## Assumptions
> - First commit before implementation should include the two docs from the last pass.
> - `tool_calls` table is reused; no new table is required unless implementation discovers a hard blocker.
> - Current `backend/mcp_layer.py` remains the execution bridge for v1.
> - Tool Relay V1 is primarily normalization, policy, and visibility, not expanded autonomy.
> - External MCP server registration belongs to the later `codex/mcp-relay-v1` branch.
</details>

> make the plan for the next features we will implement

<details><summary>1 previous message</summary>

Current branch is clean on `codex/tool-relay-v1`. Tool Relay V1 is committed, so the next practical wave should target features that make Rasputin testable as a real local AI workbench.

Based on [STAGED_IMPLEMENTATION_BACKLOG.md](./docs/STAGED_IMPLEMENTATION_BACKLOG.md), I recommend this order.

**Next Feature Wave**

1. **Warsat Deployment Lifecycle**
Branch: `codex/warsat-deploy-lifecycle`

Goal: make model deployment from Rasputin actually testable end to end.

Changes:
- Add lifecycle states: `planned`, `approvalPending`, `pulling`, `starting`, `probing`, `registered`, `failed`.
- Make deploy approval visible and usable in the Warsat UI.
- Show Docker pull/start/probe logs in one readable panel.
- Register a model only after health probe succeeds.
- Add retry, clear failed deployment, and “use this model” after success.
- Keep Docker execution approval-gated and localhost-only.

Definition of done:
- You can pick a model in Warsat, generate a launch plan, approve deployment, watch logs, see health status, and use the new model in chat.

2. **Context Governor V1**
Branch: `codex/context-governor-v1`

Goal: stop small local models from failing because Rasputin sends oversized internal context.

Changes:
- Calculate context budget from the selected model’s `contextWindow`.
- Use smaller prompt packs by mode: Chat, Code, Analyze, Write, Research, Organize.
- Limit tool schemas sent to the model based on mode.
- Compact long memory, RAG, Graphify, and tool traces before model calls.
- Show “what was included/omitted” in Task Details.

Definition of done:
- A small 1k, 4k, or 8k context model can answer basic chat without Rasputin causing context-length errors.

3. **RAG V2 / Document Intel Foundation**
Branch: `codex/rag-v2-document-intel`

Goal: make indexing folders and retrieving documents actually understandable and useful.

Changes:
- Replace/augment the current lightweight index with a real local vector-backed index.
- Keep index data under ignored local `data/`.
- Add incremental indexing by file modified time/content hash.
- Show indexed/not indexed state correctly in Workspaces.
- Add parser status for `.md`, `.txt`, `.py`, `.js`, `.json`, `.csv`, etc.
- Prepare PDF/DOCX/XLSX parsing as the next layer, not all at once.

Definition of done:
- You can index a mounted workspace, see where the index lives, search it, and get cited local results in Task Details.

4. **MCP Relay V1**
Branch: `codex/mcp-relay-v1`

Goal: let Rasputin register local MCP-style tool servers safely through the Tool Relay system.

Changes:
- Add local MCP server registry.
- Add enable/disable/discover actions.
- Normalize discovered tools into Tool Relay risk levels.
- Default discovered tools to disabled or guarded until classified.
- No direct model access to MCP transports.

Definition of done:
- Rasputin can discover local tool servers, show available tools, and keep all execution behind policy, audit, and approvals.

**Recommended Immediate Next Pass**

Start with `codex/warsat-deploy-lifecycle`.

Reason: Tool Relay just gave us the safety/trace foundation. Warsat is the most visible product differentiator and the feature you most need to test next: pulling/running a model from the UI and then using it in Rasputin. After that, Context Governor fixes model reliability, then RAG V2 makes workspace/file intelligence real.

Implemented and committed the Warsat deployment lifecycle pass on `codex/warsat-deploy-lifecycle`.

What changed:
- Warsat plans now track lifecycle phases: planned, approval, pull, start, probe health, register.
- Deploy only registers a model after the container health probe succeeds.
- Failed probes return clean failure details instead of pretending deployment succeeded.
- Warsat UI now shows lifecycle state, failed phase, health result, and deployment logs.

>
> # Rasputin Next Pass: Context Governor V1
>
> ## Completed Checklist
>
> From the staged backlog, these items are now checked off:
>
> - [x] `docs/FEATURE_HARVEST_MATRIX.md`
> - [x] `docs/STAGED_IMPLEMENTATION_BACKLOG.md`
> - [x] `codex/tool-relay-v1`
>   - Tool registry exists.
>   - `GET /api/tools` exists.
>   - Existing safe tools route through Tool Relay.
>   - Tool calls persist with redacted args/results.
>   - Task details and Activity expose Tool Relay visibility.
> - [x] `codex/warsat-deploy-lifecycle`
>   - Warsat plans include lifecycle phases.
>   - Deployment requires approval.
>   - Docker execution stays approval-gated.
>   - Health probe runs before registry save.
>   - Failed health probe does not register the model.
>   - Warsat UI shows approval, deploy, probe, register, logs, and failure state.
>
> Still open from the immediate backlog:
>
> - [ ] `codex/mcp-relay-v1`
> - [ ] `codex/context-governor-v1`
> - [ ] `codex/warsat-hardware-probe`
> - [ ] `codex/warsat-fit-scoring`
> - [ ] `codex/rag-v2-document-intel`
>
> ## Summary
>
> Next pass should be `codex/context-governor-v1`.
>
> Reason: Rasputin already hit a real vLLM context-length failure when a simple “hello” prompt produced too much agent context. RAG V2 and MCP expansion will increase context pressure, so the next safest pass is to make prompt assembly budget-aware before adding more retrieval/tool capability.
>
> ## Key Changes
>
> - Add a backend Context Governor used by agent phases before every model call.
> - Calculate a context budget from the selected model’s `context_window` and `max_tokens`.
> - Build prompt sections as separate budgeted blocks:
>   - current user message
>   - recent conversation
>   - memory recall
>   - RAG sources
>   - Graphify evidence
>   - workspace tree
>   - file snippets
>   - rules/system guidance
> - Apply deterministic trimming with priority:
>   - always keep current user message and safety rules
>   - keep recent conversation before old conversation
>   - keep cited local evidence before broad workspace listings
>   - trim workspace tree before file snippets
>   - trim snippets before dropping them
> - Add trace metadata to each task:
>   - selected model key
>   - context window
>   - max output tokens
>   - estimated input tokens
>   - included sections
>   - trimmed sections
>   - omitted sections
> - Update task details UI to show a compact “Context Budget” panel under “What Rasputin Saw.”
> - Keep existing model registry shape, but normalize bad values:
>   - missing context window defaults to `4096`
>   - values below `1024` are treated as unsafe and clamped to `1024`
>   - `max_tokens` must never become `0`
> - Keep RAG, Graphify, workspace browsing, and Tool Relay behavior unchanged except for how their results are packed into prompts.
>
> ## Public Interfaces
>
> - No new public endpoint is required.
> - Extend existing task detail/trace output with context governor events:
>   - `kind: "context_budget"`
>   - `modelKey`
>   - `contextWindow`
>   - `maxTokens`
>   - `estimatedInputTokens`
>   - `sections`
>   - `trimmed`
>   - `omitted`
> - Preserve existing `/api/tasks`, `/api/tasks/{taskId}`, `/api/tools`, `/api/rag/*`, and `/api/graph/*` behavior.
>
> ## Test Plan
>
> - Backend tests:
>   - small `1024` context model can answer a simple chat without exceeding budget.
>   - `max_tokens` never serializes as `0`.
>   - long workspace tree is trimmed before file snippets.
>   - current user message is never omitted.
>   - task trace records context budget metadata.
>   - existing Tool Relay and Warsat tests still pass.
> - UI tests:
>   - task detail drawer shows Context Budget.
>   - trimmed/omitted context is readable without exposing raw secrets.
>   - simple chat with `main-vllm` no longer fails from Rasputin-generated prompt bloat.
> - Validation commands:
>   - `npm.cmd run build`
>   - `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui`
>   - `git diff --check`
>
> ## Assumptions
>
> - This pass does not implement RAG V2, MCP server registration, or new document parsing.
> - Token estimation will use a deterministic local approximation instead of adding a tokenizer dependency.
> - Context Governor is backend-first; UI only exposes trace visibility.
> - The next branch after this should be either `codex/rag-v2-document-intel` or `codex/warsat-hardware-probe`, depending on whether the priority is knowledge quality or model deployment readiness.
</details>

Current RAG is still a JSON chunk index, and the agent builds large single prompts from memory, RAG, graph, workspace tree, snippets, and prior messages. That makes the next pass clearer: fix context governance before heavier RAG V2, otherwise better retrieval will make prompt pressure worse.

**Next Pass Plan: Warsat Hardware Probe V1**

Recommended branch: `codex/warsat-hardware-probe`

**Summary**

The next pass should make Warsat tell you whether your machine is actually ready to deploy models before you try to launch one. This unlocks better model recommendations, VRAM-aware fit scoring, and fewer “why is Docker stuck?” failures.

**Key Changes**

- Add a read-only backend hardware probe:
  - Docker CLI available
  - Docker daemon reachable
  - wrapper running inside Docker or native mode
  - Docker socket/control available or blocked
  - GPU visible to Docker, if possible
  - NVIDIA runtime/CUDA visibility, if available
  - model mount path visibility
  - current Warsat-managed containers
  - blocked reasons and manual next steps

- Add API:
  - `GET /api/warsat/hardware`
  - Response includes `ok`, `checks`, `warnings`, `blockedReasons`, `recommendations`, and `detectedHardware`.

- Add Warsat UI panel:
  - “System Readiness”
  - Clear pass/warn/block states
  - Copyable manual fix guidance
  - No automatic driver installs, Docker config edits, or host mutations
  - Links readiness directly to deployment plan requirements

- Prepare for the following pass:
  - Store enough safe probe metadata for `codex/warsat-fit-scoring`.
  - Do not implement fit scoring yet.

**Safety Rules**

- Read-only checks only.
- No Docker socket mount changes.
- No driver installs.
- No `.env` edits.
- No host filesystem mutation.
- Redact full host paths where possible; show mounted Rasputin-visible paths only.

**Tests**

- Backend:
  - Docker missing returns clean degraded status.
  - Docker daemon unavailable returns blocked status.
  - No GPU returns warning, not failure.
  - Docker control disabled is reported but does not fail the whole probe.
  - Probe response uses camelCase.
- UI:
  - Warsat readiness panel renders.
  - Blocked/warning/pass states are readable.
  - Deployment controls explain missing requirement when blocked.
- Validation:
  - `npm.cmd run build`
  - `scripts/test.ps1 -Ui`
  - `git diff --check`

**Definition Of Done**

You can open Warsat and know, before deploying, whether Rasputin can see Docker, whether Docker control is enabled, whether model folders are mounted, and whether GPU acceleration is visible enough to attempt a local model launch.

> compare this to something like odysseus, how far off are we

<details><summary>2 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll compare against Odysseus at the behavior level, not branding. I’m going to verify the current Odysseus README/roadmap and the Rasputin backlog/status so the gap estimate is grounded.
>
> <details><summary>Searched web 2 times</summary>
>
> <details><summary>Searched web 2 times</summary>
>
> - Searched web for `https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/tree/dev`
> - Searched web for `https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/blob/dev/ROADMAP.md`
>
> </details>
>
> </details>
</details>

Compared to Odysseus, Rasputin is still early, but no longer just a prototype.

Odysseus advertises a broad self-hosted workspace: chat, agent tools, Cookbook model deployment, deep research, compare, documents, memory/skills, email, notes/tasks, calendar, mobile/PWA, file uploads, web search, presets, sessions, and 2FA. It also has Docker/native setup paths and bundled services like ChromaDB, SearXNG, and ntfy. Sources: [Odysseus README](https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/blob/dev/README.md), [repo tree](https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus/tree/dev).

**Practical Gap Estimate**

Rasputin is about **35-40% of the way to an Odysseus-style local AI workspace** if we measure architecture and core flows.

It is more like **20-25% of the way to matching all advertised Odysseus features** because Odysseus covers many extra product areas: documents, deep research, compare, email, calendar, mobile/PWA, uploads, and broader integrations.

**Where Rasputin Is Stronger Or Promising**

- Safety model: Rasputin’s local-first, approval-gated design is stricter and more deliberate.
- Tool visibility: Tool Relay V1 gives us a clean base for auditable tool calls.
- Warsat direction: Rasputin now has launch plans, approval-gated deploys, lifecycle status, health probe gating, and hardware readiness checks.
- Context control: Context Governor V1 directly addresses the small-model prompt bloat problem Odysseus lists as a high-priority issue in its roadmap.
- Product identity: Rasputin is developing its own “safe local operator” lane instead of cloning Odysseus.

**Where Rasputin Is Behind**

- Agent/MCP: We have Tool Relay scaffolding, but not a full MCP server registry/discovery/execution system.
- Warsat vs Cookbook: We have the skeleton and safety path, but not true fit scoring, download management, SGLang/TGI breadth, dependency install logs, or robust cross-machine model deployment.
- RAG/Memory: Rasputin still needs proper vector retrieval, document parsing, citations, memory review polish, and import/export.
- Documents: Odysseus advertises a real document/editor workflow. Rasputin does not have Archive Editor yet.
- Research: Rasputin does not yet have approval-gated web research with citations and reports.
- Compare: Rasputin does not yet have blind multi-model comparison.
- Integrations: Email, calendar, notifications, mobile/PWA, uploads, and 2FA are not comparable yet.
- Release maturity: Odysseus has more packaging/setup surface. Rasputin has tests and Docker, but needs onboarding, docs, setup wizard, threat model polish, and cross-machine validation.

One important nuance: Odysseus itself is not presented as perfectly finished. Its roadmap calls out fresh install testing, integration audits, Cookbook reliability, Cookbook ranking/logging, and agent context bloat as high-priority work. So the target is not “catch a finished enterprise app”; it is “catch a much broader, more mature experimental wrapper.”

**Most Direct Path To Closing The Gap**

1. `mcp-relay-v1`: real local MCP server registry and tool discovery.
2. `warsat-fit-scoring`: hardware-aware model ranking.
3. `rag-v2-document-intel`: vector index, citations, PDF/DOCX/XLSX parsing.
4. `archive-editor-v1`: markdown/document editor with AI suggestions and approval-gated writes.
5. `recon-research-v1`: brokered web research with redacted approval gates.
6. `trials-model-compare`: blind model comparison and role preference saving.

If we complete those six passes, Rasputin moves from **early workbench** to **credible Odysseus-class competitor**, probably around **65-75% comparable** on the core AI-wrapper feature set while still being more safety-focused.

> build 5 individual plans to carry out the goals of those passes, then carry out each pass based off the plan you generate

> MCP tool call
